what_manipulated: juxtaposition vs. separate evaluation of two comparable options differing in quantity/number of components
what_measured: preference rating or perceived favorability of the option
experiment: Units evaluated two options that differed in quantity (e.g., a gift of lesser versus greater monetary value; an ice cream serving of 7 oz vs. 8 oz; a dinnerware set with 24 vs. 31 intact pieces) either in isolation (separate evaluation) or side-by-side (juxtaposed). Each unit provided preference or favorability judgments. The primary comparison was the reversal of preference depending on evaluation context (separate vs. juxtaposed).

what_manipulated: time pressure (present vs. absent) and information designed to shift overall affective evaluation of a hazard
what_measured: perceived risk and perceived benefit of a hazard
experiment: In Study 1, units made risk and benefit judgments about hazards under normal and time-pressure conditions. Time pressure was manipulated to limit analytic thought and enhance reliance on affect. In Study 2, units received information designed to alter overall affective evaluation (favorability) of a hazard, then provided risk and benefit judgments. The inverse relationship between risk and benefit judgments was compared across conditions, with the hypothesis that affect mediates the relationship.

what_manipulated: discussion with a co-witness (discussing memory for critical details vs. no discussion)
what_measured: accuracy of memory report for discussed versus non-discussed critical details at a final test
experiment: Units viewed an event, then participated in a discussion with a partner about selected critical details (or did not discuss). The partner provided either correct or incorrect information. At a final test, units reported what they remembered for both discussed and non-discussed critical details. Memory accuracy was compared between the discussed and non-discussed conditions, with signal analysis applied to assess the mechanisms of memory conformity.

what_manipulated: believer status (paranormal/religious believer vs. skeptic/non-believer, a measured individual difference)
what_measured: detection accuracy of face-like features in images and ratings of face-likeness and emotionality
experiment: Units viewed pictures of scenery and landscapes with and without face-like areas. In a detection task, they identified face-like regions. In a rating task, they evaluated the face-likeness and emotionality of identified areas. Detection sensitivity and response criteria were analyzed via signal detection theory. Between-group comparisons were made between believer and skeptic groups.

what_manipulated: singularity of victim (single vs. group) and presence of individually identifying information (present vs. absent)
what_measured: willingness to help (contribution amount) and reported distress
experiment: Units made decisions about donating to help a victim (or group) presented either with or without identifying information (e.g., photograph). The singularity of the victim and availability of identification were manipulated in a between-subjects design. Contribution amounts and self-reported distress were compared across conditions.

what_manipulated: presence and value of incidentally available numerical anchors in the environment during estimation (e.g., jersey number, product model number, restaurant name digits)
what_measured: numerical estimates of a quantity or value
experiment: Across three studies, units made numerical estimates (of athletic performance, market proportion, or restaurant spending) in contexts where irrelevant numerical values were incidentally present. Estimates were compared between conditions with different anchor values. Moderating factors such as domain expertise and recall of the anchor were examined.

what_manipulated: cultural context (United States vs. Hong Kong) and cardinal direction (north vs. south, presented in fictional city scenarios and preference questions)
what_measured: inferred socioeconomic status location and personal living preferences for cardinal directions
experiment: Units in two cultural contexts were presented with scenarios about where people of high versus low socioeconomic status lived in a fictional city, or were asked where they themselves would prefer to live. Cardinal direction (north vs. south) was varied. Ratings or preferences for cardinal location were compared across cultural groups, testing whether the metaphoric association between cardinal direction and valence differed by culture.

what_manipulated: ownership status of a letter stimulus (own-name letter vs. non-own-name letter, a within-subjects comparison) across 12 European languages
what_measured: preference ranking of letters; proportion of own-name letters ranked among the six most preferred letters
experiment: Units ranked visual letter stimuli from most to least preferred. The primary comparison was between letters belonging to the unit's own name (initial and/or non-initial) versus identical letters not belonging to the name. The percentage of own-name letters ranked among the six most preferred was calculated. Analysis was conducted separately for each of 12 languages to test the cross-linguistic generality of the effect.

what_manipulated: dual social identities of a target (shared vs. non-shared with the observer's ingroup) and performance outcome (success vs. failure) in a competitive context
what_measured: perceived nationality and perceived similarity of the target
experiment: In an archival study, media identification of an athlete's nationality was coded following a success versus failure event. In an experimental study, units evaluated a fictional athlete whose dual nationalities were manipulated to be either shared or non-shared with the observer's ingroup, and whose performance (success vs. failure) was also manipulated. Nationality judgments and similarity ratings were analyzed as a function of these conditions.

what_manipulated: type of reinforcement exposure training during a pre-training phase (delay-exposure, immediacy-exposure, or no-training control) for animal subjects
what_measured: choice patterns reflecting impulsivity (preference for smaller, sooner versus larger, later rewards) and the degree of delay discounting
experiment: Animal units underwent differential pre-training in which they received either extended training with delayed reinforcers, extended training with immediate reinforcers, or no training. Subsequently, choice tests measured preference between smaller, sooner and larger, later rewards. Between-group comparisons of impulsive choice rates and within-subject changes from pre- to post-training were analyzed.

what_manipulated: stimulus category (five categories identified as high preference vs. low preference for each unit) presented during a response force measurement task
what_measured: response force exerted by units to manipulate visual clarity of each stimulus category
experiment: Units first completed a verbal preference assessment for five visual stimulus categories. Subsequently, each category was presented individually in randomized order, and units used a force dynamometer to modulate visual clarity. Mean response force was measured for each stimulus category. The correlation between verbal preference rankings and actual response force for each category was examined.

what_manipulated: no explicit manipulation; this is instrument development and validation across three studies
what_measured: workplace intergenerational climate assessed via the WICS scale (five subscales: Intergenerational Contact, Retention, Positive Affect, Inclusiveness, Lack of Stereotypes)
experiment: In Study 1, an 18-item scale was developed via exploratory work. In Study 2, the instrument was refined and relationships with related constructs were tested via structural equation modeling. In Study 3, criterion, discriminant, and external validity were assessed in a diverse occupational sample. Subscale scores were correlated with workplace mentoring, attitudes about older workers, and job satisfaction.

what_manipulated: expectations about service quality (low vs. high) and actual service performance (low vs. high) delivered via written statements and photographs in an online scenario
what_measured: satisfaction judgment of the public service
experiment: Units were randomly assigned to receive either low- or high-expectation statements and to view either low- or high-performance street cleanliness photographs. Satisfaction with the service was rated. A 2x2 between-subjects design tested the expectancy-disconfirmation theory prediction that satisfaction reflects the comparison between expectations and perceived performance, with moderation by age and political ideology.

what_manipulated: no manipulation; personality traits (five-factor model) are measured predictor variables assessed at Time 1; body weight measures are outcomes tracked over ~4 years
what_measured: three body weight measures (body mass index, waist circumference, waist-to-hip ratio) at Time 1 and Time 2; changes in these measures across time
experiment: Units completed personality assessment (Mini-Markers) and body weight measurements at baseline. Body weight was reassessed approximately 4 years later in a follow-up. Cross-sectional associations between personality traits and body weight measures were tested via regression, controlling for age, gender, education, and chronic disease. Longitudinal associations between baseline personality and change in body weight over time were also examined, with moderation by gender.

what_manipulated: no manipulation; narcissism expression (measured via admiration and rivalry dimensions) is classified into categorical subgroups via latent class analysis
what_measured: latent class membership (subgroup assignment); associations of subgroup membership with personality traits (Machiavellianism, impulsivity) and adjustment indicators (self-esteem, empathy)
experiment: Units across multiple samples (German and US) completed self-report measures of narcissism (admiration and rivalry) and additional personality and adjustment measures. Latent class analysis identified distinct subgroups of narcissists based on their expression of agentic and antagonistic pathways. The four-class solution was evaluated across samples for consistency. Subgroups were then characterized by their associations with other personality traits and adjustment outcomes.

what_manipulated: emotional valence of music (happy, angry, or control) during encoding and alexithymia level (measured individual difference: high vs. low)
what_measured: recognition accuracy for emotion-related words (joy and anger words) in a later memory test
experiment: Units listened to emotionally valenced musical priming (congruent, incongruent, or neutral relative to subsequent words) during the encoding of emotion-related words. Alexithymia was measured as an individual difference. A recognition memory test was later administered. Recognition rates were compared across combinations of music type and alexithymia level to test moderation and congruency effects.

what_manipulated: no explicit manipulation; optimism (measured baseline predictor) and perceived social support assessed from both recipients' and providers' perspective
what_measured: perceived stress at 3-month follow-up; the mediation pathway through social support
experiment: In a dyadic longitudinal design, couples (units and their partners) completed baseline assessments of optimism and social support (measured from both the recipient's and provider's perspective). Perceived stress was measured at 3-month follow-up. Actor-Partner Interdependence Models tested whether the prospective relationship between baseline optimism and follow-up stress was mediated by perceived social support, comparing mediation via recipient-perceived versus provider-perceived support.

what_manipulated: firm's rating of informal competition as a threat (measured predictor); moderation examined via competitive context (level of formal competition) and institutional context (regulatory environment, availability of corruption as alternative)
what_measured: new product development (NPD) activities undertaken by formal firms (binary or frequency outcome)
experiment: Firms across Eastern Europe and Central Asia reported the degree of competition from informal (unregistered) firms and their engagement in NPD activities. Institutional and competitive context variables (formal competition, corruption options, regulatory optimism) were also measured. The direct effect of informal competition on NPD was tested via logistic or count regression, with interaction terms examining moderation by context factors.

what_manipulated: framing of the allocation task (giving frame: allocate a pool to the other unit vs. taking frame: take from a pool) and gender (male vs. female dictators)
what_measured: amount allocated in the dictator game
experiment: Units participated in a dictator game with fixed payoff possibilities. The task was framed either as giving or taking. Dictators were balanced across male and female. Allocations were compared across frame and gender, with analysis of gender-by-frame interactions.

what_manipulated: no manipulation; observational study of small-group cooperation in a two-level commons dilemma involving both investment decisions and extraction decisions; communication is observed or manipulated as a treatment in some conditions
what_measured: investment in provision of infrastructure and extraction from the common-pool resource; aggregate cooperation and coordination
experiment: Units in small groups made sequential decisions about infrastructure investment and resource extraction in an abstract commons dilemma modeled on irrigation systems. The impact of inherent asymmetry in the dilemma structure and the effect of communication on group-level coordination and cooperation were examined. Analysis focused on provision and extraction patterns and their relationship to asymmetry and communication.

what_manipulated: effort-based payoff difference between decision-maker and recipient, in a distribution scenario
what_measured: choice of justice principle (egalitarian, accountability, libertarian) to justify distribution
experiment: Units play a game where they exert effort that is differentially rewarded and determines surplus size. Units then make distribution choices over the surplus while eliciting preference for three justice principles. The key comparison is whether the justice principle chosen matches financial self-interest when the unit is paid more versus less than the recipient. Analysis examines correlation between payment status and principle selection.

what_manipulated: theoretical definition of weakness of will (akrasia versus ordinary notion)
what_measured: lay judgments of whether scenarios exemplify weakness of will
experiment: Units evaluate scenarios and judge whether described behaviors constitute weakness of will. Multiple experimental conditions present situations with varying motivational structures. Judgments are analyzed to identify which factors influence classification. Responses are examined to test whether lay concept matches philosophical akrasia definition or follows different dimensions.

what_manipulated: frequency characteristic of word immediately before target word (high versus low frequency)
what_measured: preview benefit (accuracy/speed gain for pre-viewed target word in reading)
experiment: Units read Chinese text while eye movements are tracked. Target word visibility is manipulated using a gaze-contingent display that pauses preview when eyes approach the target. Two conditions vary the frequency of the immediately preceding word. Eye movement measurements (gaze duration, fixation patterns) are compared between preview and no-preview conditions to assess benefit magnitude.

what_manipulated: HIV prevalence in geographic regions (naturally occurring variation)
what_measured: non-AIDS health services usage (antenatal care, birth deliveries, child immunization)
experiment: Multiple waves of demographic surveys across 14 countries spanning 1988-2005 are analyzed. Regions are stratified by observed HIV prevalence from most recent survey. Health service indicators are compared across region-wave combinations. Analysis examines temporal trends and whether divergence in service use coincides with HIV diffusion patterns. Semiparametric dating techniques identify inflection points.

what_manipulated: partnership formation and dissolution (transition between partnership states), with comparison of opposite-sex versus same-sex partnerships
what_measured: subjective well-being rating
experiment: Panel data tracks units over time across multiple partnership transitions (staying unpartnered, cohabiting, married). Within-unit changes in self-reported well-being are compared between transition types and partnership types. Analysis estimates causal effects by comparing well-being before and after partnership changes. Subgroup analyses stratify by cohort, gender, and sexual orientation.

what_manipulated: action type (omission versus commission) while holding intent, motive, and outcome constant; consequences (beneficial versus harmful)
what_measured: moral judgment (immorality rating) and decision evaluation (goodness rating)
experiment: Units read paired scenarios describing identical outcomes with identical intentions but one framed as omission and one as commission. Units rate either the actor's morality or option quality. Ratings are compared between action types. Additional experiments manipulate factors ordinarily distinguishing omissions and commissions (physical movement, salient alternative causes, counterfactual presence). Ratings of physical movement and causal reasoning are analyzed as potential mediators.

what_manipulated: number of product units displayed on packaging (high count versus low count)
what_measured: perceived product quantity and actual consumption volume
experiment: Three separate studies present units with snack packages varying in unit count displayed. In Study 1-2, units estimate package quantity; consumption is measured. Study 3 varies visual processing depth. For each unit, estimated quantity and actual consumption are recorded. Analysis tests whether high unit counts anchor estimates upward and whether consumption follows estimated rather than actual quantity. Moderation by visual processing is examined.

what_manipulated: reading intervention type (adaptive computer-delivered component versus non-adaptive; both versus control) among at-risk units
what_measured: reading fluency and spelling accuracy
experiment: Schools are randomly assigned to two intervention conditions or control. First-grade units are screened; at-risk units (n=140) in intervention schools receive comprehensive teacher-led instruction with or without adaptive computer learning. At-risk and control units receive standard instruction. Reading and spelling are measured post-intervention. Between-group differences are tested. Within-intervention comparison assesses whether adaptivity enhances gains.

what_manipulated: pairing of auditory tones (CS+ paired with participant's own name, CS- paired with different name, standard unpaired tone) in a conditioning paradigm
what_measured: brain event-related potential (P3 amplitude and timing) and subjective recognition of name pairings
experiment: Four experiments present units with three tone types in an oddball paradigm. Tones are paired with names (own versus different) with varying pairing frequency and timing. Brain potentials are recorded via EEG throughout. Analysis compares P3 response magnitude and latency across tone types and experiments. Experiment 2 uses masked presentation to test non-conscious effects. Recognition accuracy is probed post-task.

what_manipulated: information modality available for performance judgment (silent video only versus audio only versus both audio and video)
what_measured: accuracy of group-performance ranking (correctly identifying competition winners or orchestra rankings)
experiment: Six studies present units (experts and novices) with brief recordings of ensemble performances in different modalities. Units rank or select the top-performing group. Accuracy is measured against ground-truth rankings from actual competitions. Across studies, videos are either silent or accompanied by audio. Analysis compares accuracy rates across modality conditions. Visual cue importance is inferred from accuracy patterns.

what_manipulated: perceived government impartiality toward ethnic groups (naturally occurring variation across countries)
what_measured: sustained economic growth (GDP per capita growth >= 2% for 5+ consecutive years)
experiment: Survey data from 20 sub-Saharan African countries (Afrobarometer) spanning late 1990s onward are merged with growth data. Units (country-regions) are classified by growth status (sustained versus non-sustained). Perceived government impartiality from survey responses is assessed. Association between impartiality perception and growth emergence is tested controlling for policy content and other confounders. Analysis examines whether perception predicts growth independent of actual policy.

what_manipulated: manufacturing strategy adoption model (rigid-flexibility model versus trade-off versus cumulative approaches)
what_measured: manufacturing performance and responsiveness to market requirements
experiment: Cross-sectional database of approximately 800 manufacturing plants across five European countries is analyzed. Plant-level characteristics (process simplicity, procedural discipline, flexibility measures) are measured. Performance outcomes (responsiveness, efficiency) are assessed. Plants are classified by strategy adoption pattern. Performance differences across strategy types are examined. Country-level variation in adoption is documented.

what_manipulated: repeated interaction with stimulus (repeated checking versus repeated use versus control); stimulus type (experimental stove/light versus control light/stove)
what_measured: memory accuracy, confidence, vividness, and detail; state anxiety
experiment: Units repeatedly interact with a virtual stimulus by either checking it or using it (or neither for controls). Multiple pre-, mid-, and post-interaction assessments measure memory accuracy and subjective confidence/vividness/detail. State anxiety is rated across time points. Between-group comparisons test whether checking and use produce equivalent memory degradation. Persistence of effects after delay is examined.

what_manipulated: valence of target action (negative/harmful/blameworthy versus positive/neutral) in Gettier-case scenarios
what_measured: knowledge attribution judgment (does the protagonist know the proposition or not)
experiment: Units read Gettier-case vignettes where the protagonist performs an action that is either negatively or neutrally valenced. The epistemic structure is held constant across valence conditions. Units judge whether the protagonist possesses knowledge. Knowledge attribution rates are compared across valence conditions. Analysis tests whether action valence influences folk knowledge attribution independent of standard Gettier-case structure.

what_manipulated: political polarization measured as dispersion of self-reported political preferences; government regime type (democratic versus undemocratic)
what_measured: size of government (public spending as proportion of GDP)
experiment: Cross-national data from multiple countries are assembled. Political preference distributions within each country are computed from survey data to operationalize polarization. Government size is measured. Association between polarization and spending is tested. Separate analyses stratify by regime type (democratic versus undemocratic). Robustness is checked with controls for GDP per capita, inequality, and other variables.

what_manipulated: crime victimization status (recent victim versus non-victim)
what_measured: political participation (engagement in civic and political activities)
experiment: Survey data from five continents are analyzed. Units are stratified by reported crime victimization status. Political participation metrics (voting, organizational membership, protest, etc.) are compared between victims and non-victims. Within-victim subgroups, secondary outcomes (satisfaction with democracy, support for authoritarianism) are examined. Effect sizes are compared to education years. Heterogeneity across crime types and continents is documented.

what_manipulated: prevalence of emancipative values at individual and ecological (community/country) level
what_measured: non-violent protest participation or likelihood
experiment: Units nested within communities are surveyed for emancipative values and protest participation. Analyses decompose effects into individual-level (own values) and ecological-level (prevalence in context) components. Individual-level associations between values and protest are estimated. Ecological effects are tested: whether prevalence lifts participation above individual level (elevator), and whether prevalence amplifies individual-value effects (amplifier). Cross-level interactions are examined.

what_manipulated: exposure to information about police killing (random timing of survey administration relative to news event of police killing of Black American)
what_measured: attitudes toward metropolitan police; trust, legitimacy, or satisfaction measures
experiment: Public attitude survey is administered to units (Black British, white British, South Asian British) with questionnaire completion dates randomly distributed. Some units complete surveys shortly after police killing event; others complete before. Within-race comparison examines whether survey timing relative to event predicts attitude differences. Separate analyses by race test whether effect concentrates on Black respondents. Attitudinal measures between early and late completers are compared.

what_manipulated: substance use status (cigarette smoking status, cannabis use status, both, neither)
what_measured: frequency and distress from psychotic-like experiences
experiment: Units complete an internet survey reporting demographics, substance use, parental psychiatric history, and psychotic-like experiences (CAPE scale). Associations between substance use and experiences are estimated. Multiple regression models sequentially add controls. Cannabis-only, smoking-only, and joint associations are tested. A model excluding smoking is compared to a fully adjusted model including smoking to assess confounding. Relative effect sizes for cannabis and smoking are compared.

what_manipulated: perceived threat from immigration to national community (naturally occurring variation in concern); country-level immigration history (postwar mass immigration versus not)
what_measured: trust in national politicians and political institutions
experiment: European Social Survey data (2002-2009) spanning multiple countries are analyzed. Units report immigration concern and trust in political system. Country-level immigration history is coded. Association between immigration concern and trust is estimated in pooled models. Country-level immigration history is tested as a moderator. Presence and influence of far-right parties is assessed as potential mechanism. Heterogeneity in effects across countries is explored.

what_manipulated: language-switching practice (multilingual vs monolingual experience)
what_measured: attentional flexibility during task switching (local and global switch costs)
experiment: Units performed a nonverbal task-switching paradigm in which they viewed colored animal pictures and responded to a cue by indicating either the animal or its color. The study compared local switch costs (performance change when switching between two tasks within a mixed block) and global switch costs (performance change from a pure-task to mixed-task block) between bilinguals and monolinguals. Performance was measured via reaction time and accuracy. Statistical comparison of group means via ANOVA or t-tests was conducted.

what_manipulated: perceived rarity of an event (rare vs common event presentation)
what_measured: estimated length of the sequence from which an event was drawn (retrospective gambler's fallacy)
experiment: Units judged the length of a sequence from which a presented event was drawn. In Study 1, streaks were presented; in Study 2, single events with manipulated rarity (rare vs common) were presented; in Study 3, events from real-world domains were used. Units estimated how long the sequence was that produced the observed outcome. The main comparison was between rare-appearing and common-appearing events on sequence-length judgments. Analysis compared mean judgments across conditions.

what_manipulated: salience of post-decision regret and external accountability demands
what_measured: choice distortion by a decoy option (decoy effect magnitude)
experiment: In Experiment 1 (N=62) and Experiment 2 (N=242), units made choices in preference or prediction tasks involving a target option and a decoy option that was inferior to the target but similar to it. The manipulation made salient either the possibility of post-decision regret or external accountability demands (or control). The outcome was the proportion of units choosing the decoy. Between-condition comparisons of decoy-choice rates were conducted via chi-square or logistic regression.

what_manipulated: type of corrective information provided (denial only vs causal explanation)
what_measured: belief in misinformation after debunking (continued influence effect magnitude)
experiment: Units read a scenario containing misinformation about a political event or outcome, then received either a denial of the false claim or a denial plus a causal explanation for the event. Subsequently, units rated their belief in the misinformation and provided open-ended explanations. The primary outcome was the extent to which units continued to endorse the false information despite correction. Between-condition comparisons of misinformation belief and causal reasoning were conducted.

what_manipulated: activation of gender stereotypes about a domain (stereotyped domain vs neutral)
what_measured: performance on a domain-related knowledge test (accuracy)
experiment: Units completed a domain knowledge test either in a condition where gender stereotypes about the domain were made salient or in a control condition. A subset of units participated in an online survey examining stereotype awareness; another subset participated in an experimental condition where stereotype activation was manipulated. Performance was measured via test score. The analysis compared test accuracy across stereotype-salient and control conditions, with separate analyses for men and women.

what_manipulated: thinking about personality traits related to communion (vs control or agency traits)
what_measured: perceived ambient temperature (self-reported warmth rating)
experiment: Units engaged in a task requiring them to think about personality traits that were either communion-focused (sociability or morality) or agency-focused or neutral. Subsequently, units rated their experience of room temperature (perceptual warmth). The main comparison was between communion-activation and control/agency conditions on temperature perception ratings. Between-condition means were compared via ANOVA.

what_manipulated: diagnosis status (ASPD with psychopathy vs ASPD without psychopathy vs control; N=472 incarcerated men)
what_measured: severity of criminal behavior and emotion-processing impairment
experiment: Units (incarcerated men) were classified into three groups based on diagnostic criteria: ASPD with psychopathy, ASPD without psychopathy, and neither diagnosis (controls). The study examined the frequency and severity of criminal offenses (measured via official records) and emotion-facilitation ability (measured via emotional priming task). Group comparisons on criminal activity and emotion processing were conducted via ANOVA or chi-square tests to examine whether comorbid psychopathy uniquely predicted more severe crime and weaker emotion responses.

what_manipulated: type of deescalation intervention (reduce threat salience vs set minimum targets vs evaluate on process vs control)
what_measured: willingness to disengage from a losing course of action (investment decisions in a losing project)
experiment: Units made successive investment decisions regarding a project showing losses. The study compared the effectiveness of three deescalation strategies: (a) manipulating how threatening negative outcomes seemed, (b) establishing minimum acceptable targets that, if not met, would trigger disengagement, and (c) evaluating units on their decision process rather than outcome. The outcome was the point at which units withdrew investment or stopped allocating resources. Between-condition comparisons of investment levels and withdrawal timing were analyzed.

what_manipulated: individual differences in Big Five personality traits (Neuroticism, Extraversion, Openness, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness; across 14 samples, 200+ organizations)
what_measured: transformational leadership behavior (ratings or behavioral indicators)
experiment: Across 14 samples of leaders, personality was measured via Big Five inventories (NEO Five-Factor Inventory or Revised NEO). Leadership behavior was rated by subordinates or assessed via observer ratings. Associations between each trait and transformational leadership were estimated via correlation and multiple regression to examine unique and shared effects. Outcome leadership effectiveness was measured separately and regressed on both transformational and transactional leadership dimensions.

what_manipulated: temporal distance of regret (short-term vs long-term regret of actions vs inactions)
what_measured: magnitude and nature of regret (intensity or duration ratings in scenario tasks and recall)
experiment: Units reported their biggest regrets via telephone surveys, written questionnaires, and face-to-face interviews. Separately, in scenario experiments, units predicted the intensity of short-term and long-term regret for hypothetical actions versus inactions. A third set of units recalled their own regrets of action and inaction from two time periods. The analysis examined whether regret for actions was greater in the short term and regret for inactions was greater in the long term, via comparison of regret ratings across time windows and action-status conditions.

what_manipulated: mortality salience (reminders of death vs control; modulated by depth of thought about mortality and distraction)
what_measured: intergroup bias and preference for in-group content (author preference ratings)
experiment: In Study 1, units were reminded of death or given a control prompt, then rated their preference for a pro-in-group versus anti-in-group author; depth of mortality thought was manipulated. In Studies 2-4, variations examined whether the effect was specific to death thoughts, whether distraction modified the effect, and when accessibility of death thoughts peaked. Between-condition comparisons of author preferences and biases were analyzed. Accessibility of death-related thoughts was measured via word-fragment or thought-listing tasks.

what_manipulated: social value orientation (prosocial, individualistic, competitive classifications based on interdependence motives)
what_measured: developmental antecedents and adult outcomes (secure attachment, sibling composition, age effects on orientation prevalence)
experiment: Units completed measures of social value orientation based on decomposed-game choices (prosocial, individualistic, or competitive). Developmental antecedents including secure attachment and sibling composition were measured retrospectively or via demographic data. Age-related prevalence was examined cross-sectionally across four studies spanning early childhood to old age. Associations between orientation, attachment style, sibling number, and age were examined via chi-square and regression analyses.

what_manipulated: prior exertion of self-regulation (emotion suppression, thought suppression, or regulatory depletion vs control)
what_measured: subsequent self-control capacity (stamina, persistence, or impulse control in a second task)
experiment: In Study 1, units either suppressed emotional responses to an upsetting movie or watched without suppression; physical stamina was then measured. In Study 2, units suppressed forbidden thoughts or engaged in control-condition thinking, then attempted unsolvable anagrams; persistence was recorded. In Study 3, units suppressed thoughts then controlled amusement expression; performance was compared. Study 4 used autobiographical recall of regulatory successes versus failures. The primary analysis examined whether prior regulatory effort reduced subsequent self-control performance via comparison of depletion and control groups on the secondary task measure.

what_manipulated: cultural context (East Asian vs European American; reflected in experimental setting, sample culture, and advertising content)
what_measured: preference for uniqueness versus conformity (choice selections, pen color preferences, advertising theme analysis)
experiment: In Studies 1-3, units from East Asian and European American samples made choices among abstract figures or pens, with variation in how unique versus conformist the options appeared. Study 4 analyzed themes in Korean versus American magazine advertisements. Preferences for uniqueness versus conformity were compared between cultural groups via chi-square or frequency analysis of choices and preferences. Quantitative and content-analytical methods examined how cultural context shaped individual preference patterns.

what_manipulated: latent developmental pathway configuration (Achievers vs Conservers vs Seekers, defined by environmental mastery and personal growth at age 60)
what_measured: maturity criteria (competence, generativity, ego development, wisdom) and longitudinal personality change (emotionality, identity, self-control)
experiment: Units (111 women) from the Mills Longitudinal Study were classified into three groups at age 60 based on environmental mastery and personal growth scores. Each group's profile on four maturity criteria was examined. Longitudinal personality data from age 21 to 60 were compared across groups, examining how emotionality, identity processes, and self-control predicted group membership. Mediation analysis examined whether identity at age 43 mediated the influence of age-21 personality on age-60 developmental pattern. Between-group comparisons and mediation testing were conducted via ANOVA and regression.

what_manipulated: type of transgression scenario (moral transgressions vs nonmoral incompetence) and exposure condition (public vs private)
what_measured: emotion intensity (shame vs guilt ratings)
experiment: Units responded to hypothetical scenarios, recalled personal experiences, or provided coded literary passages in which moral transgressions or nonmoral incompetence events were either publicly exposed or kept private. Ratings or coded classifications of shame and guilt intensity were collected. The analysis examined whether public exposure was more strongly associated with shame than guilt, and whether nonmoral (competence-related) events showed distinctive shame patterns. Between-condition comparisons of shame and guilt ratings were conducted via ANOVA or regression.

what_manipulated: gender group membership (being female vs male) and stereotype strength (math=male stereotypes, operationalized as implicit association strength)
what_measured: implicit and explicit attitudes toward math/science (IAT scores, self-reported preferences and subject identification)
experiment: College units (sampled to include both math-intensive and math-avoiding groups) completed implicit association tests measuring math=male stereotypes, self=female associations, and implicit math attitudes. Explicit attitudes toward math and identification with the subject were also measured. Associations between stereotype measures and attitude outcomes were estimated via correlation and regression, with separate analyses for women and men. The analysis examined how gender stereotypes predicted math attitudes and particularly how the self-female association interfered with math self-identification.

what_manipulated: temporal span (childhood personality and conduct, measured age 8-12 vs adult personality and relationships, measured age 17-23; followed 10 years)
what_measured: adult adaptation outcomes (relationship success, antisocial conduct, emotional and behavioral adjustment)
experiment: Units (N=205) had personality assessed in childhood (ages 8-12) via parent and teacher ratings on Big Five-like dimensions (Positive Emotionality, Negative Emotionality, Constraint) and behavioral observation of conduct. Ten years later, at emerging adulthood (ages 17-23), personality was reassessed and concurrent outcomes (social and romantic success, antisocial behavior, overall adjustment) were measured. Longitudinal associations between childhood and adult personality and between adult personality and concurrent success were estimated via regression. The analysis examined how childhood and adult personality predicted current adaptation.

what_manipulated: cultural group (American vs Japanese) and diagnosticity of socially constrained behavior (diagnostic vs minimally diagnostic)
what_measured: correspondence bias (tendency to infer attitudes matching socially constrained behavior)
experiment: Units (American and Japanese) observed an actor's behavior in a situation where strong social constraints were present. The behavior was either diagnostic of an underlying attitude or minimally diagnostic of the actor's true attitude. Units then inferred the actor's attitude. Study 1 measured correspondence bias directly via attitude inference ratings. Study 2 examined the reasoning process (online inferences) via thought-listing. The analysis tested whether Japanese units, unlike American units, would show reduced correspondence bias when behavior was minimally diagnostic. Between-culture and within-diagnosticity comparisons were conducted via ANOVA and mediation analysis.

what_manipulated: Big Five personality traits (Neuroticism, Extraversion, Openness, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness measured via three different instruments across two independent samples)
what_measured: behavioral outcomes (alcohol consumption, grade point average, and other social/culturally significant behaviors)
experiment: Two independent samples of units (Ns=276 and 142) completed three different Big Five personality measures (NEO Five-Factor Inventory, Revised NEO, and Five-Factor Nonverbal Inventory). Units' behavioral outcomes including alcohol consumption, academic performance, and other criterion variables were measured or obtained from records. Personality-behavior associations were estimated via multiple regression in each sample and across measures. The analysis examined consistency of Big Five predictions across samples and measurement approaches to validate the personality measures and their predictive validity.

what_manipulated: trait variation in a motivational-transcendence construct
what_measured: scale scores on trait and state dimensions; patent holdings; nomological correlates
experiment: A trait measure of inspiration was developed and validated. Two correlational studies established a nomological network relating trait scores to predicted outcomes (state-level experiences, behavioral achievements like patents, and conceptual correlates). The validation examined whether trait scores predict diverse outcomes across life domains. No experimental manipulation is described; instead, trait variation is related to external outcomes and other constructs via correlation and regression.

what_manipulated: target characteristics (liking, misfortune, action valence) and perspective-taking instruction
what_measured: level of action identification; attribution of intentionality and mental states
experiment: Five studies examined how targets are described (high-level effect versus low-level detail) and how mental states are attributed. Units observed targets or read scenarios. In some studies, targets were varied by liking or misfortune status, and their actions were rated. One study manipulated perspective-taking instructions. The relationship between action-identification level and mind-attribution scores was examined correlatively and experimentally across conditions.

what_manipulated: relative left frontal cortical activation (via neurofeedback or action-oriented mindset induction)
what_measured: attitude change toward decision alternatives (spreading of alternatives); brain activation in left frontal region
experiment: Experiment 1 used neurofeedback to reduce relative left frontal activation in units after a decision. Attitude change (spreading) was measured and compared to controls. Experiment 2 manipulated a mindset oriented toward action implementation after a decision, measured left frontal activation and spreading. The hypothesis is that approach-oriented neural processes mediate attitude change following commitment. Analysis compared spreading and activation across conditions.

what_manipulated: type of social exchange (one addressing victim vs. perpetrator emotional needs)
what_measured: willingness to reconcile; restoration of threatened psychological resources (power, moral image)
experiment: Four studies examined whether social interactions designed to restore victims' sense of power and perpetrators' moral image increased reconciliation willingness. Units in interpersonal conflict scenarios participated in or observed exchanges. Reconciliation willingness was measured after exchanges that did or did not address the specific threats posed by each role. Analysis tested whether role-specific needs-satisfaction predicted reconciliation intentions.

what_manipulated: salience of ending / temporal finality (imagining last visit vs. routine visit; reminder on graduation day vs. no reminder)
what_measured: reported mixed emotional experience; concurrent emotion ratings
experiment: Study 1 asked units to imagine a meaningful location visit. Half imagined the final time; the other half did not. Emotional responses were collected and compared. Study 2 measured emotions of college seniors on graduation day, with experimental reminders of the ending event versus control. Mixed-emotion scores were the primary outcome. Analysis compared emotional complexity across ending-salience conditions.

what_manipulated: structural similarity between implicit and explicit test formats
what_measured: score on implicit measure; score on explicit measure; correlation between the two
experiment: Four studies systematically varied the task-structure similarity of implicit and explicit tests (using affect misattribution for the implicit measure). Units completed implicit and explicit attitude tests that differed in structural demands (response modality, timing, task logic). The same underlying construct (racial attitudes) was assessed via each test pair. Implicit-explicit correlations were compared across conditions differing in structural fit. Analysis tested whether format similarity predicts correlation magnitude.

what_manipulated: effort to avoid bias (measured naturally in Study 1; instructed in Study 2)
what_measured: 1/f noise magnitude in latency time-series; effort to regulate bias
experiment: Two studies examined the relationship between effort expenditure and 1/f noise in implicit stereotyping tasks. In Study 1, units with varying efforts to avoid bias (measured via self-report or behavioral indicators) completed implicit measures; latency data were analyzed via Fourier decomposition. Study 2 manipulated instructions emphasizing bias-control effort. 1/f noise was compared across effort conditions. The hypothesis: greater effort reduces nonrandom temporal structure in response latencies.

what_manipulated: salience of stereotype threat (anticipation of conversation with interracial partner; threat context cues)
what_measured: physical distancing behavior; implicit stereotype activation; explicit prejudice; awareness of threat
experiment: Four studies examined distancing in an anticipated-conversation paradigm. Units anticipated conversations with partners of different races under threat-present versus threat-absent conditions. Physical distance chosen was measured. Studies tested whether distancing correlated with stereotype activation (Study 1), was independent of prejudice measures (Study 2), was attenuated by learning-opportunity framing (Study 3), and whether conscious threat-awareness mediated effects (Study 4). Analysis compared distances and mediators across threat conditions.

what_manipulated: none (observational study of actual partners encountered)
what_measured: romantic interest in real partners; perceived attractiveness and earning prospects of partners; stated ideal preferences
experiment: Units participated in speed-dating events and longitudinal follow-ups. Stated preferences (ideal partner criteria) were collected before speed dating. During and after events, units rated actual partners and reported romantic interest. Partner characteristics (attractiveness, earnings) were assessed independently. Romantic interest in real partners was correlated with partner traits. Analysis tested whether stated preferences predicted actual choice and whether sex differences in preferences replicated in real-life versus hypothetical contexts.

what_manipulated: importance level of value invoked during message processing (stated as high versus low importance)
what_measured: amount of message processing; attitude toward issue; resistance to counter-arguments
experiment: Units read a persuasive message and were instructed to consider it in light of a value described as either high or low in importance. Depth of processing during reading was measured (or inferred from memory). Attitudes were assessed immediately and after exposure to counter-arguments. Analysis tested whether high-importance values increased processing depth and whether this mediated resistance to later change. Individual differences in processing were measured and entered as mediators.

what_manipulated: self-regulation resource depletion (via prior effortful task)
what_measured: tendency to seek standpoint-consistent information; commitment to own position; information search pattern
experiment: Four studies used scenarios involving political or economic decisions. Units either completed a depleting self-control task (or not) and then searched for or evaluated information relevant to their standpoint. Confirmatory bias (preference for consistent over inconsistent information) was measured via choice, recall, or rating. Mediation analyses tested whether increased commitment to one's position explained the effect of depletion on bias. Alternative explanations (ego threat, cognitive load, mood) were ruled out via measurement or experimental control.

what_manipulated: mind-set orientation (implemental/planning versus deliberative versus control) following a decision
what_measured: attitude extremity; attitude ambivalence; attitude accessibility; behavior prediction; information processing bias
experiment: Five experiments tested whether an implemental mind-set (focused on how to implement a decision) strengthens attitudes on unrelated issues. In Experiments 1-3, units made or planned a decision in one domain, then rated attitudes on an unrelated issue. Attitude extremity, ambivalence, and accessibility were measured. Experiment 4 tested behavioral prediction 1 week later. Experiment 5 examined whether the effect occurred via selective processing favoring the decision. Analysis compared attitude properties and behavior correlations across mind-set conditions.

what_manipulated: interpersonal risk (experimentally or naturally in relationships); self-esteem variation
what_measured: activation of connectedness and self-protection goals; trust behavior; reliance on partner; explicit goal reports
experiment: One correlational study and eight experiments examined how low and high self-esteem units manage competing goals of closeness and protection. Risk situations (real or hypothetical) were either present or made salient. Units' implicit goal activations (measured via response times or priming) and explicit choices were recorded. Analysis tested the hypothesis that risk activates both goals, and executive control resolves the conflict differently based on self-esteem. High self-esteem units were predicted to approach dependence; low self-esteem units to withdraw.

what_manipulated: opportunity to reflect on own capacity for similar wrongdoing
what_measured: forgiveness toward a transgressor; severity perception; empathic understanding; perceived similarity to transgressor
experiment: Seven studies (scenarios, recalled offenses, group and individual formats) examined whether units who acknowledged their own capacity for similar wrongs reported greater forgiveness. Methods mixed hypothetical and recalled scenarios; designs included correlational (existing offenses) and experimental (vignette manipulations). Severity perception, empathy, and self-other similarity were measured as potential mediators. Analysis tested mediation pathways; sex differences were examined. The effect required similarity in both offense type and severity.

what_manipulated: cultural origin of units; ethnicity and gender of target displaying an expression
what_measured: recognition accuracy for a nonverbal expression; cross-cultural consistency
experiment: Four studies examined whether a nonverbal pride expression is recognized universally. Study 1 compared recognition across U.S. and Italian samples. Study 2 tested recognition in a preliterate, isolated tribe in West Africa. Studies 3 and 4 manipulated target characteristics (ethnicity, gender, drawn figure) while holding expression constant. Recognition accuracy (proportion correct, reaction time, or ratings) was the outcome. Analysis tested whether recognition rates held above chance and whether they were equivalent across cultural and demographic groups.

what_manipulated: coherence between affective concept priming (happy or sad) and affective experience (action, feeling, or expression)
what_measured: recall of narrative content; memory accuracy
experiment: Five studies crossed the priming of affective concepts with concurrent affective experiences. Units received primes (happy or sad words) and then were induced to experience congruent or incongruent affective states via actions, feelings, or expressions. Subsequently, units recalled a story. Recall accuracy was compared across coherence conditions. The prediction: conceptual-experiential alignment enhances cognitive performance via increased epistemic confidence in affect-relevant interpretations.

what_manipulated: processing style (global versus local) induced via perceptual task or time-perspective manipulation
what_measured: assimilation or contrast in subsequent social judgment; category inclusion versus exclusion; hemisphere activation
experiment: Five experiments induced global or local processing and then measured subsequent judgments (disambiguation or social comparison). Units in global-processing conditions showed assimilation (including comparison information); local-processing units showed contrast (excluding information). Hemisphere activation and accessibility of judgment-consistent knowledge were measured as potential mediators. Analysis compared judgment direction across processing styles and tested mediational pathways.

what_manipulated: emotion communicated in bargaining (anger versus happiness); bargaining structure (possibility of deception, consequences of rejection)
what_measured: bargaining outcomes (offers, acceptances); perceived limits of opponent; negotiation success
experiment: Three experimental studies of ultimatum bargaining varied the emotion expressed by the proposer (anger or happiness) and the bargaining structure. Units proposing and responding made and evaluated offers. The hypothesis: anger signals higher limits, increasing pressure to accept low offers, but this backfires when opponents can deceive the proposer or when rejections have low costs. Analysis tested emotion effects on outcomes and identified boundary conditions via structure manipulation.

what_manipulated: diversity cues in organizational setting (minority representation level; diversity philosophy framing as colorblind versus valuing-diversity)
what_measured: perceived identity-contingent threat; trust in organization; psychological safety
experiment: Three experiments presented unit members (African American professionals) with descriptions of workplace settings varying in minority representation and diversity messaging. Settings were crossed to create four conditions (high/low representation x colorblind/diversity-valuing). Perceived threat, trust, and contingency perceptions were measured. Analysis tested whether low representation plus colorblind framing increased threat and reduced trust, and whether identity-contingent evaluations mediated these effects.

what_manipulated: units' own felt communal responsiveness toward a partner (via manipulation or measurement of natural variation)
what_measured: perceived partner responsiveness; attraction and warmth toward partner; self-disclosure; support provision; longitudinal relationship quality changes
experiment: Five studies tested projection of communal responsiveness. In Studies 1-2, units' responsiveness to partners was manipulated; perceived partner responsiveness to self was measured. In Studies 3-5, natural variation in units' responsiveness was measured and related to perceptions, behavior, and relationship outcomes. Analysis tested whether felt responsiveness predicted perceived partner responsiveness (a projection effect) and whether perceived responsiveness predicted attraction, disclosure, support, and longitudinal relationship changes.

what_manipulated: the domain of reasoning task (conjunction, framing, anchoring, outcome bias, base-rate neglect, less-is-more, affect bias, omission bias, myside bias, sunk cost, certainty effects vs. denominator neglect, probability matching, belief bias, matching bias) and cognitive ability
what_measured: performance accuracy on reasoning tasks; tendency to display rational vs. biased thinking
experiment: Seven studies examined the relationship between units' cognitive ability scores and their accuracy on diverse reasoning tasks. Units completed batteries of tasks tapping classic heuristics-and-biases phenomena. Correlations between cognitive ability and task performance were computed. Most reasoning biases showed no correlation with ability. A subset of rational thinking measures (denominator neglect, probability maximizing, belief bias, matching bias) showed positive correlations with ability. A framework predicting when ability correlates with rational tendency was developed.

what_manipulated: exposure to mortality salience (thoughts of death) and attachment style (secure vs. insecure)
what_measured: death-thought accessibility; worldview defense; self-worth; ease of recalling positive parental interactions; ease of recalling negative parental interactions; attraction to stranger similar to parent; reliance on parental vs. romantic relationships
experiment: Six studies examined how mortality salience affected units' reliance on parental attachment relationships. Studies 1-3 used mortality salience manipulation; units either thought about their parent or not. Death-thought accessibility, worldview defense, and self-worth were measured. Studies 4-5 examined memory effects and stranger attraction. Study 6 tested whether effects varied by attachment style (secure vs. insecure). Mortality salience increased positive parental recall and reduced negative parental recall; insecure units increased parental reliance whereas secure units increased romantic partner reliance.

what_manipulated: age (10 to 20 years) and item difficulty (comprehension ease)
what_measured: self-report personality trait coherence within domains; differentiation across domains; acquiescent responding variability; Big Five factor structure
experiment: Self-report personality data were examined across ages 10 to 20 from a large internet sample. Year-by-year developmental patterns were mapped. Acquiescence responding (tendency to agree with items regardless of content) and its influence on psychometric properties were quantified. Domain coherence and cross-domain differentiation were assessed separately for each Big Five trait. Verbal comprehension difficulty was coded to rule out reading comprehension as a confound. Age-related gains in coherence and differentiation differed by trait.

what_manipulated: type of deviance from group average attitude (descriptive deviance: aligned with desirable norm vs. prescriptive deviance: misaligned with desirable norm)
what_measured: willingness to express opinion; comfort expressing opinion; pride after expressing opinion; public visibility of expressed opinion
experiment: Three studies tested whether units with opinions deviating from the group average in a normatively desirable direction (descriptive deviants) were more willing to express their views than units who deviated in an undesirable direction (prescriptive deviants) or nondeviants. Study 1 measured comfort with expression. Study 2 measured pride and observer ratings of pride. Study 3 examined real-world behavior by comparing frequency of political bumper stickers. Descriptive deviants reported greater comfort and pride, and their messages appeared more frequently than prescriptive deviant messages.

what_manipulated: social role (participant vs. observer) and moral behavior (refusing unethical task vs. complying with unethical task); prior self-description of values
what_measured: liking or preference for the moral rebel vs. complier; perception that rebel rejects complier; rejection of rebel
experiment: Four studies examined whether units rejected moral rebels (those refusing unethical tasks). Studies 1 and 2 varied the unit's role: active participant in unethical task vs. observer. Units rated liking for a moral rebel or complier. Study 3 examined mediators by assessing units' perception that rebels would reject them. Study 4 manipulated whether units first described important traits or values. Units rejected rebels when they were complicit but observers preferred rebels. Rejection was mediated by perceived rejection threat and eliminated by prior self-affirmation.

what_manipulated: presence of self-handicap (excuse availability) and upward counterfactual thinking tendency
what_measured: self-esteem following failure; upward counterfactual thought generation; future performance motivation; actual subsequent performance
experiment: Five studies examined how upward counterfactual thoughts (imagining better outcomes) affected self-esteem and motivation, specifically in the context of self-handicapping (providing excuses). Units completed tasks with or without available self-handicaps. Counterfactual thinking was measured. Self-esteem and preparation for subsequent performance were assessed. When self-handicaps were available, upward counterfactuals protected self-esteem but reduced subsequent preparation and performance. The moderating role of content (excuse vs. improvement focus) was demonstrated.

what_manipulated: action type (tempts fate vs. does not tempt fate) and accessibility of negative outcomes
what_measured: perceived likelihood of negative outcomes; intuitive judgment of bad luck; accessibility of negative prospects
experiment: Six studies examined the belief that actions can tempt fate and increase negative outcome likelihood. Studies 1-2 confirmed units held this intuition. Studies 3-5 tested whether the intuition arose from automatic tendencies to attend to negative outcomes and use mental accessibility as a probability cue. Study 3 demonstrated heightened accessibility of negative outcomes for fate-tempting actions. Studies 4-5 showed this accessibility mediated likelihood judgments. Study 6 examined automaticity via reaction time. Negative outcome accessibility was shown to be the key mechanism linking fate-tempting actions to inflated negative likelihood judgments.

what_manipulated: expression of emotional vulnerability to relationship partner and reflected appraisal (belief that partner views unit as vulnerable)
what_measured: doubt in partner's authenticity of expressed positive regard; perception of partner rejection; subsequent expression of vulnerability; partner derogation
experiment: Five studies examined a feedback cycle model: units' expression of relational insecurity leads them to believe the partner views them as insecure, which causes doubt in the partner's positive expressions, which increases perceived rejection and perpetuates future insecurity expression. Study 1 identified prototypes of interactions with vulnerable people as including inauthentic regard. Studies 2-4 showed that expressed vulnerability increased doubts about partner authenticity, which predicted rejection perception and subsequent vulnerability expression. Study 5 experimentally manipulated reflected appraisal of vulnerability and measured authenticity doubts. A self-perpetuating cognitive cycle was demonstrated.

what_manipulated: time (longitudinal tracking); marital status or quality changes over time
what_measured: marital quality; marital stability (divorce vs. persistence); predictors of change in quality and stability
experiment: A narrative and quantitative review examined 115 longitudinal studies tracking 45,000+ marriages over time. Theoretical frameworks for explaining marital change were evaluated. Study methods and findings were summarized. Developmental trajectories of marital quality and stability were identified. Patterns of decline, stability, and recovery in marriages were documented. An integrated theoretical model was developed to explain how marriages change and which factors predict dissolution vs. persistence.

what_manipulated: personality trait (137 distinct constructs examined); Big Five factors
what_measured: subjective well-being (life satisfaction, happiness, positive affect, negative affect)
experiment: A meta-analytic review examined correlations between 137 personality traits and well-being outcomes across studies. Multiple literature search strategies were used. Effect sizes were extracted and aggregated. Personality traits were grouped into Big Five categories and separately examined. Relationships between trait categories and specific well-being dimensions (life satisfaction, happiness, positive affect, negative affect) were quantified. Neuroticism emerged as strongest predictor of life satisfaction, happiness, and negative affect; Extraversion and Agreeableness equally predicted positive affect.

what_manipulated: affect type (positive affect vs. negative affect) and state vs. trait affect
what_measured: job-related attitudes (job satisfaction, organizational commitment, turnover intentions, job burnout dimensions); emotional exhaustion; personal accomplishment
experiment: A psychometric meta-analysis reviewed 205 studies (total pairwise N = 62,527) examining associations between positive and negative affect and job-related attitudes. Effect sizes were extracted and aggregated across studies. Correlations between affect and attitude variables were quantified. Moderator analyses tested whether relationships varied by context. Meta-analytic multiple regression examined unique contributions of positive and negative affect to each attitude. Negative affect showed strongest correlations with emotional exhaustion (r = .54); Positive affect and negative affect both uniquely contributed to predicting job attitudes.

what_manipulated: stimulus properties (normality, abnormality of causes) and category vs. stimulus norms
what_measured: emotional response intensity; judgment accuracy; causal reasoning; perceived surprise or unusualness
experiment: A theoretical framework was presented for how norms and normality function in cognition. Norms were proposed to be constructed ad hoc via retrieval of exemplars or counterfactual alternatives rather than precomputed. Category norms (knowledge of concepts) and stimulus norms (comparative judgments) were distinguished. The theory was applied to emotional responses to abnormal-cause events, prediction and inference from behavior, and causal reasoning. Normality was assessed by comparing stimuli to norms they evoke rather than to fixed expectations. Applications to visual displays and social interactions were sketched.

what_manipulated: availability of outcome information (hindsight condition vs. foresight) and consideration of alternative outcomes
what_measured: perceived predictability of scientific results; belief in having known the outcome in advance; hindsight bias magnitude
experiment: Three experiments examined hindsight bias applied to scientific research. Units evaluated predictability of research findings after being told the outcome (hindsight condition) or without outcome information (foresight control). Outcome knowledge inflated perceived predictability. The hindsight effect was reduced when units were prompted to consider alternative possible outcomes. Results demonstrated that lay observers apply hindsight bias to scientific studies, reducing their perceived informativeness and novelty.

what_manipulated: scope or magnitude of stimulus and decision process (feeling-based vs. calculation-based valuation)
what_measured: subjective preference value; sensitivity to scope variation; functional form of value function (step function vs. linear)
experiment: Units evaluated the value of stimuli while being instructed to rely on either affective feeling or rational calculation. Value was measured across different stimulus scopes. When units relied on feeling, they showed sensitivity to stimulus presence vs. absence but insensitivity to further scope variations (step function). When units used calculation, they showed continuous sensitivity to scope (linear function). The contrasting psychological processes produced different shapes of value functions.

what_manipulated: event probability (low vs. high) and construal level (high-level abstract vs. low-level concrete representations)
what_measured: categorization breadth; inclusiveness; preference for general vs. specific descriptions; behavioral segmentation; visual abstraction accuracy; detail identification; action description focus (means vs. ends)
experiment: Seven studies tested that decreasing event probability increases abstract thinking. Units were exposed to low- or high-probability event descriptions. Effects were measured on categorization breadth (Study 1), inclusiveness (Study 2), activity description preference (Study 3), behavior segmentation (Study 4), visual abstraction (Study 5), detail identification in visual scenes (Study 6), and action coding (Study 7). Low-probability exposure increased abstract representations and decreased concrete detail focus. Results supported the hypothesis that low probability triggers high-level construal.

what_manipulated: prior executive control exertion (attention control, response inhibition, working memory load, emotion regulation)
what_measured: subsequent executive control performance on working memory span, reverse digit span, response inhibition; resource depletion
experiment: Four experiments tested whether initial executive control efforts temporarily impair subsequent control. Units completed a first task requiring attention control (Exp. 1), writing inhibition (Exp. 2), working memory (Exp. 3), or emotion regulation (Exp. 4). Immediately after, units performed a second executive control task. Prior effort reduced subsequent performance on different executive measures, supporting a limited-resource model. Alternative explanations (mood, motivation, task difficulty) were tested and ruled out.

what_manipulated: time orientation (anticipation of future events vs. retrospection about past events) and event type (positive, negative, routine, hypothetical)
what_measured: emotion intensity ratings; expected vs. remembered emotion intensity; mental simulation extensiveness
experiment: Five experiments compared units' emotional intensity during anticipation vs. retrospection about diverse events (Thanksgiving, annoying noises, menstruation, ski vacation). Units reported stronger emotions during anticipation than retrospection. Expected future emotions were slightly more intense than remembered past emotions, but this difference did not fully explain the anticipation effect. Units reported mentally simulating future events more extensively than past events. Extensive mental simulation of future events was shown to mediate the greater evocativeness of anticipation.

what_manipulated: experienced processing difficulty or disfluency (degraded font, difficult-to-read lettering, furrowed brow) and reasoning task type
what_measured: use of heuristics and defaults; reliance on peripheral cues in persuasion; syllogistic reasoning accuracy; impact of intuitive biases
experiment: Four experiments induced incidental experiences of processing difficulty via perceptual (font degradation, difficult lettering) or motor (furrowed brow) manipulations. Units then completed reasoning tasks assessing heuristic reliance (Studies 1, 3), persuasion bias (Study 2), or syllogistic reasoning (Study 4). Difficulty-induced disfluency reduced reliance on intuitive heuristics and peripheral cues, and improved reasoning accuracy. Results supported that metacognitive experiences of difficulty activate analytical System 2 reasoning processes that override intuitive defaults.

what_manipulated: recall task (order recall vs. timing recall) and stimulus timing (irregular spacing) and grouping instruction
what_measured: serial order recall accuracy; timing recall accuracy; pause timing during recall; effect of temporal grouping
experiment: Three experiments examined relationships between short-term memory for order and timing information. Units were presented digits spaced irregularly in time, then given postcued (Exp. 1-2) or precued (Exp. 3) instructions to recall order or timing. Grouping instructions, recall pause timing, input-output timing correlations, and effects of temporal crowding vs. isolation were analyzed. Temporal grouping affected both order and timing recall similarly. Recall timing was predicted by input timing. Temporally isolated items showed better recall than crowded items in precued conditions.

what_manipulated: item type (words vs. nonwords vs. orthographically similar pairs) and participant strategy (guessing vs. strict identification) and experimental design (with vs. without identical items)
what_measured: repetition blindness: failure to detect repeated items; repetition advantage: better detection of repetitions; false alarm rate for similar items
experiment: Six experiments examined when repetition blindness (RB) occurs for nonwords vs. words. Initial replication (Exp. 1) found RB for words but repetition advantage for nonwords. Experiments 2-6 tested whether strategy differences explained the discrepancy. When encouragement and task design allowed partial-information guessing, repetition advantage appeared (Exp. 2). When guessing was detectable via inclusion of non-identical similar items, RB appeared for both word and nonword conditions (Exp. 3-4). In designs with no identical items, RB was reliable for similar pairs (Exp. 5-6). Participant strategy modulated the phenomenon.

what_manipulated: characteristics of the stochastic environment (task structure with different learning requirements)
what_measured: risk-taking behavior and quality of risk-taking assessment
experiment: Units performed sequential decision-making tasks with varying task structures that imposed different learning demands across stochastic environments. A Bayesian model was fit to explain how learning and decision-making processes changed with task context. Analysis evaluated whether the model's parameters and predictions matched observed behavior across the different task types.

what_manipulated: sampling strategy (natural sampling versus deliberate sampling of relevant information) and feedback valence (positive versus negative)
what_measured: accuracy of inferences about population parameters from sample data
experiment: Units acted as a manager and sampled information from a computerized database to evaluate providers and products, choosing between a natural sampling strategy or a deliberate strategy optimized for inference. After sampling ended, units made probabilistic inferences about the actual population parameters. Computer simulations and experiments compared inference accuracy across the two strategies.

what_manipulated: none (theoretical model evaluation)
what_measured: distribution of effect sizes for two memory phenomena
experiment: A computational model was applied to predict effect-size distributions from immediate memory tasks. Model predictions were compared against observed distributions of phonological confusion effects and word-length effects. Analysis examined whether the model correctly predicted the frequency of reversals (cases where the expected effect direction flipped) and how effect sizes varied as a function of overall performance level.

what_manipulated: retrieval practice followed by relearning (presence versus absence of prior retrieval-induced forgetting)
what_measured: final recall accuracy of relearned items and cumulative forgetting effects
experiment: Units studied category-exemplar pairs, underwent retrieval practice and relearning trials, and then took a final recall test. The key manipulation was whether items had been subjected to retrieval-induced forgetting before relearning. Analysis compared recall of relearned items that had been forgotten versus those that had not.

what_manipulated: stimulus presentation schedule (intermixed presentation versus blocked presentation of similar stimuli)
what_measured: discrimination accuracy between similar stimuli on a test
experiment: Units received exposure to pairs of similar visual stimuli under either intermixed conditions (where presentations of the two stimuli were separated from one another) or blocked conditions (where all presentations of one stimulus preceded all presentations of the other). Discrimination performance on a subsequent test compared the two exposure regimes. Analysis tested whether spacing differences between the conditions could explain the observed effect.

what_manipulated: phonological similarity between to-be-remembered items and irrelevant sound stream, and type of recall test (free versus serial)
what_measured: recall accuracy and intrusion error rate
experiment: Units studied lists of phonologically related items while hearing an irrelevant sound stream that either matched or differed in phonological content. Recall was tested in two formats: free recall or serial-position recall.
