Analysis examined whether phonological similarity between items and sounds disrupted each recall type differently, and whether category-cueing processes explained the disruption pattern.

what_manipulated: temporal relationship between picture and distractor word (simultaneous versus delayed by 1000 ms), semantic relatedness of distractor to target, and frequency of target name
what_measured: naming latency
experiment: Units named pictures with superimposed distractor words. In one condition, pictures and words appeared simultaneously and units responded immediately. In another condition, the picture preceded the word by 1000 ms and units delayed their response. Distractors were either semantic coordinates or unrelated to the picture. Analysis examined whether semantic interference effects and frequency effects appeared in both timing conditions.

what_manipulated: predictable switches between stimulus categories across trials
what_measured: magnitude of effects of stimulus category on phonological processing
experiment: Units read aloud words and nonwords across five experiments. Stimulus category (word versus nonword) switched unpredictably or predictably between trials. Changes in reading processing were inferred from the magnitude of category effects on response latency. Analysis tested whether phonological processes adapted according to predictions of the pathway control hypothesis.

what_manipulated: type of element relationship embedded in stimulus sequences (adjacent versus nonadjacent dependencies) and task demands requiring active processing of specific relationship types
what_measured: learning of relations between elements, measured by recognition or discrimination of new instances
experiment: Units encountered sequences of materials containing both adjacent and nonadjacent element pairings. Task demands determined which type of relationship units needed to actively process. Learning was assessed by testing units on new sequences containing only the type of relationship they had been required to process. Analysis evaluated whether spatial contiguity of elements or attentional focus determined what relationships were learned.

what_manipulated: none (computational model application to existing data)
what_measured: fit of single-system model to patterns of priming, recognition, and fluency effects
experiment: A computational model was applied to continuous identification paradigms from prior studies. Model simulations were used to test whether patterns interpreted as evidence for multiple memory systems could instead be explained by a single unified system. Analysis examined priming without recognition, changes in reaction time patterns across recognition outcomes, and fluency effects in amnesic patients.

what_manipulated: language background (monolingual versus bilingual)
what_measured: inhibitory control performance across three different inhibition paradigms
experiment: Monolingual and bilingual units completed three tasks measuring different aspects of inhibition: stop signal performance, inhibition of return, and attentional blink. Measures of active inhibition (stopping action in progress) and reactive inhibition (maintaining goals to filter irrelevant information) were derived from task performance. Between-group comparisons tested whether language background predicted inhibition abilities.

what_manipulated: semantic relations between prime words (categorical versus functional versus unrelated alignment with arithmetic structure)
what_measured: magnitude of automatic activation of arithmetic facts
experiment: Units performed a task designed to elicit obligatory activation of arithmetic facts when seeing number pairs. Before seeing each number pair, word pairs were presented that either aligned or misaligned with the arithmetic structure through their semantic relation. Analysis measured whether semantic primes facilitated automatic activation of the corresponding arithmetic fact, compared across aligned and misaligned priming conditions.

what_manipulated: task context (picture naming, distractor picture type, word presentation, word reading) and semantic or phonological relatedness of concurrent stimuli
what_measured: latency of gaze shifts and vocal response timing
experiment: Units performed picture naming and word reading tasks while eye movements and naming latencies were recorded. In different experiments, units encountered semantically related distractors, phonologically related distractors, or unrelated distractors, and the effect of distractor on latencies was measured. Analysis examined how activation spreads from concepts to phonological forms and whether activation patterns differ across task contexts.

what_manipulated: attentional cue timing (retrospective cue presented after encoding and before testing versus no cue)
what_measured: change detection accuracy in visual working memory
experiment: Units encoded arrays of visual items and performed change detection on a subsequent test. On some trials, a retrospective spatial cue was presented 1 second after encoding and 0.5 seconds before testing, directing attention to one item. Analysis compared change detection accuracy between cued and uncued trials and tested whether improvements resulted from reduced decay, reduced interference, or increased attentional robustness against interference.

what_manipulated: the statistical structure of feature dimensions (perfect versus partial covariation between color and shape features)
what_measured: detection of learned statistical regularities, measured as implicit learning of feature patterns
experiment: Units were exposed to sequences of colored shapes where shape-color relationships varied. In some conditions, each shape appeared in only one color (perfect covariation). In others, shapes and colors were partially decoupled. Implicit learning was tested by presenting new sequences and measuring whether units detected violations of learned regularities. Analysis determined whether learning operated over integrated objects or independent features, depending on feature correlation structure.

what_manipulated: item strength manipulation method (spaced presentations versus processing time versus processing depth) and directed forgetting instruction
what_measured: magnitude of directed forgetting effect (recall difference between items to-be-remembered and items to-be-forgotten)
experiment: Units studied lists of items that were strengthened either through spaced exposures or through deeper processing without context changes. After study, units received instructions to forget some items. Final recall was tested and the magnitude of forgetting for strong versus weak items was compared across the different strength-manipulation methods. Analysis evaluated whether context strength or item strength determines the directed forgetting effect.

what_manipulated: notational format of numbers (same notation versus different notations; Arabic versus Indian numerals)
what_measured: automatic processing of numerical magnitude (reaction time and accuracy to task-irrelevant numerical dimension)
experiment: Bilingual units performed size comparison and same-different judgment tasks with digit pairs. Pairs were presented in the same notation or different notations. In the size comparison task, units compared physical size while irrelevant numerical magnitude was varied. In same-different task, units judged physical identity while notation congruity varied. Analysis measured whether numerical magnitude was automatically processed across notations.

what_manipulated: type of test probe (positive probes matching list items, new probes, intrusion probes that matched items studied in different locations) and spatial location
what_measured: recognition accuracy and serial position effects
experiment: Units studied lists of items in specific spatial locations and performed short-term recognition by indicating whether probes matched list items at the specified locations. Probe types included matches, new items, and intrusions (matching items from different locations). Serial position curves were plotted for each probe type. Analysis examined whether dual-process theory (familiarity and recollection) predicted the observed patterns.

what_manipulated: stereotype activation (source identity labeled as consistent versus inconsistent with stereotype) and expectation congruence of test items with stereotypes
what_measured: source attribution accuracy and false memory recollections
experiment: Units studied statements presented by two sources and learned that one source had a stereotyped identity. On a source memory test, units attributed items to sources. Analysis examined whether stereotype-consistent attributions increased and whether units reported detailed recollections of stereotypes they had falsely remembered. Experiments tested whether false recollections resulted from familiarity misattribution or from constructed recollections.

what_manipulated: number of task switches within a continuous complex span task and concurrent working memory load
what_measured: recall accuracy in the complex span task
experiment: Units performed continuous complex span tasks requiring maintenance of items while performing processing operations that involved task switches. The number of task switches per trial was varied while keeping time parameters constant. Concurrent working memory load was manipulated orthogonally. Analysis examined whether recall performance declined as a function of the number of switches and whether item maintenance load influenced switching effects.

what_manipulated: stimulus quality (visual degradation) and word frequency; varied nonword type (legal vs. pseudohomophone)
what_measured: lexical decision speed (response time) and response-time distribution characteristics
experiment: Units performed lexical decisions on words and nonwords varying in stimulus quality and frequency, with nonword type manipulated between conditions. Response times and distributional properties were analyzed to reveal whether effects combined additively or interactively. Four experiments tested different combinations of stimulus properties and nonword types. Mean response times and full response-time distributions were compared across conditions.

what_manipulated: item-specific proportion congruent: how often a particular word appears in its congruent vs. incongruent color (varied probability, e.g., 75% vs. 25%)
what_measured: Stroop effect magnitude (difference in response time between congruent and incongruent trials)
experiment: Units performed a Stroop task where stimulus words appeared in matching or mismatching colors. The frequency of congruency was manipulated separately for each word item. Response times were analyzed to compare predictions of modulation versus contingency hypotheses. Reanalysis of prior data and new experiments tested whether units adjusted attention based on word identity or predicted expected responses.

what_manipulated: sequence repetition: presentation of an identical sequence of items multiple times vs. varying sequences within an experimental session
what_measured: serial recall accuracy and probability of repeating previously produced responses (correct or erroneous)
experiment: Units performed serial recall of item sequences throughout a session. One sequence was repeated across trials while others varied. Learning was indexed by the increased probability of reproducing prior responses. Analysis examined both correct responses (standard Hebb effect) and erroneous responses, revealing cumulative learning of both. Statistical tests assessed how prior response frequency predicted future recall.

what_manipulated: direction of practice and operand type: whether units practiced multiplication only, factoring only, or both; tested on multiplication or factoring problems
what_measured: accuracy and speed in fact retrieval (multiplication or factoring response)
experiment: Units practiced arithmetic problems (multiplication or factoring) and were tested on related problems in the alternate direction. Experiment 1 tested transfer from multiplication to factoring; Experiment 2 tested transfer from factoring to multiplication; Experiment 3 trained both directions. Performance was compared within items to detect facilitation or interference. Bidirectional links in memory were inferred from transfer patterns.

what_manipulated: action vs. inaction goals instilled through word primes (e.g., 'action' vs. 'rest'); tested across multiple behavioral contexts
what_measured: magnitude of motor output (e.g., doodling, eating) and cognitive output (e.g., recall, problem-solving)
experiment: Units received word primes designed to activate action or inaction goals. Subsequent behavior was measured across diverse tasks including motor and cognitive domains. Initial experiments (1-5) tested whether the same primes influenced qualitatively different behaviors; final experiments (6-7) tested whether goal-achievement reduced prime effects. Performance was compared between action and inaction prime conditions.

what_manipulated: scenario content: whether described action involved gay or heterosexual kissing; measured disgust sensitivity (trait, correlational)
what_measured: attribution of intentionality to an agent; implicit automatic associations (via Implicit Association Test)
experiment: Study 1: Units read scenarios where an agent's action had a side effect of promoting gay or heterosexual kissing, then rated whether the outcome was intentional. Study 2: Units completed an Implicit Association Test measuring automatic associations with sexual orientation groups. Disgust sensitivity was measured as a trait. Correlations and moderation analyses examined whether disgust sensitivity predicted judgment patterns and automatic associations.

what_manipulated: visual perspective in action imagery: first-person own perspective vs. third-person observer perspective; measured and manipulated within imagery and photography
what_measured: action identification level: abstract (goal/outcome-focused) vs. concrete (component-process-focused) interpretation
experiment: Units viewed or imagined actions presented from first-person or third-person visual perspectives. Action identification level was measured via response to how units would describe the action. Experiments manipulated perspective through instructions or stimuli and assessed resulting identification level. Bidirectional tests examined whether perspective influenced identification and vice versa.

what_manipulated: native language background: English vs. Spanish vs. Asian-language speaking units; timing via longitudinal assessment
what_measured: reading achievement and growth rates in overall reading and reading subskills
experiment: Growth trajectories were estimated from longitudinal reading assessment data in a large population sample. Units were grouped by native language. Multilevel latent variable growth modeling estimated initial achievement and growth rates. Socioeconomic status effects were modeled as a covariate. Language-group differences were compared in trajectories and the effects of SES were examined as a moderator.

what_manipulated: song stimuli: naturally varying in autobiographical salience, arousal, familiarity, emotional valence; individual differences in nostalgia proneness and personality
what_measured: nostalgic experience intensity; emotional response (positive, negative, mixed); mood and personality dimensions as moderators
experiment: Units listened to music excerpts and rated nostalgic feeling. Song properties (salience, arousal, familiarity, emotions elicited) were assessed. Individual differences (nostalgia proneness, mood, personality dimensions) were measured. Multiple regression and moderation analyses examined how song and person factors predicted nostalgia intensity and emotional responses.

what_manipulated: working memory demand: maintenance load (1 vs. 2 items) and distractor interference (confusable vs. non-confusable with memory items); within-subjects factorial design
what_measured: accuracy and speed in delayed recognition of memoranda; performance on trial immediately following high/low demand trials
experiment: Units performed a working-memory delayed-recognition task. Demand factors (memory load and distractor confusability) were crossed within-subjects. Performance on a trial was analyzed as a function of demand level on the previous trial. Dynamic adjustments in cognitive control were inferred from post-high-demand performance benefits relative to post-low-demand performance.

what_manipulated: color of stimulus context: red background/clothing vs. other colors; varied across 7 experiments; gender of perceiver (women vs. men perceiving men vs. women)
what_measured: perceived attractiveness, sexual desirability, and status; awareness of color manipulation
experiment: Units viewed images of men presented on red or control-color backgrounds or in red vs. control-color clothing. Attractiveness and sexual desirability were rated. Status perceptions were measured. Seven experiments tested effects across contexts (backgrounds, clothing, photographs). Gender comparisons examined whether effects were specific to women viewing men. Awareness was assessed through debriefing.

what_manipulated: social class (lower vs. upper socioeconomic status) as independent variable measured via demographic characteristics or manipulated via prime/context
what_measured: prosocial behavior: generosity, charity, trust, helpfulness; mediators (egalitarian values, compassion); moderators
experiment: Four studies examined prosocial behavior across social-class groups. Study 1 measured generosity; Study 2 measured charitable giving; Study 3 measured trust; Study 4 measured helping. Class differences were analyzed. Mediation analyses tested whether egalitarian values and compassion explained class-based prosocial differences. Moderator analyses examined boundary conditions.

what_manipulated: temporal direction: judgment of unethical or ethical actions in the future (prospective) vs. equivalent past actions (retrospective); event distance held constant
what_measured: moral judgment severity; expected punishment; willingness to sacrifice for fair treatment; emotional intensity
experiment: Seven studies presented units with scenarios of good or bad deeds located in the future or equidistant past. Units rated moral acceptability and judgment severity. Punishment deservingness was assessed. Economic sacrifice was measured via game decisions. Emotional responses were examined. Temporal direction and valence (good vs. bad) were analyzed with emotions as potential mediators.

what_manipulated: temporal sequence: response obtained before (vs. after) target stimulus; established effects time-reversed (erotic stimuli, negative stimuli, priming target, habituation target, recall target)
what_measured: response magnitude (approach/avoidance, priming effect, habituation, recall facilitation); effect sizes and statistical significance
experiment: Nine experiments tested for retroactive influence by reversing the temporal order of stimulus and response in well-established psychological effects. Units' responses were obtained before target stimulus events occurred. Effect sizes (Cohen's d) were calculated across conditions. Individual differences in stimulus-seeking were measured and correlated with effect magnitude. Statistical significance and replication across experiments were examined.

what_manipulated: physical warmth experience: warm baths/showers, physical coldness manipulation, or interpolated warmth following rejection recall
what_measured: loneliness (chronic and induced); affiliation and emotion-regulation needs; awareness of physical-social warmth link; bathing frequency
experiment: Study 1 correlated chronic loneliness with warm-bath frequency. Study 2 manipulated physical coldness and measured loneliness increase. Study 3 triggered affiliation/emotion-regulation needs via rejection recall, then provided warmth; needs were remeasured. Study 4 assessed whether units explicitly recognize the warmth-loneliness link. Correlations, manipulations, and mediation analyses tested substitutability of physical and social warmth.

what_manipulated: scope of information processing: associative progression (connecting multiple concepts) vs. restricted single-topic processing; manipulated via task instructions
what_measured: mood state; self-reported affect
experiment: Two experiments manipulated information-processing scope and measured subsequent mood. Experiment 1 compared associative progression to single-topic processing. Experiment 2 tested whether conceptual plurality alone (without progression) accounted for mood differences. Statistical comparisons examined mood as a function of processing type. Analysis converged on the role of conceptual advancement rather than plurality.

what_manipulated: social structure type: hierarchical relationships (asymmetric power) vs. egalitarian relationships (symmetry) vs. other comparison structures; presentation modality (pairs of faces, diagrams, learned relationships)
what_measured: processing fluency (identification speed, memorization time, learning ease); preference ratings; decision-making efficiency
experiment: Five studies presented units with social relationships or organizations varying in hierarchy. Study 1: facial-dominance pairs identified faster in hierarchy vs. equality. Study 2: hierarchy diagrams memorized faster. Study 3: learned hierarchical relationships fastest. Study 4: hierarchical companies required easier decisions. Study 5: tested familiarity basis. Fluency and preference were measured; liking was predicted by processing ease.

what_manipulated: body focus: revealing vs. nonrevealing pictures; or directed attention to physical vs. psychological characteristics
what_measured: mind perception on two dimensions (agency: self-control/action; experience: emotion/sensation); moral responsibility; moral patienthood (sensitivity to harm)
experiment: Six experiments tested the effect of body focus on mind attribution. Experiments 1-4 varied body focus (pictures or attention direction) and measured agency and experience perceptions. Experiments 5-6 tested moral intuitions (responsibility and sensitivity to harm). Body focus was compared between conditions. Analysis examined the redistribution of perceived mental capacities and consequences for moral judgment.

what_manipulated: purchase type: experiential (vacations, concerts) vs. material (clothes, gadgets); framing of items as experiential vs. material qualities
what_measured: self-centrality: physical proximity to self (drawing task), spontaneous mention in life narrative, overlap with identity; perceived insight into person's true self; satisfaction and happiness
experiment: Five studies examined whether experiences are more self-central than possessions. Study 1: units drew purchases closer physically if experiential. Study 2: experiential purchases mentioned more in life stories. Studies 3A-3C: experiential frame increased perceived self-overlap and insight value. Study 5: self-centrality correlated with greater satisfaction. Comparisons tested whether self-centrality explained the experience-satisfaction advantage.

what_manipulated: secret-keeping: recall or preoccupation with important personal secrets vs. control; secret burden measured as trait (importance, thought devotion)
what_measured: perception of hills (steepness), distances, effort for physical tasks; helping behavior; action tendency (likelihood of helping with physical tasks)
experiment: Four studies examined units who harbored important secrets or were primed to think about them. Units estimated hill steepness, distance to objects, and effort required for tasks. Helping behavior was measured. Burdensome-ness of the secret and cognitive preoccupation were assessed. Comparisons and regressions examined whether secret burden effects paralleled the effects of physical weight on perception and action.

what_manipulated: structure of two-outcome choice sequences (timing, magnitude, and presence of zero-padding outcomes)
what_measured: choice preference (patience vs. impatience in intertemporal decisions)
experiment: Units made pairwise choices between sequences involving two monetary outcomes distributed across time. The structure of the sequences was varied systematically (e.g., adding front-end amounts, explicitly stating zero outcomes, relocating timing). Choices were analyzed to detect preference patterns and violations of standard economic predictions, with quantitative fitting of the tradeoff model to choice data.

what_manipulated: sexual orientation identity status (heterosexual, bisexual, gay across adolescence)
what_measured: muscularity concern, weight gain attempts, and weight/shape concern trajectories from ages 9-25
experiment: A longitudinal cohort of 5,868 units from a prospective study spanning 16 years reported preferences and body-change behaviors across multiple timepoints. Generalized estimating equations modeled the effect of sexual orientation on growth trajectories for three outcomes. Sexual minority status was compared against heterosexual baseline groups to detect differences in developmental patterns.

what_manipulated: incidental exposure to money-related primes (subtle reminders of money vs. nonmoney concepts)
what_measured: endorsement of system-legitimizing beliefs (support for capitalism, belief in just-world, support for group dominance)
experiment: Units received primes designed to activate money concepts or control concepts, then completed measures assessing attitudes toward economic systems and social hierarchy. Five separate experiments tested the effect across different outcome measures (system endorsement, victim-blaming, group dominance, market preference, transplant system preference). Statistical comparisons tested whether money primes increased ideology-relevant responses relative to control primes.

what_manipulated: presence vs. absence of overconfidence (naturally varying or experimentally induced)
what_measured: achieved social status within groups and perceived competence by observers
experiment: Across six experiments, units varied in their confidence about their abilities or performances. In observational studies, confident units' subsequent social status in group interactions was measured. In experimental studies, overconfident units were assigned to groups and their status outcomes were tracked. Behavioral analysis identified the mechanisms (signals of competence) linking overconfidence to status. Mediational analyses tested whether desire for status motivated overconfidence.

what_manipulated: delay interval between encoding and memory test (short vs. long retention periods)
what_measured: independent forgetting rates for different object properties (color, state, exemplar identity)
experiment: Units were shown real-world objects and later tested on memory for multiple features. Over three experiments, retention intervals were varied to track how different features were forgotten. Statistical independence of feature memory was tested by analyzing whether recall accuracy for one feature predicted accuracy for another. If features are bound in memory, dependence should persist; if separate, independence should emerge over time.

what_manipulated: received behavioral treatment (greedy, equal, or generous) in a resource-division game followed by opportunity to divide with a new unit
what_measured: generalized reciprocity behavior (reciprocation of received behavior toward a new unit)
experiment: In five experiments, units received initial allocations varying in fairness, then divided resources with a new unit. The tendency to reciprocate received behavior was measured across outcomes. Analyses tested whether receiving greed or generosity was paid forward in kind. Mediation analyses examined the role of affect in driving asymmetries. One experiment manipulated positive affect to test causal predictions.

what_manipulated: mode of information collection (active selection vs. passive reception of data)
what_measured: accuracy of category learning and hypothesis testing performance
experiment: Units engaged in a category-learning task under either selection or reception conditions. In selection mode, units chose which observations to examine; in reception mode, data were presented. Yoked pairs ensured units in reception saw identical information sequences to their paired selection learner. Performance was compared across conditions to identify differences arising from the mode of information control rather than information content.

what_manipulated: processing mode (unconscious incubation vs. conscious deliberation vs. immediate decision)
what_measured: accuracy of lie detection (correct identification of truthful vs. deceptive claims)
experiment: Units watched videos or heard statements containing deception, then made truth judgments under different processing conditions: unconscious thinking (engaging in a distractor task before deciding), conscious deliberation, or immediate judgment. Detection accuracy was measured as the proportion of correct classifications. Mediation analyses tested whether unconscious processing improved integration of cue information.

what_manipulated: exposure to reminders of structure (in nature or social systems) vs. control reminders
what_measured: willingness to engage in goal-directed action (motivation to pursue objectives)
experiment: Across five experiments, units received primes intended to activate awareness of structure or received control primes. Subsequently, willingness to pursue goals or engage in motivated action was measured via questionnaire or behavioral choice. Comparisons tested whether structure reminders increased goal pursuit intent relative to control conditions.

what_manipulated: articulation direction during word pronunciation (inward-moving consonants vs. outward-moving consonants vs. control)
what_measured: preference ratings for novel words or names and choice behavior in social interaction
experiment: Across nine experiments (N=822), units pronounceced or silently read words containing consonants that required inward or outward mouth movements. Preference was measured via explicit rating or by observing which social partners units chose to interact with. Experiments manipulated language (German vs. English), target valence, and reading modality. Aphasia patients lacking covert articulation were tested to verify causal mechanism.

what_manipulated: employment status transition (continuous employment vs. unemployment duration vs. reemployment) over 4 years
what_measured: personality trait change (five-factor model: agreeableness, conscientiousness, openness, and others)
experiment: A sample of 6,769 units completed personality assessments at two timepoints 4 years apart. Employment histories were tracked and units categorized into groups (remained employed, temporarily unemployed, reemployed). Latent change models estimated mean-level personality change for each group. Comparisons tested whether unemployment predicted personality change relative to remaining employed.

what_manipulated: number and concordance of observed agents' actions (one agent, two agents with identical actions, two agents with opposing actions)
what_measured: automatic imitation effect magnitude (ease and speed of producing matching vs. non-matching responses)
experiment: Units performed a standard automatic imitation task while observing one or two agents performing hand movements. Movement congruency was manipulated (agents performing identical or different actions). Response times and error rates quantified imitation effects. Comparisons tested whether observing multiple agents produced additive, canceling, or attention-based effects. Control conditions ruled out attentional capture explanations.

what_manipulated: goal type (approach vs. avoidance goals) during multistage goal-prioritization decisions
what_measured: deviation from optimal prioritization according to dynamic programming model
experiment: Units made a series of prioritization decisions while pursuing either two approach goals or two avoidance goals. Decisions were modeled against the dynamic programming solution for expected value maximization. Deviations from optimality were analyzed to test whether risk aversion (approach) and risk-seeking (avoidance) predicted which goal was prioritized. Comparison tested actual behavior against normative predictions.

what_manipulated: presence of heuristic disease cues and chronic or acute disease threat (germ aversion disposition or prime vs. control)
what_measured: categorical assignment of targets to in-group vs. out-group
experiment: Using a minimal group paradigm, units categorized neutral and disease-cued targets as in-group or out-group members. Two experiments tested whether chronic germ aversion and experimentally primed disease concern increased out-group categorization of disease-cued targets. Racial category of targets was manipulated to test interaction with pathogen threat. Comparisons tested effect sizes across conditions.

what_manipulated: request sequence (direct request for smaller favor vs. initial large request followed by smaller request)
what_measured: compliance with the smaller request (yes/no)
experiment: Three experiments tested whether an initial extreme request (refused) followed by a moderate request increased compliance relative to requesting only the moderate favor. Control conditions varied the framing and content of requests. Compliance rates were compared across conditions to test the reciprocal-concessions hypothesis. Mechanism was inferred from compliance patterns.

what_manipulated: moral properties of actions (morally negative vs. non-moral action structure held constant)
what_measured: folk judgment of intentionality (whether an action was intentional vs. accidental)
experiment: Units read scenarios describing actions identical in structure and control (e.g., gun use with one-in-six success chance) but differing in moral valence (shooting a person vs. a target). Judgments of intentionality were collected and compared to assess whether moral badness or agent blameworthiness influenced intent attributions. This was presented as a theory discussion paper drawing on empirical findings.

what_manipulated: comparative frame (more-than vs. less-than statements) and expectation of cognitive fluency (disfluency manipulation in one study)
what_measured: preference for statements, agreement with opinions, judgments of truth
experiment: Across seven studies, units were exposed to logically equivalent comparative statements framed as "more than" or "less than." Preference, agreement, and truth judgments were measured. One study manipulated expectations about processing fluency (told to expect difficulty) to test cognitive fluency mechanisms. Comparisons tested the robustness and moderators of the more-less asymmetry.

what_manipulated: organizational profit motive (for-profit vs. non-profit label) and temporal framing (long-run vs. short-run thinking prompt)
what_measured: beliefs about firm harm/benefit to society and anti-profit ideology endorsement
experiment: Seven experiments measured anti-profit views of real firms and of hypothetical organizations. Labels (for-profit vs. non-profit) and harm information were varied to assess effects. Subsequent experiments prompted long-run or short-run thinking before re-measuring anti-profit beliefs to test whether temporal perspective moderated the bias. Comparisons tested mean differences in anti-profit endorsement across conditions.

what_manipulated: deception strategy (truthful statements used to mislead vs. lies by commission vs. honest disclosure) in negotiation
what_measured: perceived ethicality, likelihood of impasse, outcomes (value claimed), and reputational harm
experiment: Units engaged in negotiation tasks where a counterpart used paltering (truthful misleading), lying, or honest strategies. Outcomes were measured in terms of negotiation success, impasse, and target perception of ethical violation. Experiments manipulated whether paltering occurred in response to direct questions vs. unprompted. Comparison assessed relative frequency of strategy use and consequences across strategies.

what_manipulated: awe-inducing stimuli (vast, perspective-challenging content) vs. positive control vs. neutral control
what_measured: humility (self-diminishment, balanced self-perception, acknowledgment of external contributions)
experiment: Five experiments induced awe through stimuli (videos, nature exposure, expansive visual perspectives) or measured naturally occurring awe-proneness. Humility was assessed via peer ratings, self-report, and behavioral measures (balanced self-presentation, attribution of success to outside forces). Mediational analyses tested whether awe-related appraisals (vastness, worldview challenge) predicted self-diminishment, which predicted humility. Comparison tested awe against positive and neutral control conditions.

what_manipulated: temporal framing of political message (past-focused vs. future-focused appeal to nostalgia)
what_measured: agreement with or support for political ideas; disagreement reduction between groups
experiment: Nine separate studies test whether communicating political ideas with past-focused framing increases support among conservative units. Studies 1-6 manipulate message framing (past vs. future) and measure support across diverse political topics. Studies measure agreement ratings, support indices. Study 7 manipulates cognitive processing mode to test when effect occurs. Study 8 tests directional asymmetry. Study 9 synthesizes results via meta-analysis. Comparisons are between-subjects; analysis tests main effects, mediating mechanisms (nostalgia), and interactions with processing depth.

what_manipulated: temporal focus (bringing to mind past self vs. future self)
what_measured: perceived rationality-vs.-emotionality of the self; choice of enrichment activity; self-control task performance
experiment: Six studies examine how time orientation affects trait attribution and behavior. Studies 1a-1c measure units' trait ratings of their past vs. future selves on rationality and emotionality dimensions. Study 2 tests whether asymmetry appears when rating others or is unique to self-judgment. Studies 3-4 present behavioral outcomes following the temporal manipulation: enrichment preference (study 3) and task performance (study 4) with context variation (performance labeled as measuring control vs. enjoyment). Analysis uses ratings and behavioral measures to test the asymmetry hypothesis and consequences.

what_manipulated: none (methodological review; no direct manipulation)
what_measured: meta-analytic indicators of selection bias and reporting irregularities in prior literature
experiment: A critical commentary and methodological reassessment of the money-priming literature. Authors apply meta-analytic tools (publication bias, p-curve, effect-size heterogeneity) to assess whether effect irregularities indicate selection bias or p-hacking. Analysis examines both published replications showing effects and preregistered replications showing null results to evaluate which pattern is more consistent with true effects versus biased reporting.

what_manipulated: environmental setup and decision-making context in agent-based learning tasks; simulations vary key agent and environment attributes
what_measured: formation of stable false beliefs despite continued experience; learning accuracy
experiment: A conceptual review identifies mechanisms of learning traps, followed by computational simulations demonstrating how selective attention interaction with environment properties produces false-belief stability. Then, a series of experiments presents units with dynamic environments and observes belief formation over time. Units make decisions and receive feedback; analysis tests whether stable false beliefs emerge despite disconfirming experience. Experiments test robustness against various hypothetical interventions. Statistical tests examine frequency and persistence of belief formation.

what_manipulated: experience of disrespect (recalled or behavioral context); endorsement of cynical beliefs; behavioral expression of disrespect toward others
what_measured: cynicism (belief in moral bankruptcy of people); experience of disrespect; treatment of others with disrespect
experiment: Study 1 is a cross-national survey showing association between reported disrespect and cynicism. Study 2 uses longitudinal data across 4 years to test bidirectional prediction. Studies 3-5 are preregistered experiments: Study 3 primes memory of disrespect experiences and measures subsequent cynicism; Studies 4-5 manipulate cynicism endorsement and measure whether others treat units disrespectfully in response. Study 6 is a weeklong diary study tracking daily experience of disrespect and cynical beliefs. Analysis tests vicious-cycle hypothesis via cross-lagged predictions, experimental manipulation, and mediation.

what_manipulated: none (observational; measured political ideology)
what_measured: anterior cingulate cortex activity (conflict-detection neural response); political ideology
experiment: Units with varying political ideologies complete tasks designed to elicit conflict or error responses while event-related potentials are recorded. Analysis measures anterior cingulate activity (a conflict-related component) and tests correlation with self-reported political ideology. Comparison is observational: units naturally differ in ideology; analysis reveals whether neural sensitivity to task conflict covaries with ideology.

what_manipulated: visual cue availability (self-refilling bowl obscuring consumption volume vs. normal bowl with accurate visual cue)
what_measured: food intake volume; subjective intake estimation; satiety perception
experiment: A between-subjects experiment assigns units to eat soup from either normal bowls (accurate visual cue) or self-refilling bowls (biased visual cue obscuring true consumption). Units eat ad libitum. Outcome measures include actual consumption volume (weighed), subjective estimates of intake, and satiety rating. Analysis compares intake and subjective ratings between visibility conditions via ANOVAs; also tests whether effects vary by BMI.

what_manipulated: none (observational; age group)
what_measured: socio-cognitive abilities (theory of mind, metacognition); socio-affective abilities (empathy, compassion); factual reasoning
experiment: Units of varying ages complete a naturalistic task designed to measure multiple facets of social understanding simultaneously. Analysis separates socio-cognitive components (theory of mind, metacognition about social content) from socio-affective components (empathy, compassion ratings) and compares to non-social metacognition and reasoning. Comparisons are observational across age groups. Statistical tests assess age-related differences for each component.

what_manipulated: eccentricity (distance from fixation point) and facial expression valence (happy, fearful, neutral)
what_measured: valence-recognition accuracy; eye gaze position
experiment: Units perform a facial valence judgment task while gaze is tracked. Faces appear at varying eccentricities in the parafoveal region (up to 4 degrees). Each face displays positive, negative, or neutral emotion. Units judge emotional valence while gaze location is recorded. Analysis measures recognition accuracy across eccentricities and valences. Between-subjects analysis tests whether performance decreases differently for different emotion types as distance from fixation increases. Tests include examination of whether effects are due to speed-accuracy tradeoffs or response biases.

what_manipulated: exposure to online game teaching misinformation production techniques (gameplay vs. no gameplay or delayed gameplay)
what_measured: ability to spot and resist misinformation; misinformation detection accuracy
experiment: A large-scale pre-post design assigns units to play an online browser game where they learn six misinformation-production techniques (polarization, emotion-invoking, conspiracy, trolling, blame-deflecting, impersonation). The control condition receives no gameplay or delayed gameplay. Outcome measures include accuracy on misinformation detection tasks pre and post gameplay. Analysis tests whether gameplay improves detection ability and whether effects vary by demographic factors (education, age, political ideology, cognitive style).

what_manipulated: syntactic category structure in three-word sequences (repeated vs. non-repeated within syntax; syntactically licit vs. illicit patterns)
what_measured: pattern detection ability; learning of repetition structure in sequences
experiment: Units listen to sequences of three words and learn whether the sequences follow simple repetition patterns (e.g., word 1 and word 2 both from the same syntactic category). Sequences are constructed so the target pattern is either consistent or inconsistent with possible syntactic structures. Units are instructed to look for patterns. Analysis measures learning success (whether units detect the pattern) via performance metrics or explicit reports. Tests compare learning success when patterns align with syntactic structures versus when they conflict with syntax.

what_manipulated: linguistic framing of survey question (personal identity phrasing e.g., "being a voter" vs. behavioral phrasing e.g., "voting")
what_measured: voter registration interest; actual voting behavior; voter turnout
experiment: Three randomized experiments vary survey phrasing. Experiment 1 presents questions about voting with identity-focused vs. behavior-focused language and measures interest in registering to vote. Experiments 2-3 manipulate phrasing in actual election contexts and measure voter turnout using official state voting records. Analysis compares registration interest and turnout rates between phrasing conditions.

what_manipulated: social class (measured via personal report; also manipulated via experimental framing in later studies)
what_measured: unethical behavior indicators (traffic violations, unethical choices, theft, lying, cheating, endorsement of unethical conduct)
experiment: Studies 1-2 are observational, comparing traffic violation frequency across social classes. Studies 3-7 are between-subjects experiments: units are assigned to high or low social class conditions or respond as themselves; outcomes measure unethical behaviors (taking valued goods, lying in negotiation, cheating for prizes, endorsing workplace unethical conduct). Mediator analysis tests role of attitudes toward greed. Comparisons test main effects of social class on unethical behavior.

what_manipulated: presence of noise in auditory sentence input (sentences with/without word errors); semantic prior expectation compatibility
what_measured: sentence interpretation (word choice for ambiguous meaning); comprehension accuracy
experiment: Units listen to and interpret three-word sentences. Manipulation varies the semantic plausibility of alternative interpretations and the presence/type of input errors (insertions vs. deletions from intended utterance). Outcomes measure which interpretation units select or how units resolve ambiguity. Analysis tests whether interpretations follow Bayesian predictions: units should favor interpretations close (in edit distance) to the input if semantically plausible, and asymmetry between insertion and deletion error types is predicted by size principle.

what_manipulated: intentionality of harm (intentional vs. unintentional action causing damage); task choice availability (blaming/condemning/punishing vs. other appealing alternatives)
what_measured: motivation to blame (choice persistence, effort, rate, duration); blame and condemnation intensity; harm magnitude estimation
experiment: A preliminary experiment compares harm-magnitude overestimation for intentional vs. unintentional harms. Experiments 1-2 measure motivation to blame using task-choice paradigms: units are offered choices between blaming-related tasks and other appealing activities under intentional-harm vs. unintentional-harm scenarios. Effort, persistence, rate, and duration of blaming behavior are recorded. Analysis tests whether units preferentially choose and persist with blaming when harms are perceived as intentional versus unintentional.

what_manipulated: option to intervene as a third party in a conflict (interval possible vs. not possible); risk to third party from intervention (high vs. low)
what_measured: cooperation rate among disputants; third-party intervention rate; outcome payoffs
experiment: Six experiments use the Peacemaker Game paradigm: two units act as disputants choosing cooperation vs. competition; a third unit chooses whether to intervene (if available). Intervention changes game payoffs and introduces risk to the intervener. Between-subjects variations manipulate presence of third-party option and risk level. Outcomes measure cooperation frequency among disputants, intervention frequency, and resulting payoffs. Analysis tests whether mere availability of third-party intervention affects cooperation and whether reducing intervention risk increases intervention rates and mutual welfare.

what_manipulated: postural expansiveness vs. contraction (in field interactions and static photos)
what_measured: romantic attraction (speed-dating yes/no response); mate selection in online-dating context
experiment: Field study 1 (speed dating): units interact briefly while nonverbal behavior is coded; postural expansiveness is extracted; outcome is whether dating partner reciprocates interest (yes/no). Field experiment 2 (online dating): photographs vary in whether the subject displays expanded vs. contracted posture; outcome is selection frequency by mate-seekers. Mediation analysis tests whether dominance perception explains the effect of expansiveness on selection.

what_manipulated: economic inequality level (high vs. low, measured via state-level data in study 1; manipulated experimentally in study 2 via portrayal of inequality in participants' home states)
what_measured: generosity (real-stakes giving amount); income level
experiment: Study 1 is observational: units of varying income in states of high vs. low inequality are offered a real giving opportunity; generosity (actual money allocated) is measured and correlated with income and inequality level. Study 2 is a between-subjects experiment: inequality in units' home states is described as high or low (experimental manipulation); units then face a giving opportunity. Outcomes measure actual giving by income level under high vs. low inequality conditions.

what_manipulated: patient race (black vs. white) in medical scenarios; measured endorsement of false biological-difference beliefs
what_measured: pain rating; treatment recommendation accuracy; endorsement of false biological beliefs
experiment: Study 1 measures laypersons' endorsement of false beliefs about racial biological differences and correlates this with pain ratings assigned to black vs. white targets in vignettes. Study 2 extends to medical trainees: endorsement of false beliefs is measured; medical students/residents rate pain for black vs. white patients and make treatment recommendations. Analysis tests association between belief endorsement and pain-rating bias, and whether this mediates treatment-recommendation errors.

what_manipulated: volitional concealment of personal information
what_measured: judgments of trustworthiness and social valuation of the actor
experiment: Multiple experiments examined whether units choosing to withhold personal information are judged differently by observers than units choosing to reveal the same information. Some studies tested whether observers' judgments vary when the concealment decision is voluntary versus forced. Other studies tested whether concealment actually reduces trustworthiness ratings or only perception thereof. The research tested both unflattering and flattering information domains and compared observer judgments against predictions made by the units themselves.

what_manipulated: exposure to counterstereotypical exemplars; desire for certainty induced via task framing; group membership stereotypes (real or fabricated)
what_measured: evaluation and resource allocation toward counterstereotypical individuals
experiment: Units sorted individuals into groups based on physical appearance and stereotypes. Some units were exposed to counterstereotypical group members and evaluated them, allocating resources. A separate manipulation induced desire for certainty in some units before evaluation. The study tested whether evaluations differed by political ideology and whether increased certainty-seeking in liberals replicated the conservative pattern of negative evaluation.

what_manipulated: information about a target's ecology (desperate vs. hopeful); race information (Black vs. White); combinations of race and ecology cues
what_measured: inferences about the target's behavioral strategy (fast vs. slow life-history traits)
experiment: Units made inferences about individuals based on race cues alone, ecology cues alone, or combinations. Studies initially demonstrated that units hold ecology-based stereotypes. Subsequent studies independently varied race and ecology information to test whether race or ecology dominated inference. Comparisons tested whether the inference pattern tracked race stereotypes or reflected the actual ecology information provided.

what_manipulated: engagement in unethical behavior; delay between behavior and memory assessment
what_measured: clarity and vividness of memory recall for the unethical action versus baseline memory
experiment: Across nine studies with over 2,000 units, participants engaged in unethical actions or control actions and then reported memory of those actions at various time delays. Memory clarity and vividness were measured for unethical versus ethical or neutral actions. The analysis tested whether memory degradation over time was specific to unethical actions and whether this effect predicted repeated unethical behavior.

what_manipulated: teacher mindset training toward empathic discipline (vs. control); exposure to the mindset intervention (online, brief)
what_measured: student suspension rates; student respect for teachers; student classroom motivation
experiment: An initial experiment had teachers adopt either empathic or punitive mindsets and measured the effect on student respect and motivation. A second laboratory study tested whether students maintained motivation under empathic versus punitive discipline. A randomized field experiment exposed teachers in multiple schools to a brief online intervention promoting empathic discipline and measured year-long suspension rates, student respect, and motivation across 31 teachers and 1,682 students.

what_manipulated: whether the cost-benefit calculation is observable to others; time spent deliberating (observable vs. obscured)
what_measured: likelihood of cooperative choice; observer perception of trustworthiness; actual trustworthiness as measured by subsequent behavior
experiment: Two economic game experiments manipulated whether units' decision process was observable. In one, units chose whether to reveal the precise costs of cooperation; in another, time spent deciding was measured. Units' cooperative behavior was compared across observable and non-observable conditions. Separately, observers rated the trustworthiness of units in each condition and participated in subsequent games to measure actual trustworthiness.

what_manipulated: fairness of the allocation procedure (fair vs. unfair); timing of fairness assessment (pre-allocation vs. post-allocation)
what_measured: allocator predictions of receiver happiness; actual receiver happiness; receiver preference for fair vs. unfair procedures
experiment: Allocators assigned resources to receivers using fair or unfair procedures and predicted the happiness impact on receivers. Receivers then reported their actual happiness. Studies tested whether allocators overestimated fairness impact by comparing allocator predictions to receiver reports. Timing manipulations revealed when fairness salience peaks. Final studies directly compared receiver happiness following fair-and-small versus unfair-and-large allocations.

what_manipulated: subjective socioeconomic status relative to others (induced via visual hierarchy ladder or comparative framing)
what_measured: appetite for high-calorie foods; automatic food preference; actual food intake in snack and meal contexts
experiment: Across four studies, units were induced to feel lower, higher, or neutral socioeconomic status relative to peers. Appetite, food preference, and consumption were measured via implicit association, choice tasks, and actual eating opportunities. The studies tested whether status-induced appetite occurred independent of actual financial constraints and whether it generalized across food contexts.

what_manipulated: temporal focus of environmental comparison (past-focused vs. future-focused vs. control); presentation to conservative vs. liberal units
what_measured: climate change attitudes; behavioral intentions regarding climate action; policy support
experiment: Six studies exposed units to information about environmental conditions presented with past or future temporal framings. Responses about climate beliefs, behavioral intentions, and policy preferences were measured. Analyses compared conservative versus liberal units across temporal conditions to test whether past-focus reduced the ideological gap in climate action support.

what_manipulated: presence and salience of thank-you gift incentive in donation request (field and online conditions)
what_measured: donation rate; donation amount; attention to gift versus intrinsic motives
experiment: A field experiment mailed donation requests with and without thank-you gifts to prospective donors. Donation rates and amounts were compared. An online study replicated the effect while manipulating the visual salience of the gift. Analysis tested whether gift effects were driven by attention shift away from intrinsic motives and whether effects were largest for donors with prior high intrinsic motivation.

what_manipulated: facial characteristics of the scientist (competence, morality, attractiveness); presentation format (text, image, video)
what_measured: perceived scientist competence and morality; perceived quality of scientific work; choice to view science communication; judgments of research quality after viewing
experiment: Studies first measured correlations between facial traits and judgments of competence, morality, attractiveness, and interest in the scientist's work. Subsequent studies had units choose which science news items to view or read, paired with scientists varying in appearance. Final studies had units read science news paired with scientists differing in facial characteristics and rate the research quality.

what_manipulated: belief in free will (measured or experimentally weakened via intervention); other units' behavior context (internal vs. external constraint)
what_measured: attribution bias (overestimation of internal factors in others' behavior); punishment and reward recommendations
experiment: Six studies measured the correlation between belief in free will and the correspondence bias in attributions. Two studies experimentally weakened free will belief and measured subsequent attribution changes. A final study tested whether the free will-attribution relationship mediated prescribed punishment and reward behavior.

what_manipulated: role in two-player interaction (thief vs. victim); learning feedback about opponent behavior across rounds
what_measured: strategy choices (rigid vs. flexible punishment/theft); fitness outcomes in the game
experiment: The study combined evolutionary game theory analysis, reinforcement learning simulations, and behavioral experiments with human units playing a theft-punishment game across multiple rounds. Behavior was analyzed to identify whether units relied on flexible adaptive strategies or rigid contingent strategies. Game payoffs measured fitness relative to strategy type and opponent diversity.

what_manipulated: dehumanizing versus humanizing language describing a victim; type of harm (instrumental profit vs. moral justice)
what_measured: support for practices causing harm; willingness to harm victims in vignettes for instrumental gain or moral reasons; spontaneous dehumanization of targets
experiment: Survey studies measured attitudes toward harmful practices (sweatshops, animal testing, drone strikes causing civilians/terrorist deaths) and correlated attributions of reduced humanity with support for harm. Vignette experiments had units decide whether to harm a stranger for money or for their immoral behavior, while manipulating dehumanizing language. Measurement tracked spontaneous dehumanization when imagining harm.

what_manipulated: exposure to information about rising economic inequality (vs. control information); framing of inequality trends
what_measured: beliefs about opportunity (structural vs. individual factors); support for policy addressing inequality
experiment: Units (n=1,501) were shown information about economic inequality trends or control information. They then reported beliefs about the relative importance of structural and individual factors in economic mobility. Separately, they indicated support for various policy approaches to addressing inequality. Analysis tested whether inequality information affected opportunity beliefs and whether this predicted policy support.

what_manipulated: permanence of shared content (temporary vs. permanent storage); task instruction and salience of permanence
what_measured: willingness to take and share self-portraits; disinhibition in photograph content; observer impressions of sharer judgment and character
experiment: Field and online experiments had units take and share self-portraits under temporary or permanent sharing conditions. Compliance with the sharing request and disinhibition in photo content were measured. Separately, observers rated the judgment and character of sharers based on the photos, either knowing the original sharing condition or not. Follow-up studies examined persistence of impressions and effects among friends.

what_manipulated: information search strategy (comparative vs. integrative features); time horizon comparison (sooner vs. later reward framing); unobtrusive search manipulation
what_measured: discounting of delayed rewards; patience in intertemporal choice; susceptibility to acceleration versus delay framing
experiment: Initial observation identified heterogeneity in search strategies during choice between sooner and later rewards. A manipulation randomly assigned units to conditions promoting comparative (feature-by-feature) or integrative (option-by-option) search. Comparisons measured how choice and discounting rates differed by strategy and whether search effects persisted when participants were unaware of the manipulation.

what_manipulated: exposure to information about past racial economic equality; salience of societal discrimination; anchoring on personal circumstances; demographic groups (White, Black; high-income, low-income)
what_measured: perceived Black-White economic equality (past and current); accuracy of estimates versus national statistics
experiment: A survey (n=1,377) measured estimates of Black-White economic equality across income and race groups, comparing estimates to actual statistics. Follow-up experiments tested whether making discrimination salient improved estimate accuracy and whether personal anchoring increased overestimation. Analysis examined whether optimism bias and belief in a just world predicted overestimates.

what_manipulated: training in cognitive regulation strategies for food craving (versus control); emphasis on negative consequences of unhealthy foods versus positive benefits of healthy foods; brief training exposure (ROC-T)
what_measured: food craving for healthy versus unhealthy items; food valuation (willingness to pay); food choice distribution; actual food consumption
experiment: Studies first showed that cognitive strategies modified craving for both unhealthy and healthy foods and changed valuation. Subsequent studies tested a brief training program (Regulation of Craving Training) on food choices in laboratory and applied settings. Measurements included choice shifts to healthier options, generalization to nontrained items, and actual caloric intake reduction. A final study confirmed training was necessary beyond framing effects alone.

what_manipulated: exposure to opposing beliefs in a bipartisan social network (vs. single-party or control); partisan priming (party logos or peer identities); climate communication from NASA
what_measured: interpretation accuracy of climate data; belief polarization (conservatives vs. liberals); social learning of target interpretation
experiment: An online experiment exposed units to structured bipartisan social networks discussing climate data, with or without partisan priming. Units' interpretations of climate trends and polarization were measured before and after network exposure. Analysis compared conditions with and without partisan salience and tested whether social learning mechanisms explained improvements in accuracy or maintenance of polarization.

what_manipulated: the gender of a professional described; whether referring to them by surname vs full name
what_measured: the likelihood of using surname reference; perceived eminence and deservingness of status benefits
experiment: Units were exposed to descriptions of scientists or professionals, manipulated by gender (male vs female). In archival analyses, researchers counted surname usage in student reviews and radio discussions. In experimental conditions, units wrote about male or female scientists matched on all other attributes. In a separate rating task, units evaluated professionals referred to by surname vs full name for fame, eminence, and deservingness of awards. Analysis compared surname usage rates and perceived status judgments across gender conditions.

what_manipulated: the context of evaluation (different domains like choosing peers, food, services, candidates)
what_measured: the amount of information consumed before forming a judgment; whether beliefs about information use match actual behavior
experiment: Seven studies examined units evaluating targets (peers, marriage candidates, politicians, foods, goods, services) in impression-formation tasks. Units made judgments about targets and reported their expectations about how much evidence they would consider. Analysis compared expected information consumption to actual consumption, and measured overpayment for decision aids and overwork to impress others. The core design tested prediction errors: units consistently overestimated the amount of information they would gather before reaching conclusions.

what_manipulated: the emotional valence of video news content (positive vs negative); geographic/cultural context (17 countries, 6 continents)
what_measured: psychophysiological reactivity (presumed via physiological measures) to news stimuli; cross-national differences in negativity bias magnitude
experiment: Units from 17 countries viewed real video news content differing in emotional valence (negative vs positive). Psychophysiological reactions were measured during viewing. Analysis examined whether units showed stronger physiological responses to negative than positive content, and whether this effect varied by country. The study highlighted both average effects and substantial individual-level variation in responsiveness to news content.

what_manipulated: relational diversity of social interactions (richness and evenness of relationship types); variation across individuals and over time
what_measured: well-being; multiple operationalizations across samples (e.g., life satisfaction, health outcomes)
experiment: Four distinct samples (US preregistered survey, American Time Use Survey, WHO Global Aging and Adult Health Study, French mobile app users) reported their relationship types and well-being. Analysis computed specification curves examining whether relational diversity predicted well-being, controlling for total social interaction, activity diversity, and other covariates. Both between-subjects (across individuals) and within-subjects (over time) associations were tested. The relationship between portfolio diversity and well-being was examined as a unique predictor independent of interaction frequency.

what_manipulated: salience of religious concepts (God); variety of decision contexts (entertainment, food, investments, medical)
what_measured: willingness to accept AI-based recommendations; reliance on human recommenders; feelings of human fallibility and smallness
experiment: Eight preregistered experiments manipulated God salience (salient vs control) and measured units' willingness to consider AI recommendations vs human recommendations in various contexts. Studies 1-4 tested whether God salience increased AI acceptance across multiple domains. Studies 3-4 examined the mediator (feelings of smallness and recognition of human fallibility). Study 5 tested an alternative explanation (similarity in mysteriousness between God and AI). Study 6 analyzed real-world financial decision data from 21 countries using robo-advisors. Analysis compared recommendation choices and acceptance rates across salience conditions.

what_manipulated: syntactic construction type (gerund vs infinitival complements); order of construction exposure in repeated tasks
what_measured: acceptability ratings of sentences; construction choices in sentence completion; priming effects from prior constructions
experiment: Units (German learners of English) completed corpus-informed experiments. Distinctive collexeme analysis identified verbs that distinguish between gerund and infinitival constructions. Units then rated sentence acceptability and completed sentence fragments. Analysis measured rating-to-production priming (whether construction ratings influenced subsequent completions), production-to-production priming (whether producing one construction primed producing the same type next), and accumulative effects across many trials. A corpus analysis identified construct-distinctive verbs to use as experimental stimuli.

what_manipulated: speaker language background (L1 English vs L1 Tagalog); job prestige (high vs low); speaker performance level (high vs low)
what_measured: perceived professional competence; comprehensibility ratings; treatment preference; accent-related bias effects
experiment: Ninety-six units listened to audio recordings of 12 speakers (6 native English, 6 native Tagalog) in work contexts. Recordings were systematically varied on prestige and performance level. Units rated speakers on three continua: professional competence, treatment preference, and linguistic comprehensibility. Analysis examined main effects of language background and accent on all three dimensions, and tested interactions with job prestige and performance level. The study measured how foreign accent effects were magnified or reduced depending on job prestige context.

what_manipulated: proficiency level (A1-B2); annotation type (fine-grained interlanguage annotation vs standard approaches)
what_measured: morpheme accuracy order across proficiency levels; types of morpheme errors; morpheme emergence patterns
experiment: Analysis of a learner corpus of L1 Spanish learners of English across four proficiency levels (A1-B2). Units produced written or spoken English in naturalistic or classroom contexts. Interlanguage Annotation (ILA) was applied manually to identify morphemes and error subtypes. Analysis examined the morpheme accuracy order at each proficiency level and compared against prior Morpheme Order Studies. The study used a standardised proficiency test to categorize units, allowing examination of how morpheme patterns varied with proficiency.

what_manipulated: perceived financial security; degree of financial insecurity threat (in experimental conditions)
what_measured: perceived meaning in life; life satisfaction or existential well-being
experiment: Study 1 examined correlations between reported financial insecurity and meaning in life across units, controlling for income. Study 2-3 experimentally manipulated threats to financial security and measured impacts on meaning perceptions. Analysis tested whether financial security predicted meaning above and beyond income, and whether the pathway from income to meaning was mediated by financial insecurity. The relationship between financial security (subjective sense of affordability) and existential well-being was examined.

what_manipulated: emotional content of a focal object (positive, negative, or neutral) in an encoding phase; type of memory cue at retrieval (item vs background)
what_measured: recall accuracy for background details; recall of associated scene elements
experiment: Units encoded color objects on neutral backgrounds; object valence was manipulated (positive, negative, neutral). At retrieval, units received either the item or background as a cue and were asked to recall the associated scene element. Analysis compared cued recall of background information across emotional and neutral item conditions. Unlike recognition tasks, cued recall examined whether emotional items triggered recall of contextual details more than neutral items, testing whether background information was encoded despite attentional narrowing.

what_manipulated: facial redness (incrementally varied); facial expression (angry vs neutral); facial masculinity (high vs low)
what_measured: perceived anger in the face; perceived health of the target
experiment: Experiment 1 presented faces with manipulated redness across angry and neutral expressions. Units rated each for anger and health. Analysis examined whether redness effects differed by expression condition. Experiment 2 extended by also varying facial masculinity. Analysis tested the linear and curvilinear effects of redness on anger and health, and examined whether masculine face structure moderated these effects. Perceived anger and health were the two outcome measures across conditions.

what_manipulated: emotional content category of photographs (disgusting vs frightening); interrelatedness of stimuli within each category (matched vs unmatched)
what_measured: recall accuracy for photographs; attention allocation to disgusting vs frightening images
experiment: Study 1 presented units with sets of disgusting and frightening photographs matched for interrelatedness. Units then recalled which photographs they had seen. Analysis compared recall rates and measured whether disgust advantage persisted when images were matched on organization. Study 2 applied Latent Semantic Analysis to quantify photograph relatedness. Mediation analysis examined whether attention differences explained the recall advantage for disgusting over frightening photographs.

what_manipulated: stimulus presentation duration (500 vs 1250 msec); emotional content of faces (threatening, happy, neutral)
what_measured: reaction time in the dot probe task; evidence of attentional bias (faster detection of probes at threat locations)
experiment: Units with varying trait anxiety completed a forced-choice dot probe task. Emotional face stimuli (threat, happy, neutral) were presented at two durations. Units detected probes that replaced faces, and reaction times indicated attentional location. Analysis compared RT differences (bias toward threat locations vs other locations) across anxiety levels and durations. The study examined time course of attentional biases and whether anxious units avoided happy faces or only showed vigilance for threat.

what_manipulated: moral valence of a behavior (good vs bad, praiseworthy vs blameworthy); other contextual details of hypothetical actions
what_measured: judgments of whether an action is intentional; conceptual criteria for intentionality
experiment: Four experiments presented units with descriptions of actions varying in moral valence while holding other features constant. Units judged whether described behaviors were intentional. Analysis examined whether moral qualities influenced intentionality judgments compared to control conditions. Alternative explanations were tested by manipulating other action features. Results indicated that the moral evaluation of a behavior influenced whether units classified it as intentional.

what_manipulated: philosophical commitment (objectivist vs nonobjectivist views of morality); type of moral violation (canonical vs other)
what_measured: endorsement of nonobjectivist claims about morality; performance on the moral/conventional task
experiment: Five experiments presented units with moral violations and philosophical claims. In the first part, units endorsed or rejected nonobjectivist statements about moral issues. In a second task (in four experiments), units completed the standard moral/conventional task, distinguishing violations in terms of their domain. Analysis examined whether units who rejected moral objectivity still treated moral violations in the characteristic ways of typical moral judgment, and whether they distinguished moral from conventional violations.

what_manipulated: moral valence of an outcome; presence or absence of skill/control/luck in achieving it; type of action described
what_measured: judgments of whether an action was performed intentionally; role of skill vs moral features in intentionality
experiment: Units considered scenarios describing actions that succeeded or failed, with varying levels of skill, luck, and control, and varying moral outcomes. Units judged whether actions were performed intentionally. Analysis examined whether skill and control considerations were necessary conditions for intentionality judgments, or whether moral features trumped them. Results compared judgments across conditions where luck, skill, and moral valence were systematically varied.

what_manipulated: whether a scenario is deterministic; variation in agent action and outcome details
what_measured: folk judgments of free will; folk judgments of moral responsibility
experiment: Two studies surveyed units' judgments about agents in deterministic scenarios. Units read vignettes and answered whether the agent acted of their own free will and whether they were morally responsible. Analysis examined the distribution of free-will and responsibility judgments, comparing endorsement rates across scenarios. The study mapped folk intuitions onto philosophical positions in the free-will debate, testing whether most units align with libertarian or compatibilist views.

what_manipulated: moral valence of an object of value; other contextual features of scenarios
what_measured: folk attributions of desiring; folk attributions of moral beliefs; third-person mindreading accuracy
experiment: Units read scenarios and attributed desires and moral beliefs to characters described. Analysis examined whether folk attributions varied by moral valence of the target of desire.
