The study tested whether moral goodness is a constitutive feature of the concept VALUING, or whether egocentric biases in third-person mindreading explain the pattern. Results suggested that intrinsic biases in mindreading mechanisms, rather than conceptual features, better explain folk attribution patterns.

what_manipulated: moral valence of a behavior; atypicality or unusualness of an agent's action
what_measured: folk causal judgments; assignment of causal responsibility
experiment: Units evaluated scenarios in which an agent performed an atypical action with varying moral valence. Units indicated whether they attributed causation to the agent for an outcome. Analysis examined the independent contributions of moral valence and atypicality to causal judgments. Results compared prior findings that causation is primarily influenced by moral valence against the alternative that atypicality is a key factor, finding that both factors contribute.

what_manipulated: reality status of an experience (real vs virtual); status quo of the experience (familiar/ongoing vs novel)
what_measured: willingness to plug into a virtual reality machine; preference for reality vs virtual experience
experiment: Units considered variations on Nozick's experience machine scenario in which aspects of the thought-experiment were modified. Analysis examined whether units' reluctance to plug in was due to preferring reality per se, or due to status quo bias (preference to continue current life as experienced). Units rated acceptability and desirability of discontinuing their current life. Alternative explanations were tested by examining the role of life continuity versus reality status.

what_manipulated: presentation format of a scenario (agent with or without moral motivation)
what_measured: willingness to attribute moral judgment to an agent
experiment: Units read scenarios describing an agent with moral understanding but lacking moral motivation (or control scenarios). The study varied whether the outcome variable was attributed moral understanding versus moral belief. Multiple survey designs tested these conditions across different participant groups. Comparisons examined whether subjects attributed moral judgments more readily under some framings than others, using descriptive statistics and proportional analysis across conditions.

what_manipulated: none (observational measure of therapist behavior)
what_measured: client perception of therapist alliance activity; relationship to alliance quality and session outcomes
experiment: Units (170 clients across 42 therapists) completed a new measure assessing their perceptions of therapist behaviors related to alliance. Factor analysis identified four subscales reflecting monitoring relationship, goal-setting, progress tracking, and feedback avoidance. Multilevel models tested associations between subscales and client-rated alliance and session outcomes, accounting for therapist clustering.

what_manipulated: none (observational analysis of media agendas)
what_measured: association between issue agendas in newspapers, television, and political advertising
experiment: Content analysis coded issue agendas in newspaper coverage, television coverage, and political advertising from a campaign. Cross-lagged correlations tested directional influence between agendas at successive time points. Pearson correlations quantified strength of intermedia and advertising-media associations.

what_manipulated: type of stimulus (faces vs houses); probe type and delay
what_measured: recognition accuracy and matching accuracy for complete stimuli versus parts
experiment: Units performed 2-alternative-forced-choice recognition and matching tasks on stimuli (faces or houses) presented as wholes or parts. The study tested for a complete-over-part advantage by comparing accuracy across conditions. Multiple experiments manipulated probe type, delay, and procedural factors (precueing, feature naming) to assess whether holistic processing was mandatory or flexible.

what_manipulated: none (observational review of prior empirical work on moral-action-intentionality bias)
what_measured: folk attributions of intentionality as influenced by moral considerations
experiment: Narrative review synthesizing empirical literature on how moral judgments bias folk ascriptions of intentional action. The review examined whether immorality of acts affects perceived mens rea in legal contexts. Analysis used an affective model of blame attribution to explain biasing mechanisms and discussed implications for jury impartiality.

what_manipulated: morphological structure of word flankers (transparent suffix, pseudo-suffix, non-suffix, unrelated)
what_measured: lexical decision accuracy and latency to target word
experiment: Units made lexical decisions to a central target word flanked by related or unrelated words. Conditions varied whether flankers shared morphological structure (transparent suffix, pseudo-morphological, or no structure) or were orthographically or phonologically unrelated. Reaction time and accuracy compared facilitation across morphological versus non-morphological conditions to isolate the effect of transparent structure.

what_manipulated: range of response scale categories (low range: 0-2.5 hours vs high range: 0-4.5+ hours)
what_measured: self-reported daily behavior; subsequent judgment of average behavior and evaluation of that behavior
experiment: Units reported television use on scales with different category ranges, then made judgments about average usage and evaluated the behavior. Manipulation of scale range tested whether response categories served as anchors affecting both reported behavior and downstream judgments. Comparisons examined behavior reports, average-use estimates, and evaluations of importance/satisfaction across scale conditions.

what_manipulated: number and structure of preceding specific questions before a general question
what_measured: judgment to the general question (assimilation or contrast relative to specific-question priming)
experiment: Units answered a general question preceded by either one specific question, multiple specific questions, or no preceding question. The abstract-response predictions depended on whether questions were perceived as belonging to the same conversational context. Analysis tested whether preceding questions caused assimilation (information reuse) or contrast (nonredundancy reinterpretation) effects on the general judgment using comparison of means or effect sizes.

what_manipulated: presence and characteristics of commercial interruptions (ad quality, interruption type)
what_measured: enjoyment of television program
experiment: Units watched television programs with or without commercial interruptions (varying in quality and type). Six studies tested enjoyment ratings across conditions and explored boundary conditions (adaptation proneness, program type). Comparisons examined whether interruptions affected program enjoyment through a proposed adaptation-disruption mechanism, with moderation by individual differences and stimulus properties.

what_manipulated: none (observational analysis of wage gaps by firm size and job responsibility)
what_measured: wage gaps as a function of job responsibility level
experiment: Panel data from Sweden and occupational cross-section from US were analyzed using fixed-effects regression models. Outcomes (wage gaps by firm size) were regressed on predictor (job responsibility measured via direct measures or supervisory status). Analysis tested whether wage gaps increased with responsibility level and compared patterns across blue-collar and white-collar workers.

what_manipulated: variety in an assortment (identical vs varied colors/shapes) and context salience
what_measured: perceived quantity of items in the assortment
experiment: Units estimated quantity of dots or shapes displayed in patterns with varied or identical colors/geometric properties. Additional studies tested whether perceived quantity affected pouring behavior when using varied items to match a reference sample. Manipulations tested salience of overall area and number of item types. Comparisons used quantity estimates and measured poured amounts across conditions.

what_manipulated: source expertise (high vs low) and source certainty (high vs low)
what_measured: persuasion and consumer involvement with message
experiment: Units received persuasive messages from sources varying in expertise and expressed certainty. Three experiments manipulated these factors and measured persuasion (attitude change or choice) and involvement (attention or cognitive engagement). Predictions tested an incongruity hypothesis: low-expertise sources gain persuasiveness through certainty; high-expertise sources through uncertainty. Comparisons examined persuasion across source-certainty conditions.

what_manipulated: system threat (attack on legitimacy) and individual confidence in system legitimacy
what_measured: consumption choice (preference for direct vs indirect system defense)
experiment: Units' confidence in system legitimacy was measured. All units then received a threat (verbal attack) to the system. Consumption choices were presented allowing direct or indirect defense mechanisms. The study tested whether threat activates defense motivation in all units but that individuals differing in confidence adopt different defense strategies. Comparisons examined choice patterns and preferences across confidence levels after threat.

what_manipulated: product orientation in visual depiction (toward dominant hand vs away)
what_measured: purchase intention; secondary: attention resources in embodied simulation
experiment: Units viewed advertisements with products oriented to facilitate or limit embodied mental simulation (e.g., hand-dominant orientation). Four studies measured purchase intentions and tested predictions about motor response activation. Boundary conditions included perceptual resource occupation and product valence (positive vs negative). Comparisons examined purchase intentions across depiction conditions and tested mediation through mental simulation.

what_manipulated: horizontal position of product image in advertisement (congruent vs incongruent with left-right time mental model)
what_measured: attitude toward product
experiment: Units from left-to-right reading cultures viewed ads with products positioned left or right. Four studies measured attitude toward time-related or time-attributed products. Manipulations tested congruence with the left (past) right (future) mental timeline. Mediation by processing fluency and moderation by need for structure were tested. Comparisons examined attitudes across position conditions and tested reverse effect in right-to-left reading populations.

what_manipulated: role (seller vs buyer/owner) and feature quality (high vs low level construal features)
what_measured: price estimate (selling price vs buying price)
experiment: Four studies assigned units to seller or buyer roles and presented products with superior high-level or low-level features. Price estimates were collected under different mental-set conditions (why vs how focus). Analysis compared price estimates across role, feature quality, and mental-set conditions to test whether differential construal levels mediate seller-buyer price gaps. Mediation by construal level and moderation by feature type were examined.

what_manipulated: perceived resource constraint (high vs low scarcity of time or money) and planning method availability (efficiency vs priority planning)
what_measured: resource-coping strategy selection and consumer behavior outcomes (spending, impulsivity)
experiment: Units faced resource-scarcity scenarios requiring coping strategies. Studies manipulated constraint level and measured whether units chose efficiency planning (stretching resource) or priority planning (sacrificing goals). Multiple methods tested both financial and time constraints. Analysis examined how strategy choices varied with constraint severity, examined perceived savings and costs, and tested whether budgeting moderated relationships between constraint and dysfunctional behaviors.

what_manipulated: presence of own smartphone (visible vs removed/unavailable)
what_measured: available cognitive capacity; secondary: cognitive task performance
experiment: Two experiments exposed units to the presence or absence of their own smartphone during unrelated cognitive tasks. Cognitive capacity was measured through performance on working-memory or attention tasks. The study predicted and tested whether mere presence occupies cognitive resources, with moderation by smartphone dependence (measured individual difference). Comparisons examined task performance and available capacity across smartphone-presence conditions stratified by dependence.

what_manipulated: object category (food vs. non-food) presented in immersive environment
what_measured: speed of approach/withdrawal motor action execution
experiment: Units performed repeated grasping or warding actions toward different object categories in a virtual reality setting using their dominant hand. Two categories compared: high-calorie food objects versus non-food control objects. The speed of action execution measured and correlated with individual differences (body mass index, diet-related attitudes). Behavioral approach bias quantified as speed differential between categories.

what_manipulated: exposure to social-media cues versus control cues
what_measured: spontaneous hedonic affective reaction (implicit)
experiment: Two studies with total N=200 units classified by usage frequency (frequent vs. less-frequent). Units exposed to either social-media or control cues. Spontaneous affective reactions measured using an implicit indirect task (Affect Misattribution Procedure). Differential affective response compared between cue types and usage groups. Mediation analysis tested whether affective reactions account for the relationship between usage frequency and craving.

what_manipulated: prompted imaginative activity (genre variation)
what_measured: resistance to engaging in imaginative task
experiment: Two empirical studies examining units' engagement with prompted imaginative activities across different genres. Resistance measured as difficulty or reluctance in engaging with particular imaginative scenarios. Genre identified as a significant but low-salience factor explaining resistance patterns. Study establishes psychological reality of the phenomenon and investigates factors contributing to resistance.

what_manipulated: linguistic context of action descriptions (side effects, intentionality phrasing)
what_measured: language judgment: whether behavior classified as intentional
experiment: Units asked to judge whether behaviors described in ordinary language constitute intentional actions, with particular focus on cases involving side effects. Experimental manipulation of the linguistic framing and context of action descriptions. Classification patterns analyzed to understand how ordinary language users employ relevant terminology.

what_manipulated: country context (32 nations with varying social norms)
what_measured: participation in female-typed household task performance
experiment: Observational data from 32 countries from the International Social Survey Program (2002). Task participation patterns scaled using Mokken scaling to identify underlying task hierarchy. Multinomial multilevel models tested micro-level predictors of task-participation patterns and macro-level country differences. No experimental manipulation; observation of naturally occurring task-sharing variation.

what_manipulated: economic conditions (GDP growth, unemployment rates, crisis policies) and EU politicization
what_measured: dimensions of EU framing (cosmopolitan, utilitarian, libertarian, communitarian meanings)
experiment: Longitudinal pooled Eurobarometer survey data from 2004 to 2013 across EU countries. Four dimensions of EU meaning measured at multiple time points. Evolution of framing examined in relation to economic performance indicators and EU-level policy events. Cross-national variation in framing patterns linked to economic conditions and politicization. Multiple regression models estimated differential effects on education groups.

what_manipulated: parental socioeconomic status (high vs. low)
what_measured: timing and type of first union formation (direct marriage vs. cohabitation)
experiment: Observational data from 25 European countries (European Social Survey Round 3, 2006/2007). Units' entry into first co-residential union timing and form predicted from parental SES. Two-step meta-analytical models estimated country-specific effects and cross-national variation. Mediation analysis tested whether individual education accounts for SES effect. Cross-national moderation by demographic transition stage examined.

what_manipulated: recipient age group (younger vs. older units), donation form (money vs. time), and social relationship (relatives vs. nonrelatives)
what_measured: amount donated across recipient categories
experiment: Units (N=89 younger, N=66 older adults) completed a hypothetical donation task allocating money and time to relatives and nonrelatives at varying social distances. Between-subjects comparisons across age groups. Donation amounts and patterns (kin selection bias, social discounting) analyzed to identify age-related differences in prosocial behavior across contextual factors.

what_manipulated: exposure to proactive self-management intervention course
what_measured: proactive coping skills, goal achievement, self-efficacy in self-management
experiment: Units recently diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes (N=180) randomized to intervention (n=78) or control (n=102) group. Intervention incorporated five-step proactive coping plan derived from theory. Outcomes measured post-course including course evaluations, proactive coping, goal attainment, and self-efficacy. Between-group comparisons tested intervention efficacy on proximal outcomes.

what_manipulated: product display format (single unit in isolation vs. multiple identical product replicates grouped)
what_measured: perceived product efficacy and homogeneity
experiment: Five experiments varying product categories and display contexts (ads, special displays, online retailing). Units shown either a single product or multiple identical product replicates and rated efficacy, homogeneity, and unified purpose perceptions. Between-subjects comparisons across display formats. Mediating mechanism tested through homogeneity and entitativity perceptions.

what_manipulated: salience of goal conflict (presence vs. absence of conflicting goals)
what_measured: time allocation to work versus leisure activities
experiment: Seven experiments manipulating perception of goal conflict between unrelated domains. Units subsequently made time allocation decisions for work and leisure activities independent of the conflict domain. Time spent on work and leisure tracked. Mediating mechanism tested through ease of justification for each activity type. Analysis examined whether goal conflict influences allocation even when activities unrelated to conflict.

what_manipulated: salience of financial constraint (limited vs. unconstrained discretionary money)
what_measured: preference for material goods versus experiential purchases
experiment: Seven studies with experimental manipulation of financial constraint salience and measurement of preference shifts. Units chose between material and experiential purchases under varying constraint conditions. Longevity of purchase examined as proposed mediator. Moderators tested: frivolity of goods, longevity manipulation, explicit memory salience. Analyses examined preference stability across conditions.

what_manipulated: economic conditions (poor vs. good economic environment)
what_measured: resource allocation to daughters versus sons
experiment: Series of experiments manipulating economic condition salience and presenting hypothetical resource allocation scenarios (bequests, Treasury bonds, extracurricular activities). Between-subjects comparisons of allocation preferences for daughters versus sons across economic contexts. Perception of child reproductive value measured as proposed mediator. Individual differences (risk aversion, monogamy preferences) examined as moderators. Age of child varied to test reproductive-value account.

what_manipulated: logo shape (circular vs. angular) and cognitive load (visuospatial working memory constraint)
what_measured: product/company attribute judgments
experiment: Five experiments with between-subjects manipulation of logo shape (circular vs. angular). Units judged product and company attributes. Cognitive load varied by irrelevant visual imagery to test working memory mechanism. Logo shape introduced either as explicit brand element or through incidental priming. Attribute relevance (matching vs. mismatching logo shape associations) manipulated. Imagery generation tendency measured as individual difference.

what_manipulated: salience of resource availability (scarcity vs. abundance context)
what_measured: novelty of product use solutions
experiment: Experiments manipulating salience of resource scarcity versus abundance in an initial context, then measuring novelty of solutions to product usage problems in a subsequent unrelated consumption context. Within or between-subjects designs comparing creative solution quality. Proposed mediator (constraint mindset) and mechanism (reduced functional fixedness) examined across multiple product types.

what_manipulated: lateral display position of healthy versus unhealthy items (left vs. right)
what_measured: choice preference and consumption volume of healthy items
experiment: Seven studies with between-subjects manipulation of lateral display position (healthy-left/unhealthy-right vs. healthy-right/unhealthy-left) of food options. Units made choices between healthy and unhealthy options and consumed selected items. Choice preference and consumption quantity measured. Proposed mechanism tested: congruence with mental organization of food healthfulness enhances processing ease and self-control.

what_manipulated: exposure to limited-quantity promotion advertising (scarcity ads vs. control)
what_measured: actual aggressive behavior outside the purchasing domain
experiment: Seven studies using multiple behavioral measures of aggression. Units exposed to limited-quantity promotion advertisements or control materials. Aggressive behavior assessed in tasks unrelated to the promoted product domain. Proposed mechanism: perception of other shoppers as competitive threats and physiological preparation to aggress. Mediation pathway tested across multiple outcome measures.

what_manipulated: color saturation level of displayed products
what_measured: perceived product size, evaluations, and willingness to pay
experiment: Six experiments with color saturation manipulation across various product shapes and hues. Units rated product size, value, and purchase intent. Downstream outcomes measured including evaluations and willingness to pay under different usage goals (large vs. small size needed). Arousal induced by color saturation tested as mechanism. Comparative size perception of surrounding environment measured when different-saturation products used as benchmark.

what_manipulated: product rating distribution pattern (bimodal/polarized vs. unimodal/consensual)
what_measured: product desirability and self-expression perception
experiment: Eight studies examining effects of polarizing products (indicated by bimodal user rating distributions). Units shown products with bimodal versus unimodal rating distributions and rated desirability and self-expressiveness. Self-concept clarity and consumption context (self-expression relevant vs. irrelevant) varied. Attribute type (self-expressive vs. quality) manipulated. Identity level (individual vs. group) examined as moderator of effects.

what_manipulated: income level and financial situation (measured and experimentally induced)
what_measured: perceived financial well-being and overall well-being
experiment: Mixed-methods study using consumer financial narratives, large-scale surveys, and experiments. Two separable constructs of perceived financial well-being measured: current money management stress and expected future financial security. Novel measures developed and validated. Relationship to overall well-being estimated controlling for other life domains and objective financial indicators. Income groups compared for differential antecedent patterns. Relative importance of stress versus security to overall well-being estimated.

what_manipulated: self-construal (independent vs. interdependent orientation) and group size (small vs. large)
what_measured: choice made on behalf of self and multiple others for a shared outcome
experiment: Six studies across different choice contexts (wine, books, movies) in which a decision-maker selects an option that will be shared by a group. The decision-maker's self-construal is measured or varied, and group size is manipulated (small vs. large). Choices are analyzed for the degree to which they reflect self versus others' preferences. Mediation analysis tests whether differential attention to others explains the combined effect of self-construal and group size on choice.

what_manipulated: self-concept clarity (low vs. high uncertainty about identity) and subscription type (identity-relevant vs. identity-irrelevant)
what_measured: choice to keep or quit an ongoing subscription; willingness to adopt a new subscription
experiment: Units with varying self-concept clarity are presented with decisions to continue or discontinue subscriptions, and to adopt new subscriptions. The study distinguishes identity-relevant from identity-neutral subscriptions. Analysis tests whether low self-concept clarity predicts subscription retention for identity-relevant services and whether the effect differs from one-time purchases. Additional analyses examine whether the mechanism involves threat to self-concept stability.

what_manipulated: product anthropomorphism (humanized vs. non-humanized product options)
what_measured: comparative judgment strategy and resulting preference (absolute vs. dimension-by-dimension comparison)
experiment: Six experiments in which units compare two options presented as either anthropomorphized or non-anthropomorphized. Choices and information-processing behavior are measured via self-report, MouseLab software tracking, and eye-tracking. Analysis examines whether anthropomorphism shifts judgment strategy from dimension-by-dimension to holistic absolute judgment. Mediation tests perception of products as integrated entities. Moderation by motivation for accuracy or ease is examined.

what_manipulated: question topic: objective fact with partisan salience (inauguration crowd size comparison)
what_measured: likelihood of deliberately providing false response vs. stating genuine belief
experiment: Survey of units with varying political affiliation and engagement. A factual question with an objectively verifiable answer (crowd size comparison) that has high partisan salience is asked. The premise is that if responses are false despite the answer being clear and obvious, this indicates intentional expressive responding rather than genuine misperception. False responses are correlated with partisan identity and political interest level to assess the role of partisan motivation.

what_manipulated: probability of being allowed to trade (randomized assignment; low vs. high probability)
what_measured: choice to keep or trade item; willingness-to-accept valuation for item
experiment: Two experiments: (1) Exchange experiment in which units are endowed with an item and randomly assigned a probability (low vs. high) of being permitted to trade it away; retention choice is measured. (2) Valuation experiment in which units are randomly assigned a high or low probability of obtaining an item and then give their willingness-to-accept selling price. Analysis tests whether higher-probability treatments shift reference points, resulting in higher valuations or lower retention.

what_manipulated: random assignment to position in income distribution (last place vs. other positions)
what_measured: gamble choice; allocation decisions in distributional games; survey attitudes toward income redistribution policy
experiment: Laboratory experiments with three components: (1) Gamble task in which units are randomly placed in different positions in a distribution and choose between gambles; (2) Modified dictator game in which units in second-to-last position allocate money to the person above or below them; (3) Analysis of survey data correlating position in income distribution with stated preferences for redistribution policy. Tests whether units more often choose upward-moving gambles and upward-directed giving when in last place.

what_manipulated: primary-grade retention (retained vs. promoted)
what_measured: high school completion; college entry; college completion
experiment: Longitudinal study with units followed from primary school through college. Causal effect of grade retention is estimated using propensity score matching and sibling fixed-effects models to control for background confounds. Mediators (middle school achievement, high school expectations) are examined. Analysis traces when and through which mechanisms units recover from or persist with the scarring effect of early retention.

what_manipulated: time horizons (unspecified, expanded, or limited)
what_measured: preference for helping colleagues vs. career advancement vs. company innovation
experiment: Units (N=555) are randomly assigned to one of three time-horizon conditions (control: unspecified; experimental: expanded or limited horizons). Units then choose from three work-activity options and indicate their preference. Age is the predictor of interest. Analysis tests whether age predicts helping-colleague preference only under unspecified time horizons, and whether time-horizon framing eliminates age differences.

what_manipulated: intensive behavioral treatment (40 hours/week EIBT vs. standard special education classes)
what_measured: IQ; language development; nonverbal skill; adaptive behavior; educational placement level
experiment: A 3-year prospective outcome study comparing two groups: (1) 21 units receiving intensive behavioral treatment (35-40 hours/week), and (2) 21 matched units in special education classes (assignment based on parental preference, not randomization). Independent examiners assess units at baseline and years 1-3 using standardized instruments (IQ, language, nonverbal skills, adaptive behavior). Repeated-measures analyses of covariance test whether treatment group gains exceed control group gains. Final educational placement is recorded.

what_manipulated: presence or absence of a weak electrical prepulse preceding an intense electrical shock (prepulse at 40-60 ms before shock; at perceptual or 25% above threshold)
what_measured: rated pain intensity of electrical shocks
experiment: Units first have their pain threshold determined via electrical stimulation. Units then rate the painfulness of 25 intense electrical shocks (1.5x pain threshold, ~160V) presented either alone or preceded by weak electrical prepulses. Prepulse effectiveness is compared across units stratified by pain sensitivity. Analysis tests whether prepulses reduce rated pain and whether the effect is stronger in units with lower pain thresholds.

what_manipulated: action structure (joint action vs. individual action) in a goal-pursuit task
what_measured: preference for joint vs. individual action; effort expended on joint vs. individual task
experiment: Five experiments in which units perform goal-achievement tasks that can be completed either jointly with another unit or individually. The joint action option is costlier (requires more effort) than individual action but achieves the same outcome. A utility-based approach measures units' preference for performing actions together despite the higher instrumental cost. Experiments use different task types and measure both choice and actual effort allocation.

what_manipulated: none (observational study of personality traits via self- and partner-rating)
what_measured: relationship satisfaction
experiment: Pairs of units (74 married, 136 dating couples) provide self-ratings and cross-ratings of their own and partner's personality traits (Big Five and affectivity). Each unit's relationship satisfaction is then correlated with four sets of personality ratings: own self-rated traits, partner's rating of own traits, partner's self-rated traits, and own rating of partner's traits. Regression models test which personality dimensions best predict satisfaction and whether self- and partner-ratings yield similar predictive patterns.

what_manipulated: none (measurement and exploratory factor analysis of spirituality constructs)
what_measured: dimensions of spirituality expressions; relationship to Big Five personality model
experiment: Study 1 (N=534): factor analysis on 11 existing spirituality measures to identify latent dimensions. Study 2 (N=938): replication of factor structure and development of a new 98-item instrument (ESI) to operationalize the spirituality model. Correlations between ESI subscales and the NEO Personality Inventory-Revised are computed to test whether spirituality dimensions are unique from Big Five traits. Reliability and validity of the ESI are assessed.

what_manipulated: outcome affective content (affect-rich vs. affect-poor prizes) and probability (low vs. high)
what_measured: preference between a high-probability affect-poor outcome and a low-probability affect-rich outcome
experiment: Units make a series of risky choices between lotteries that vary in prize type (affect-rich outcomes like kisses or shocks vs. affect-poor monetary outcomes) and probability. Analysis tests the prediction that probability-sensitivity functions (weighting functions) will be more S-shaped for affect-rich than affect-poor outcomes. A probability-outcome interaction is examined: affect-poor prizes are preferred under certainty but lose preference at low probabilities.

what_manipulated: contingency between conditioned stimuli (novel objects) and unconditioned stimuli (valenced items: positive vs. negative)
what_measured: attitude toward novel conditioned stimuli (explicit and implicit measures)
experiment: Two experiments in which units view hundreds of randomly presented words and images, with critical pairings of novel objects (conditioned stimuli) with positive or negative items (unconditioned stimuli). In a surprise evaluation task, attitudes toward the novel objects are measured via both explicit rating and implicit assessment. A covariation estimation task tests whether units explicitly remember the pairings. Analysis examines whether paired valence shifts attitudes toward the neutral novel objects despite lack of explicit memory for the pairings.

what_manipulated: temporal sequence and presence of positive affect
what_measured: broad-minded coping strategy adoption and emotional well-being
experiment: Units completed self-report measures of affect and coping at two time points 5 weeks apart. Regression analyses assessed whether positive affect at time 1 predicted coping strategy at time 2, and vice versa, controlling for other variables. Mediational analysis examined serial reciprocal relationships. Analyses used prospective prediction to infer causal direction.

what_manipulated: transient spatial attention via peripheral precuing
what_measured: temporal resolution as measured by two-flash fusion threshold
experiment: Units completed a task measuring temporal resolution (the minimal time interval at which two light flashes are perceived as separate rather than merged) under conditions of manipulated transient spatial attention via peripheral precuing. The critical comparison was temporal resolution in attended versus unattended spatial locations. Changes in fusion thresholds were analyzed.

what_manipulated: marital status transitions (marriage to divorce) and marital duration
what_measured: arrest frequency and offending patterns
experiment: Monthly-level data across 13 years tracked status changes and arrest outcomes for a nationally representative sample. Within-individual multilevel models compared arrest rates during married periods versus following divorce, examining whether reductions in offending were enduring (stable across separation) or situational (reversing upon divorce). Moderating effects of marital duration were examined.

what_manipulated: racial/ethnic identity cues in written communication
what_measured: responsiveness of government bureaucrats to requests and tone of responses
experiment: Units (housing officials) received email requests for assistance varying in sender racial/ethnic identity (white, black, Hispanic). Response rates and email tone were compared across conditions. Field experiment measured whether officials responded and the language register of responses as functions of applicant identity.

what_manipulated: body mass index of visual stimuli presented to units
what_measured: estimated weight of bodies and just-noticeable difference in weight discrimination
experiment: Experiment 1: Units estimated weight of images varying in BMI; linear regression assessed underestimation magnitude as BMI increased. Experiment 2: Units compared pairs of bodies varying in BMI and discriminated which was heavier; analysis of variance tested whether the just-noticeable difference (minimum perceptual change) varied with stimulus BMI. Both experiments tested predictions derived from perceptual bias theories.

what_manipulated: stability vs. instability of causal relationships while holding overall causal strength constant
what_measured: endorsement of causal claims and willingness to use causal relationships as guides to action
experiment: Experiment 1: Units judged causal and explanatory claims for relationships varying in stability; regression assessed whether endorsement differed by stability, controlling for causal strength. Experiment 2: Manipulated generalization scope to test alternative explanations. Experiment 3: Measured action guidance judgments. Across studies, stability effects were isolated from confounds.

what_manipulated: not applicable (test development and validation study)
what_measured: global oral linguistic proficiency in a second language as measured by elicited imitation
experiment: Units completed an elicited imitation test, an oral narrative task, and a background questionnaire. Correlation and regression analyses examined whether test scores predict oral proficiency ratings, stratified by language ability level and learner type. Test validity was assessed via convergence with other proficiency measures.

what_manipulated: not specified (abstract too brief)
what_measured: not specified (abstract too brief)
experiment: Insufficient information in abstract to describe procedure, analysis, or comparison. Abstract provides only title and citation information.

what_manipulated: socioeconomic background (indexed by maternal education) as a grouping variable
what_measured: neural processing efficiency during selective attention and ability to filter irrelevant information
experiment: Young units listened to two narrative stories simultaneously while cued to attend to one; event-related brain potentials (ERP) were recorded to probe stimuli embedded in attended and unattended stories. Between-group analysis compared ERP components (selective attention effects) in units from higher versus lower socioeconomic backgrounds. Analysis focused on early (100 ms) filtering efficiency.

what_manipulated: absolute amount and proportion of resources given away by a puppet character
what_measured: ratings of niceness or fairness of the gift-giver
experiment: Units (children and adults) observed puppet characters distributing resources and rated which character was nicer. Within-subjects design crossed absolute amount given with proportion of available resources given. Analysis examined which cue (absolute vs. proportional) dominated judgments and how this changed with development. ANOVA and descriptive analysis of choice patterns were used.

what_manipulated: not applicable (philosophical literature review and reference list)
what_measured: not applicable (philosophical literature review and reference list)
experiment: Philosophical reference list and literature review; no empirical study design. Content appears to be a bibliography of philosophical works on intention and intentional action rather than a description of an experiment.

what_manipulated: implicit theory of intelligence (fixed vs. malleable) taught in Study 2; pre-existing implicit theory assessed in Study 1
what_measured: grade trajectories in mathematics and classroom motivation
experiment: Study 1: Longitudinal design tracking grades over two years; regression with implicit theory as predictor assessed linear trends in grades. Study 2: Randomized intervention: units assigned to receive either an incremental theory teaching session or control; post-intervention grades and motivation were compared between groups. Mediational analysis examined effects through learning goals, effort beliefs, and attributions.

what_manipulated: type of visual stimulus presented (social vs. other, inferred from design)
what_measured: hemodynamic activation (oxyhemoglobin concentration) in temporal lobe regions
experiment: Young units viewed visual stimuli while near infrared spectroscopy measured blood oxygenation in bilateral temporal lobes. Analysis identified regions showing significant changes in oxyhemoglobin relative to baseline. Localization of activation to specific temporal sites was examined to infer involvement in social brain networks.

what_manipulated: anchor values (self-generated vs. provided) and information search conditions
what_measured: magnitude of adjustment from anchor values and final estimates
experiment: Units made estimates of quantities under conditions where anchoring values were self-generated or provided, and where search for accurate information was encouraged or not. Regression analyses compared adjustment magnitudes (actual adjustment - insufficient adjustment) across conditions. Studies 1a-1b examined termination at plausible values; Studies 2a-2c tested whether encouraging deeper search reduced insufficient adjustment.

what_manipulated: numerical information presented in various formats and numeracy level of units as a measured grouping variable
what_measured: judgment and decision outcomes including susceptibility to framing effects
experiment: Four studies varied in task type but all involved numerical probability comprehension. Units varying in numeracy (measured via assessment) completed judgment or decision tasks; correlation and regression analyses examined whether numeracy predicted task performance and framing susceptibility, controlling for general intelligence. Between-group analyses compared high-numerate versus low-numerate units' use of numerical principles and affective responses.

what_manipulated: power level via priming manipulation in some studies; power as individual difference measured in correlational study
what_measured: perspective-taking accuracy, egocentric bias, and empathy-related judgments
experiment: Experiments 1-3: Units were primed with high vs. low power and then completed perspective-taking tasks (drawing orientation, knowledge adjustment, emotion recognition) measuring egocentric bias. Accuracy scores and response patterns were compared between power conditions. Additional study measured individual differences in power and correlated with perspective-taking measures. Mixed analysis of variance tested interaction effects of power condition.

what_manipulated: own outcome knowledge (whether unit knew event result) as a within-subject factor
what_measured: false-belief reasoning accuracy; ability to predict another unit's belief given contradictory information
experiment: Units watched an event and then reasoned about what another person would believe about that event, either knowing or not knowing the actual outcome. Accuracy on false-belief reasoning tasks was compared within-subjects across knowledge conditions. Analyses tested for curse-of-knowledge bias (own knowledge impairing reasoning about others' false beliefs). Plausibility ratings were examined as a mediator.

what_manipulated: beliefs attributed to an actor (true vs. false beliefs about object location)
what_measured: anticipatory eye gaze toward predicted action target
experiment: Young units watched a scene where an actor held a false belief about an object's location, then watched as the actor searched for the object. Eye-tracking recorded where units looked in anticipation of the actor's search. Gaze was directed to locations consistent with the actor's false belief rather than the true object location. Timing and direction of anticipatory looking were analyzed.

what_manipulated: conflict between response options presented during decision making
what_measured: trajectory and velocity of reaching movements toward response options
experiment: Units made evaluative judgments (e.g., whether murder is justified) by moving a computer mouse toward response options (agree/disagree). Reaching trajectories were recorded and analyzed for deflection toward non-chosen alternatives. On trials with high response conflict, trajectories were compared to low-conflict trials. Analysis examined whether simultaneous pull from multiple alternatives influenced motor execution rather than just final decision.

what_manipulated: presence or absence of response conflict on a current trial; presence of prospective memory cues
what_measured: goal shielding as indexed by forgetting of prospective memory cues and interference susceptibility
experiment: Units performed a spatial task while monitoring for rare prospective memory cues. Trials varied in whether they contained response conflicts. Primary analysis compared prospective memory cue detection on conflict versus nonconflict trials; cues were noticed less often on conflict trials. Secondary analysis examined whether this reflected heightened goal shielding and distraction inhibition on conflict trials themselves. Accuracy rates compared across conditions.

what_manipulated: age group (younger vs. older adults)
what_measured: number of episodic (internal) and contextual (external) details generated in autobiographical narratives
experiment: Units of different ages were asked to generate past and future autobiographical events. Transcriptions were segmented into details and classified as internal (episodic) or external. The number of details was compared between age groups and across past/future event types. Correlations between detail counts and relational memory abilities were also examined.

what_manipulated: target viewpoint (prototypical vs. nonprototypical views) and target definition (name-based vs. action-based)
what_measured: fixation probability and fixation duration during visual search
experiment: Units performed visual search for targets defined either by their names or by the action typically performed on them. Targets appeared in prototypical or nonprototypical views. Eye movements were recorded. First-fixation probability and fixation duration were analyzed by target view and definition type, stratified by visual field location.

what_manipulated: exposure to text promoting a belief in determinism vs. neutral text
what_measured: cheating behavior and belief in free will
experiment: Units were assigned to read either deterministic or neutral messages. In Experiment 1, cheating was measured via a task where a flawed computer program could reveal answers; mediation analysis tested whether reduced free-will belief explained increased cheating. In Experiment 2, cheating was measured via self-reported payment for cognitive performance. Tests included comparisons of cheating rates and mediation analysis.

what_manipulated: stimulus onset asynchrony (SOA) between two targets in rapid serial visual presentation
what_measured: detection accuracy and distractor intrusions at different temporal intervals
experiment: Units viewed rapid sequences of items and reported two targets (T1 and T2) presented at varying SOAs (roughly 200-500 ms). Distractor intrusions were analyzed as a function of SOA to estimate temporal selection suppression, latency, and precision. Results were described as dissociated time-course effects.

what_manipulated: presence or absence of extremal edge (viewpoint-specific self-occlusion cue) on a surface border; cue type (shading vs. texture gradient)
what_measured: depth perception and figure-ground organization judgments
experiment: Units viewed images containing surfaces with and without extremal edges along shared borders. In separate experiments, cues were provided via shading or texture gradients. Units judged relative depth and figure-ground assignment. Comparisons tested whether extremal-edge surfaces were perceived as closer and as figure.

what_manipulated: number of tones in a chord and interstimulus interval (ISI) duration
what_measured: change-detection accuracy for frequency shifts
experiment: Units made same/different judgments on successive chords and rated tone-frequency comparisons. The change was always a frequency shift in one tone. Complexity (number of tones) and ISI (0 ms vs. 2 s) were varied. Performance was compared across complexity and ISI conditions.

what_manipulated: object words denoting spatial locations (head vs. foot, high vs. low objects)
what_measured: target-identification accuracy and speed for visual stimuli
experiment: Units viewed object words and subsequently identified visual targets presented at various vertical positions. The prediction was that words encoding typical object locations would orient attention and activate spatial simulations, impairing target identification when targets appeared at the word's typical location. Identification accuracy was compared across word-target congruence conditions.

what_manipulated: external motivation to respond without prejudice (low vs. high) and face race and expression (White/Black, neutral/happy) and presentation duration (30 ms vs. 450 ms)
what_measured: attentional bias on a dot-probe task
experiment: Units with low and high external motivation were presented with face pairs (White and Black, neutral or happy) at two durations. A probe followed, and reaction times indicated attentional bias. Attentional patterns were compared across motivation level, race, expression, and duration conditions.

what_manipulated: commission of errors vs. correct responses during a task
what_measured: error-related negativity (ERN) amplitude and defensive startle response magnitude
experiment: Units performed a task inducing errors. Event-related potentials (ERP) were recorded to measure ERN. Startle magnitude was measured following errors and correct responses. Regression analysis tested whether ERN amplitude predicted individual differences in startle potentiation after errors.

what_manipulated: induced loneliness (chronically lonely vs. experimentally primed) and exposure condition (observational vs. experimental for Studies 2-3)
what_measured: anthropomorphism and belief in humanlike nonhuman agents
experiment: Units were assessed for chronic loneliness (Study 1) or induced to feel lonely (Studies 2-3). Anthropomorphic judgments and belief in humanlike agents were measured. Three studies examined whether loneliness increased attributions of humanlike qualities. Study 3 tested whether the effect was specific to negative affect or a general negative-state effect.

what_manipulated: precision of numerical anchor (precise vs. rounded)
what_measured: magnitude of adjustment away from anchor in numerical judgments
experiment: Units made numerical estimates after exposure to either precise or rounded anchors. Five studies varied anchor precision and measurement domains. Adjustment magnitude was compared between anchor-precision conditions. Subjective scale resolution was inferred from patterns of adjustment.

what_manipulated: male relationship status (single vs. attached) and female menstrual-cycle phase (low vs. high fertility)
what_measured: attractiveness ratings of males
experiment: Partnered women (N=208) rated attractiveness of men described as single or attached. Phase of menstrual cycle was assessed. Attractiveness ratings were compared across male-status and cycle-phase conditions, stratified by male masculinity (genetic-quality cue).

what_manipulated: evaluative goal (positive, negative, or overall evaluation of names) during fMRI
what_measured: amygdala activation in response to positive and negative stimuli
experiment: Units performed fMRI while evaluating famous names along different goal dimensions. Amygdala activation was measured in response to positive and negative names under each goal condition. Within-subject contrasts tested whether activation patterns varied by evaluative goal and stimulus valence.

what_manipulated: cognitive fluency (fluent vs. disfluent stimulus processing) induced via visual perceptual, conceptual priming, or linguistic manipulations
what_measured: level of mental construal (concrete vs. abstract) and perceived psychological distance
experiment: Three studies manipulated fluency via different mechanisms: visual perceptual fluency, conceptual priming, and linguistic fluency. Units processed stimuli and made construal judgments or distance estimates. Construal level and distance judgments were compared between fluency conditions.

what_manipulated: encoding task (survival relevance rating vs. imagery rating, pleasantness rating, self-reference rating, word generation, intentional learning, or scenario relevance rating)
what_measured: retention of words on a later memory test
experiment: Units encoded words under different task conditions. Retention was later tested. Memory performance was compared across encoding conditions, with survival processing pitted against multiple control conditions known to produce strong retention.

what_manipulated: speaker visibility (speaker exits/returns to room) and linguistic frame (statement 'there's...' vs. question 'where's...') and object visibility configuration
what_measured: selection of target object on word-learning trials based on speaker's gaze
experiment: Two experiments manipulated what the speaker could see and the linguistic framing of the novel word. Units (ages 2-4) heard a novel word with varied speaker context. Target selection was compared across visibility and linguistic conditions, and above-chance performance was tested.

what_manipulated: language (French vs. English) and naming task (color naming vs. object naming) and phonological similarity between color and object names
what_measured: phonological congruency effect on naming latency
experiment: Units from French and English-speaking populations named colored objects. Phonological similarity between color and object names was manipulated. Naming latencies were compared across language, task, and phonological-similarity conditions. Word-order constraints were inferred from cross-linguistic contrasts.

what_manipulated: behavioral information about group members (positive vs. negative behaviors) and time delay (immediate vs. days later) and evaluation type (implicit vs. explicit)
what_measured: attitude toward new group members (generalization from original member)
experiment: Units learned positive and negative behaviors performed by individuals from two groups. Implicit and explicit evaluations of new group members were assessed immediately and days later. Generalization was compared across time, evaluation type, and group-membership link.

what_manipulated: glucose ingestion (sugar vs. sugar substitute) and prior self-control task (depleted vs. not depleted)
what_measured: susceptibility to the attraction effect (decoy bias) in decision making
experiment: Units consumed either sugar-sweetened or sugar-free lemonade. Before the decision task, some units performed a self-control task. Units then made choices between options where a decoy option was present. Attraction-effect magnitude was compared across glucose and depletion conditions.

what_manipulated: priming of nonconscious achievement goals vs. control priming during exposure to novel environments
what_measured: implicit learning of environmental structure
experiment: Two experiments using markedly different implicit-learning paradigms primed nonconscious achievement goals or control conditions. Units were exposed to completely novel environments. Learning of environmental structure was assessed. Comparisons tested whether goal priming facilitated implicit learning.

what_manipulated: exposure to static images depicting motion in a particular direction
what_measured: motion aftereffect in opposite direction
experiment: Units view a series of static photographs with implied motion in one direction, then are tested with real-motion probes moving in various directions. Motion aftereffects are assessed by measuring the illusion of motion perceived in the opposite direction. Within-subjects comparison: same units tested before and after viewing static images. Analysis examines transfer of adaptation between implied and real motion domains.

what_manipulated: presence of threat-relevant stimulus among distractors
what_measured: visual detection latency
experiment: Units search for a target image within an array of eight distractor images and detection time is measured. Between-subjects comparison (implicitly via age group) but more fundamentally: presence of threat-relevant target versus nonthreatening targets. Same units search for multiple target types (snake, flowers, frogs, caterpillars). Speed comparisons across target types within units. Statistical test compares detection times for threat versus nonthreat targets.

what_manipulated: spatial distance prime (closeness versus distance task)
what_measured: multiple outcomes: enjoyment of embarrassment depiction, emotional distress from violent media, food calorie estimation, emotional attachment strength
experiment: Units complete a spatial task: plotting points on a coordinate plane in either spatially close or spatially distant arrangement. Four separate experiments follow, each measuring different dependent variables (enjoyment, distress, food estimation, attachment). Between-subjects comparison: closeness-primed units versus distance-primed units on each outcome. Statistical tests examine main effects of distance prime on each outcome measure across four studies.

what_manipulated: measurement method type (self-report versus peer-report versus perceived national character)
what_measured: correlation with behavioral and demographic predictors
experiment: Behavioral and demographic predictors are identified. Cross-national scores on a trait are obtained via three measurement methods. Correlations are computed between predictors and each measurement method's scores at the country level. Observational design: no manipulation, only measurement comparison. Statistical test compares correlation magnitudes across measurement methods to assess which validly captures true between-country differences.

what_manipulated: task type: confrontational versus nonconfrontational context
what_measured: motivation to regulate emotion toward anger
experiment: Units are told they will complete either a confrontational or nonconfrontational task. Before the task, they choose an emotion-inducing activity that will either increase anger or excitement. Between-subjects comparison: confrontational task units versus nonconfrontational units on activity choice. Subsequently, units perform the assigned task and performance is measured. Within-task: anger effect on performance is assessed. Statistical tests examine whether anger-increasing activity selection differs by task type and whether anger improves performance in confrontational but not nonconfrontational contexts.

what_manipulated: prediction type offered: accurate, optimistic, or pessimistic
what_measured: normative endorsement by units and attribution to others
experiment: Units are asked what kinds of predictions they believe themselves and others ought to make regarding personal outcomes. Responses are collected regarding endorsement of accuracy, optimism, and pessimism as normative. Observational design: units report their beliefs without experimental manipulation of belief formation. Statistical analysis summarizes and compares endorsement patterns across prediction types.

what_manipulated: fairness of offer (fair versus unfair, controlling for monetary payoff)
what_measured: self-reported happiness and brain activation in reward and emotion-regulation regions
experiment: Units view and rate monetary offers that are either fair or unfair in distribution but equated for personal monetary gain. Units report happiness during rating. Brain imaging captures activation in reward regions and prefrontal/insula areas during evaluation. Within-subjects design: same units rate both fair and unfair offers. Statistical tests compare happiness ratings and activation patterns between fair and unfair conditions. Mediation analysis explores relationship between activation patterns and acceptance of unfair offers.

what_manipulated: gender of unit (male versus female)
what_measured: perceptual sensitivity and decisional bias in interpreting behavior cues
experiment: Units view nonverbal behavioral cues and categorize them as indicating sexual interest or friendliness. Data are analyzed using signal detection theory, decomposing judgments into perceptual sensitivity (d-prime) and decision criterion (beta). Between-subjects design: male units versus female units. Statistical analysis compares sensitivity and bias parameters across genders. Gender differences are tested to distinguish between threshold explanation versus perceptual insensitivity explanation.

what_manipulated: dominance level of unit (high versus low)
what_measured: spatial attention preference: vertical versus horizontal dimension
experiment: Units with measured dominance levels complete spatial attention paradigms (two different paradigms across studies). Response times to probes along vertical and horizontal dimensions are recorded. Between-subjects comparison: high-dominance units versus low-dominance units. Statistical tests examine whether dominance predicts faster responding to vertical probes. Analyses control for overall response speed to isolate spatial attention effects.

what_manipulated: possession status (owned versus not owned) and desire level (want versus do not want)
what_measured: happiness rating
experiment: Units report on 52 material items: whether owned and extent of desire. Happiness is measured separately. Wanting-what-have is computed as the extent desired for items actually owned. Having-what-want is computed as possession status of highly desired items. Within-subjects design: same units rate multiple items. Regression analysis examines unique variance in happiness explained by each variable. Mediation analysis tests whether wanting-what-have and having-what-want mediate effects of gratitude and maximization.

what_manipulated: attention allocation (focused attention versus withdrawn attention)
what_measured: precision of representation: individual object location versus centroid location
experiment: Units view arrays of objects under two attention conditions: attention focused within array versus attention focused elsewhere. Precision of representing individual object locations and centroid (mean location) are measured using discrimination or estimation tasks. Within-subjects design: same units tested under both attention conditions. Three separate experiments test this pattern. Statistical analysis compares degradation of location versus centroid representation under attention withdrawal.

what_manipulated: contribution pool type: within-group cooperative pool versus between-group competitive pool option
what_measured: monetary contribution amounts to each pool type
experiment: Units are assigned to groups and receive an endowment. They decide how much to contribute to either a within-group pool (benefits in-group at personal cost) or a between-group pool (benefits in-group and harms out-group at personal cost). Between-subjects comparison via pool availability: groups with choice versus groups with restricted availability. Within-subjects: same units indicate intended contributions. Group communication condition is varied (intragroup communication present versus absent). Statistical tests examine contribution patterns and the effect of communication on cooperation.

what_manipulated: aggregation: individual estimates versus averaged group estimates
what_measured: accuracy of estimate (error magnitude)
experiment: Units make individual estimates or judgments on a task. Group estimates are computed as averages of individual estimates. Accuracy is measured by comparing estimates to true value. Between-subjects or within-subjects comparison of individual error versus average error. Statistical analysis tests whether average error is smaller than typical individual error, examining independence of individual errors as underlying mechanism.

what_manipulated: time interval: 30 minutes, 12 hours awake, 12 hours including sleep
what_measured: memory accuracy for emotional objects versus backgrounds
experiment: Units view negative-emotion scenes containing focal objects in contextual backgrounds. Memory is tested at three delays: 30 minutes, 12 daytime hours, 12 nighttime hours. Within-subjects design: same scenes tested at multiple time points in different conditions. Memory for focal objects and background information is separately assessed. Statistical analysis examines forgetting curves for objects versus backgrounds across delays, comparing rates of decay between sleep and awake conditions.

what_manipulated: temporal direction of event: future versus past
what_measured: compensation required or offered for equivalent event
experiment: Units imagine receiving or providing compensation for events happening at future or past time points, equidistant from present. They indicate how much compensation is required or fair. Between-subjects comparison: future events versus past events. Within-subjects comparison also conducted (same units judge both temporal directions). Affect is measured separately. Statistical tests examine whether temporal value asymmetry holds, and mediation analysis tests whether affect difference explains compensation asymmetry.

what_manipulated: cleanliness salience: cognitive activation or physical cleansing action
what_measured: moral judgment severity (wrongness rating)
experiment: Units undergo a cleanliness manipulation: either cognitive activation of cleanliness concept or physical hand-washing following disgust induction. Subsequently, units rate moral wrongness of various actions. Between-subjects design: cleanliness-manipulation units versus control units. Within-subjects: same units judge multiple moral actions. Statistical tests compare wrongness ratings between conditions. Analysis examines whether cleanliness salience reduces moral judgment severity compared to disgust baseline.

what_manipulated: weight of object held (heavy versus light clipboard)
what_measured: multiple outcomes: monetary value judgments, importance of fairness, consistency of related judgments, polarization of argument agreement ratings
experiment: Units hold either a heavy or light clipboard while making judgments and ratings. Between-subjects design: heavy clipboard condition versus light clipboard condition. Within-subjects: same units provide multiple judgment types (monetary value, fairness importance, argument strength ratings). Statistical tests examine main effects of weight on each outcome. Analysis includes comparison of consistency metrics and polarization patterns.

what_manipulated: handedness (right-handed versus left-handed units)
what_measured: perceived arm and hand length
experiment: Units view their own right and left arms and hands, individually, and provide length estimates. Between-subjects design: right-handed units versus left-handed units. Within-subjects: same units estimate length of both arms. Perceived length is compared to actual measured length to assess bias. Statistical tests examine whether perceived length differs by arm side and whether this asymmetry is moderated by handedness.

what_manipulated: action structure: action versus inaction, varying consequences for harm and benefit outcomes
what_measured: moral judgment: permissibility rating
experiment: Units evaluate multiple moral dilemmas via web interface. Each dilemma presents a choice between action and inaction, with specified outcomes: lives saved and lives lost under each option. Within-subjects design: same units judge multiple dilemmas. Judgments are compared to predictions from the principle of double effect. Between-subjects: examine consistency across demographic groups. Statistical analysis tests whether judgment patterns align with double effect principle and explores individual differences.

what_manipulated: valence of side-effect: good side-effect versus bad side-effect
what_measured: knowledge attribution regarding agent's awareness of side-effect
experiment: Units read scenarios in which an agent performs an action that produces a side-effect of specified valence. Units judge whether the agent knew the side-effect would occur. Between-subjects design: good side-effect condition versus bad side-effect condition. Same action and agent outcome structure, only side-effect valence varies. Statistical tests compare knowledge attributions between valence conditions.

what_manipulated: cultural distance of target individual relative to perceiver's own culture
what_measured: objectivism versus relativism in moral judgments
experiment: Six studies presented units with moral scenarios involving targets from their own culture or increasingly distant cultures. Units judged the moral truth-value of actions or statements across these conditions. Analysis tracked the proportion of objectivist versus relativist responses as cultural distance varied. No specific statistical test is detailed; comparisons appear descriptive across the culture-distance manipulation.

what_manipulated: prior engagement with a reflection task; argument strength and deliberation time in a moral scenario
what_measured: utilitarian versus non-utilitarian responses to moral dilemmas
experiment: Experiment 1: units completed a reflection task (Cognitive Reflection Test) before responding to moral dilemmas; utilitarian response rates were compared between high and low reflectiveness conditions. A follow-up associated trait reflectiveness with utilitarian judgment. Experiment 2: units considered a morally charged scenario; a two-factor design crossed argument strength and deliberation time. Analysis examined how these factors and their interaction predicted moral judgment. Statistical tests are not specified in the abstract.

what_manipulated: fertility expectations recorded in adolescence
what_measured: college enrollment (4-year vs 2-year vs none) and college persistence
experiment: Longitudinal analysis of N=7,838 adolescents with college enrollment outcome, and subsamples (4-year n=2,605; 2-year n=1,962) with persistence outcomes. Data from the National Longitudinal Survey of Youth 1997. Analysis examined association between adolescent fertility expectations and subsequent college enrollment; pregnancies and college dropout risk were also examined. Statistical methods not specified; the abstract notes rare pregnancy events among college women.

what_manipulated: relationship duration, age, health status, and relationship history
what_measured: presence or absence of sexual activity in past 12 months
experiment: Event-history analysis and logistic regression on N=1,502 married units from a nationally representative sample of 3,005 noninstitutionalized adults aged 57-85 (National Social Life, Health, and Aging Project). Outcome was binary (sexual inactivity past 12 months: 29% reported). Analyses identified independent associations of predictor variables with sexual inactivity; gender differences were also examined.

what_manipulated: individual differences in inhibitory control
what_measured: accuracy in phonological perception and production in a second language
experiment: Units (Spanish learners of L2 English and American learners of L2 Spanish) completed a retrieval-induced inhibition task to measure inhibitory control. Phonological accuracy was assessed via speeded ABX categorization (perception) and delayed sentence repetition (production). L2 vocabulary size was measured as a covariate to isolate proficiency effects. Correlational analysis examined relationships between inhibition and segmental perception, consonant production, and vowel production accuracy.

what_manipulated: the default option for organ donor registration status
what_measured: organ donor registration rates and donations
experiment: Natural and experimental data examined policy defaults affecting the decision to become an organ donor. Units were observed or randomly assigned to conditions with different default options (opt-in vs opt-out or similar). Outcome was donor registration rate or donation behavior. Analysis compared registration and donation rates across default conditions.

what_manipulated: induction of moral threat or unethical behavior
what_measured: mental accessibility of cleansing concepts, desire for cleansing products, likelihood of cleansing behavior, and moral self-image threat
experiment: Three studies experimentally induced moral threat. Study 1 examined mental accessibility of cleansing concepts and desire for cleansing products following moral threat. Study 2 measured likelihood of taking antiseptic wipes. Study 3 examined whether physical cleansing alleviates the upsetting consequences of unethical behavior. Analysis tracked increased accessibility, greater product desire, and greater cleansing likelihood in moral-threat conditions; post-cleansing reductions in moral threat were also examined.

what_manipulated: governance rules, communication, and opportunity for costly punishment in a resource-harvesting environment
what_measured: resource harvesting rates and use of costly punishment
experiment: Laboratory experiment with units harvesting resources in an environment with spatial and temporal dynamics mimicking field conditions. A within-subjects design varied governance rules and communication opportunities. Costly punishment was available. Outcome measures were total resource harvesting (gross benefits) and frequency of costly punishment. Analysis compared harvesting rates and punishment use across conditions.

what_manipulated: post-decision hand washing (physical cleansing)
what_measured: postdecisional dissonance effect (attractiveness rating of chosen vs rejected alternative)
experiment: Units made a choice between two alternatives. Half underwent hand washing; half did not. Outcome was the magnitude of postdecisional dissonance—increase in rated attractiveness of chosen alternative and decrease in rejected alternative. Between-subjects design compared dissonance effect in cleansing versus control condition.

what_manipulated: tactile properties of held or touched objects (weight, texture, hardness)
what_measured: impressions and decisions about unrelated people and situations
experiment: Six studies involved units holding heavy or light clipboards, solving rough or smooth puzzles, or touching hard or soft objects while making judgments about unrelated targets (job candidates, social interactions, negotiations). Tactile properties were manipulated between-subjects. Outcomes included importance ratings of job candidates, difficulty judgments of social interactions, and rigidity in negotiation. Analysis examined effects of tactile properties on target judgments.

what_manipulated: presence and beliefs of a social agent
what_measured: reaction times and looking times in a visual detection task
experiment: Adults and 7-month-old infants performed a visual object detection task. An agent with known (but task-irrelevant) beliefs was either present or had previously left the scene. Within-subjects or repeated-measures design compared reaction times (adults) or looking times (infants) when an agent's belief matched the participant's versus when it conflicted. Analysis examined whether agent beliefs modulated response latency independent of participants' own beliefs.

what_manipulated: learning condition: test-restudy versus restudy only
what_measured: accuracy of mediators (information linking cues to targets) and effectiveness in eliciting target responses
experiment: Units studied foreign language translations under two conditions: test-restudy practice versus restudy only. A between-subjects design manipulated learning condition. Outcome measures were retrievability of mediators at test and likelihood that retrieved mediators elicited correct target responses on a final test. Analysis compared mediator quality and performance across conditions.

what_manipulated: number of times a food is imagined to be consumed
what_measured: actual consumption of the imagined food and desire to eat it
experiment: Five studies manipulated imagined consumption frequency. Units repeatedly imagined eating a target food (e.g., cheese) either many times or few times, imagined eating a different food, or did not imagine eating food. A between-subjects design varied imagined consumption. Outcome was actual consumption of the target food at a subsequent test. Analysis examined consumption amount and mediating desire for the food.

what_manipulated: learning activity: retrieval practice versus elaborative studying with concept mapping
what_measured: meaningful learning as assessed by comprehension and inference test questions
experiment: Units studied science texts via retrieval practice or elaborative studying with concept mapping. A between-subjects design manipulated learning activity. Outcomes were performance on final tests assessing comprehension and inference-requiring questions. A within-subjects component tested transfer: final criterion tests used both comprehension questions and concept-map creation. Analysis compared learning gains across study conditions.

what_manipulated: expressive writing about test worries (brief writing assignment immediately before exam)
what_measured: exam performance (score) on a high-stakes test
experiment: Two laboratory and two classroom experiments used randomized assignment to expressive-writing or control condition. Units either wrote about their thoughts regarding an upcoming high-stakes exam or completed a control task immediately before the exam. Outcome was exam score. Subgroup analysis examined whether effects differed for units with high versus low test anxiety. Analysis compared exam performance across conditions and anxiety groups.

what_manipulated: expectation of future information access (availability of search engines)
what_measured: recall of the information itself versus recall of where to access it
