experiment: Four studies presented units with difficult questions. In some conditions, units expected future access to information via search; in others they did not. Outcomes were recall accuracy for the information itself and recall for the location or access method. A between-subjects design varied expectation of access. Analysis examined priming effects (increased computer thoughts when facing difficult questions) and subsequent recall patterns.

what_manipulated: presence and type of affirmative action policy (quota vs. preferential treatment vs. control)
what_measured: composition of selected candidates and overall task performance efficiency
experiment: Units compete in a laboratory-based task under different policy regimes. One study manipulates affirmative action policy type and measures how selection composition and overall outcome differ. A second study examines a naturally varying policy (village-council female reservation in West Bengal) across two election cycles, observing downstream effects on family aspirations, school time, and household chores. Comparisons are made across conditions and time periods to assess policy impact on composition and efficiency.

what_manipulated: cognitive processing mode (analytic vs. intuitive, via experimental trigger or individual difference)
what_measured: strength of religious belief or disbelief
experiment: Individual differences in analytic thinking (measured via reasoning tasks) are correlated with religious disbelief. Four experiments use subtle manipulations known to trigger analytic processing and measure subsequent change in religious belief. Comparisons are made between manipulated and control units to test whether analytic processing causes disbelief. Statistical association and causal inference from experimental conditions are examined.

what_manipulated: type of recent episodic memory engagement (encoding a novel object vs. retrieving an old object)
what_measured: mnemonic bias in subsequent memory task performance (pattern separation vs. pattern completion)
experiment: Units engage in either object encoding or retrieval. Subsequent memory performance is tested on tasks requiring either pattern separation (identifying subtle changes) or pattern completion (integrating stored information). The comparison between encoding-primed and retrieval-primed conditions reveals whether recent engagement shifts mnemonic biases. Within-unit design measures how prior task type affects subsequent performance.

what_manipulated: presence or level of resource scarcity (low vs. high scarcity condition)
what_measured: attentional allocation and decision-making outcomes (e.g., borrowing behavior in simulations)
experiment: Across multiple experiments, units are placed in scarcity-inducing vs. control conditions. Attentional engagement and choice behavior are measured. Comparisons between scarcity and control reveal shifts in attention and decision patterns. Analysis examines how scarcity-induced attention reallocation mediates or predicts downstream behavior choices.

what_manipulated: visual stimulus cue (body cues present vs. absent; facial expressions paired with congruent vs. incongruent bodies)
what_measured: discrimination and perception of emotion valence (positive vs. negative)
experiment: Units view video clips of emotional scenes with isolated bodies, isolated faces, or compounds of facial expressions paired with bodies of opposite emotional valence. Tasks include emotion discrimination (discriminate positive from negative) and affect perception rating. Comparisons between stimulus types reveal whether bodies or faces drive discrimination and whether faces show illusory affect in context. Unit mimicry and perception shifts are measured as a function of stimulus composition.

what_manipulated: financial scarcity (experimentally induced thoughts of financial constraints vs. control; or naturally occurring pre- vs. post-harvest farmer status)
what_measured: cognitive performance (e.g., fluid cognition, attention, working memory assessments)
experiment: Study 1: Units are randomly assigned to think about finances vs. neutral control, then complete cognitive tasks. Comparison of performance between groups is made, separately by income level. Study 2: Same farmers are tested twice, before and after harvest, varying in wealth. Within-unit analysis compares cognitive performance in poor (pre-harvest) vs. rich (post-harvest) conditions. Mediators (time availability, nutrition, work effort, stress) are measured or controlled to isolate the causal mechanism.

what_manipulated: type of reading material (literary fiction vs. popular fiction vs. nonfiction vs. nothing)
what_measured: performance on theory-of-mind assessments (affective and cognitive)
experiment: Five experiments compare units randomly assigned to read literary fiction, popular fiction, nonfiction, or serve as control (no reading). After reading, units complete tests of affective and cognitive theory of mind. Comparisons between literary fiction and other conditions reveal whether literary fiction enhances theory-of-mind performance. Analysis examines temporary effects immediately post-reading.

what_manipulated: presence of external stimuli or isolation (being alone with thoughts vs. access to various stimuli)
what_measured: preference for remaining alone (vs. engaging external stimulus or self-harm via shock)
experiment: Units are placed in an environment alone with minimal external stimuli for a period. Their choice to either remain alone, engage provided stimuli, or self-administer mild electric shock is recorded. Comparisons between units (or conditions) reveal preferences for stimulus engagement over solitude. Data are analyzed by demographic group (gender noted).

what_manipulated: overhead cost assignment (donors pay vs. donors do not pay; overhead framing: covered by external donor vs. standard information about seed money or matching)
what_measured: donation amount and donation rate (likelihood of donating)
experiment: A laboratory experiment manipulates overhead cost burden (donors pay vs. do not pay) and measures donation decisions. A field experiment with 40,000 units tests three conditions: (1) overhead-free donations (covered by initial seed money), (2) seed money information, (3) dollar-for-dollar matching. Donation rate and total donation amount are compared between conditions. Statistical analysis quantifies the effect of overhead framing on giving behavior.

what_manipulated: linguistic structure or rule type presented in an artificial language (familiar vs. unfamiliar abstract rules)
what_measured: looking time or attention to sentences with different rule structures
experiment: Infants are exposed to an artificial language with specific abstract algebraic rules. Test sentences either follow the familiar rule or violate it (unfamiliar structure). Attention (looking time) is measured for each sentence type. Comparison of looking time between familiar and unfamiliar conditions reveals whether infants discriminate rule structures. The design rules out alternative explanations (counting, transitional probability, simple networks).

what_manipulated: framing or formulation of the same decision problem (e.g., gain frame vs. loss frame; lives saved vs. lives lost)
what_measured: preference or choice made (choice of option, risk tolerance in choice)
experiment: Units face decision problems presented in logically equivalent but differently framed versions (e.g., positive frame as gains vs. negative frame as losses). Choice or preference is recorded for each frame. Comparisons show preference reversals when the same problem is reframed. Multiple problem types and stakes (monetary, human lives) are tested. Analysis demonstrates frame-dependent preference shifts.

what_manipulated: progression through programming course sequence (introductory non-major vs. CS1/CS2/CS3 sequence) or experience level
what_measured: frequency and type of static analysis errors detected in code submissions
experiment: Student code submissions across five semesters (over 500,000 submissions, ~10 million errors) are analyzed using static analysis tools. Errors are categorized by type (formatting, Javadoc, logic flaws). Error rates are compared between groups (CS majors vs. non-majors) and tracked over progression through the course sequence. Correlations between error type and eventual program correctness are examined. Analysis describes how error patterns change with experience level.

what_manipulated: origin attribution of online profile text (human-written vs. AI-written; or mixed presentation)
what_measured: trustworthiness perception of service provider; hiring or selection intention
experiment: Three experiments present hiring managers with online profiles of service providers (e.g., Airbnb hosts). Profile origin is either labeled or suspected to be AI-written, human-written, or presented as a mixed set. Trustworthiness perception and selection intention are measured. Comparisons between conditions reveal effects of AI-origin attribution. The Replicant Effect (reduced trust in AI profiles only when mixed profiles are shown) is documented through this comparison.

what_manipulated: prior cognitive load (90 minutes of demanding cognitive task vs. watching neutral documentaries)
what_measured: physical endurance (time to exhaustion at fixed workload) and perception of effort during exercise
experiment: Sixteen units undergo a randomized crossover design. Each unit completes one of two 90-minute conditions (demanding cognitive task or control video), then cycles to exhaustion at 80% peak power. Time to exhaustion is the primary outcome. Perception of effort (Borg scale), cardiorespiratory responses, mood, and motivation are measured post-intervention. Physiological mediators are assessed. Within-unit comparison reveals whether cognitive fatigue affects physical performance and through which mechanisms.

what_manipulated: inflation or price variation scenario (implicitly assumed to be presented; survey context)
what_measured: monetary evaluation tendency (nominal vs. real mental accounting)
experiment: Survey questions present scenarios with price or inflation variation. Respondents' mental representations and evaluations are probed. Responses are analyzed to infer whether people think in nominal vs. real terms and how this bias emerges. The study reviews existing survey data and proposes that money illusion arises from parallel nominal and real representations, with interaction bias. Conceptual analysis is combined with empirical examination of response patterns.

what_manipulated: task context (cognitive control paradigm vs. sequential choice task); individual differences in cognitive control capacity
what_measured: model-based vs. model-free reinforcement learning behavior; individual differences in goal-directed choice
experiment: Units complete established cognitive control tasks (measuring utilization of goal-related context) and a separate sequential choice task (measuring model-based vs. model-free choice). Individual differences in cognitive control predict model-based behavior in the RL task. Behavioral correspondence between the two tasks is tested. Analysis infers whether common computational mechanisms underpin controlled behavior and model-based choice.

what_manipulated: compensation scheme (piece-rate non-competitive vs. tournament competitive incentive scheme); selection opportunity
what_measured: choice of compensation scheme (entry into tournament vs. remaining in piece-rate)
experiment: Units perform a real task under piece-rate compensation, then under tournament compensation. Both conditions are experienced. Units then choose their preferred compensation scheme for a final performance round. Gender differences in tournament entry are the primary outcome. Comparisons between genders examine entry rates. Performance and alternative mediators (risk aversion, feedback aversion, overconfidence, preference for competition) are measured to explain the gender entry gap.

what_manipulated: agent psychological state vs. moral status of behavior (in vignette scenarios); cultural context (cross-cultural study)
what_measured: concept application (intention vs. intentional action labels; judgment of whether behavior is intentional)
experiment: Units (from different cultures) read vignettes describing agent behavior varying in psychological states and moral status. Units judge whether the agent acted with intention and whether the action is intentional. Conceptual analysis identifies how psychological state and moral status predict concept application. Cross-cultural comparisons reveal universality or variation in folk concepts. Methods include behavioral experiments and linguistic analysis of concept use in multiple languages.

what_manipulated: contextual factors affecting agency and responsibility judgments (varying vignette or scenario details)
what_measured: folk intuitions about agency, determinism, and moral responsibility
experiment: Units respond to different scenarios or vignettes designed to elicit agency and responsibility judgments in varied contexts. Responses reveal whether people treat agency as indeterminist or determinist and whether they view responsibility as compatible or incompatible with determinism. Comparisons across conditions show context dependence of these judgments. Experimental methods identify psychological mechanisms underlying conflicting folk intuitions.

what_manipulated: capability signal presented in a job candidate profile (high-capability vs. adequate low-capability)
what_measured: hiring manager perception of candidate commitment and hiring decision
experiment: Four experiments present hiring managers with job candidate profiles varying in capability signals. Managers rate candidate commitment to the organization and make hiring decisions. Comparisons between high-capability and low-capability candidates reveal perceived commitment differences and selection penalty. Proposed mediators (perceived organizational interest, flight risk due to outside options) are measured. Analysis identifies when and why capability signals reduce hiring probability despite being relevant to job performance.

what_manipulated: categorization by latent class based on knowledge and attitudes about science, religiosity, and religious interpretation preferences
what_measured: attitudes about political issues (abortion rights, stem cell research) and preferences for scientific versus religious understanding
experiment: Units are drawn from a large population survey (General Social Survey). Latent class analysis identifies three groups based on observed attitudes and knowledge. Regression models assess how group membership predicts attitudes on specific political issues. The analysis does not manipulate variables but classifies units post-hoc and examines correlations.

what_manipulated: respondent location relative to party position on left-right spectrum; policy distance between respondent and each party
what_measured: valence judgment (numerical rating of party quality or appeal)
experiment: Units are drawn from a large survey database (Comparative Study of Electoral Systems). Regression analysis examines how policy distance and location relative to spectrum center predict valence scores. Differential item functioning is corrected to account for systematic rating differences. No experimental manipulation; associations are measured across multiple surveys.

what_manipulated: subnational political regime type (competitive versus dominant-party system); regional economic performance
what_measured: voting calculus (influence of economic performance on incumbent support in provincial and national elections)
experiment: Units are voters in regions with different regime characteristics. Analysis compares whether economic voting patterns differ between competitive and dominant-party subnational contexts in Argentina and Mexico. The design leverages natural variation in regime characteristics within countries rather than experimental assignment.

what_manipulated: exposure to visual viewpoint from high versus low vertical position
what_measured: risk-taking behavior (choices or actions in risky scenarios)
experiment: Units are randomly assigned to view scenery from high or low elevation perspectives in laboratory and real settings. Studies 1-3 examine the effect in controlled settings; Study 4 uses real-world observations. A mediation analysis (Study 2) tests whether illusory sense of control explains the elevation effect. Study 3 tests whether mental construal level moderates the effect.

what_manipulated: ad structure: before/after presentation versus progression (intermediate steps); consumer goal (quick results versus change acceptance); ability to achieve results quickly
what_measured: perceived credibility of ad and persuasion (purchase intention or belief change)
experiment: Units are exposed to different ad structures and consumer goals are manipulated or measured. Studies 1-3 test whether progression ads increase process simulation and credibility. Studies 4-5 test moderators (simulation impairment and skepticism). Studies 6-7 test reversals when fast results are desired and possible. Effects are measured on perceived credibility and persuasion outcomes.

what_manipulated: price presentation mode: stimulus-based (both prices visible) versus memory-based (one or both prices retrieved from memory)
what_measured: magnitude of left-digit bias in price comparison (perceived difference between prices)
experiment: Units are participants in five experiments and a scanner panel study. Stimulus-based conditions show prices side-by-side; memory-based conditions require recall. The left-digit bias magnitude is measured by comparing perceived difference to actual numeric difference. Price representations (perceptual vs. conceptual) are theorized to mediate the effect.

what_manipulated: source type (personal: friends/family versus impersonal: organization); perceived source credibility
what_measured: willingness to engage in connective-type collective activities (commenting, liking, sharing on social media)
experiment: Units (N=208) receive messages from personal or impersonal sources. Cognitive elaboration and perceived source credibility are measured. Mediation analysis tests whether elaboration mediates the effect of source type on engagement, with source credibility as a moderator. Direct effects of personal involvement and perceived efficacy are also examined.

what_manipulated: reference group for bias comparison (self versus average American versus classroom peers versus airport travelers); exposure to bias description
what_measured: perceived susceptibility to cognitive and motivational biases (self-rated judgment compared to others)
experiment: Study 1 surveys units comparing self-ratings to various reference groups. Study 2 tests whether exposure to bias descriptions changes self-assessments. Study 3 examines whether participants maintain claims of accuracy after bias education. Study 4 compares self-serving attributions in self versus peer evaluations. The consistent finding is greater perceived bias in others than self.

what_manipulated: threat context (realistic threat condition versus control); stereotype content (positive model-minority attributes versus other positive attributes)
what_measured: attitudes and emotions toward a target group
experiment: Study 1: units are assigned to read group descriptions with model-minority or alternative positive attributes and rate attitudes in realistic threat context. Study 2: measures threat and stereotypes as predictors of attitudes. Studies 3-4: replicate effect in experimental interactions with units from target group. Mediation analysis tests whether threat explains the stereotype-to-attitude relationship.

what_manipulated: language concreteness (concrete versus abstract versions of identical statements); mental construal level (concrete versus abstract prime); spatial proximity of statement presentation (near versus far)
what_measured: judgment of statement truth (probability rating)
experiment: Experiment 1-2: units judge truth of statements written in concrete or abstract language; vividness is measured as a mechanism. Experiment 3: units are primed with concrete or abstract mind-set before judging truth. Experiment 4: spatial distance is manipulated (proximal versus distant presentation). Language concreteness and construal level fit produce additive effects on truth judgments.

what_manipulated: retrospective report of intentions (inferred from post-hoc questionnaire); experimental manipulation of intention to rate primes versus targets; option to pass on trials where influenced
what_measured: prime-to-target affect transfer effect size and reliability in the affect misattribution procedure
experiment: Experiment 1: larger effect sizes correlate with retrospective reports of both intentional priming and unintentional influence, indicating confabulation. Experiment 2: direct manipulation of instruction to rate primes versus targets produces systematic effect differences. Experiment 3: option to pass does not reduce priming. Manipulations (not retrospective reports) reveal whether participants genuinely intend to rate primes.

what_manipulated: cultural context (Asian American versus European American); parental pressure; mother-child interdependence norms
what_measured: academic motivation following failure; perceived relationship strain with mother; achievement outcomes
experiment: Units are high school students from Asian American or European American contexts. Baseline interdependence and pressure are measured. Following failure feedback, motivation is assessed. Analysis tests whether pressure strains relationships differently across cultural contexts and whether AAs are particularly motivated by pressure conveying interdependence. No random assignment; comparison is between naturally occurring cultural groups.

what_manipulated: construal level (high versus low); perceived changeability of feedback domain (changeable versus fixed)
what_measured: acceptance or dismissal of negative diagnostic feedback; motivation for self-change versus self-protection
experiment: Four studies examine how construal level and changeability jointly determine feedback acceptance. Low-level construal promotes short-term self-protection (dismissal). High-level construal promotes long-term self-change (acceptance), but only when change is perceived as possible. Underlying cognitive and motivational mechanisms are examined. Units are exposed to negative feedback under different construal and changeability conditions.

what_manipulated: causal explanation type (genetic versus environmental) for criminal behavior
what_measured: perceived control of perpetrator; perceived internal causality; expectation of reoffending; sentencing decision
experiment: Three studies (N=600 total) assign units to read case descriptions with genetic or environmental explanations. Mediation and path analysis reveal that genetic explanations simultaneously reduce perceived control (defensive) and increase internal attribution and reoffending expectation (harmful). These countervailing mechanisms predict sentencing in opposite directions, yielding no net effect on final verdict.

what_manipulated: cultural context (Westerners versus Easterners); task type (eager versus vigilant); outcome type (independent versus interdependent)
what_measured: metamotivational beliefs about whether promotion or prevention motivation benefits task performance
experiment: Units from different cultural backgrounds rate how well different motivations fit different tasks. Analysis tests whether beliefs about task-motivation fit generalize across cultures and whether prevention bias (rather than promotion bias) emerges in Eastern contexts. Beliefs about fit for independent and interdependent outcomes are compared across cultures.

what_manipulated: anchor value at predetermined percentile (15th versus 85th percentile of calibration distribution); judgment task (whether anchor is higher or lower than true value)
what_measured: estimation accuracy and anchoring effect size (transformed into percentile position in calibration group)
experiment: A calibration group provides estimates of uncertain quantities. Anchored units judge whether an anchor value is higher or lower than the true estimate, then estimate the quantity. The procedure allows transformation of estimates into percentiles and pooling across problems. Anchoring-like bias in the initial anchor judgment and confidence are examined.

what_manipulated: generic type (masculine versus other types) in German language descriptions
what_measured: cognitive retrieval of male and female exemplars from memory (response to prompt to name favorite example in category)
experiment: Native speakers of German are presented with different generic forms and asked to retrieve and name exemplars. Memory retrieval is compared across generic types. The study measures whether linguistic structure influences which gender exemplars are retrieved.

what_manipulated: caloric drink intake (sugar-sweetened versus artificially sweetened soft drink); blood glucose levels as measured biomarker
what_measured: future discounting rate (preference for smaller-sooner versus larger-later monetary rewards)
experiment: Units are randomly assigned to consume sugar-sweetened or artificially sweetened drinks. Blood glucose is measured as a biomarker of body-energy budget. Units then make intertemporal choices between rewards. Discounting rates are compared across groups while controlling for sex, age, BMI, and taste. The analysis examines whether blood glucose regulates choice as a function of metabolic state.

what_manipulated: activation of good-luck superstition via saying (e.g., 'break a leg'), action (fingers crossed), or lucky charm versus control condition
what_measured: performance on task (golf, motor dexterity, memory, anagram solving); perceived self-efficacy; task persistence
experiment: Experiments 1-4 randomly assign units to superstition-activation or control and measure subsequent task performance. Mediation analysis (Experiments 3-4) tests whether perceived self-efficacy mediates the effect. Experiment 4 examines task persistence as a mechanism by which self-efficacy improves performance. Studies span golfing, motor dexterity, memory, and anagrams.

what_manipulated: grammatical framing of behavior descriptions (imperfective vs. perfective aspect)
what_measured: attribution of intentionality to a person
experiment: Participants read descriptions of a person's behavior framed in one of two grammatical aspects and rated the person's intentionality. The study compared attribution levels between groups receiving imperfective vs. perfective descriptions. A mediation analysis examined whether imagined action detail explained the effect.

what_manipulated: presence of ensemble statistical structure (mean size) across items
what_measured: remembered size of individual items in a display
experiment: Participants studied colored-circle displays and recalled item sizes. The analysis examined whether recalled sizes were biased toward ensemble statistics (mean color-group size and overall mean). Multiple displays were tested to assess encoding at different levels of abstraction.

what_manipulated: taste perception (sweet, bitter, or neutral control beverage)
what_measured: moral judgment severity regarding transgressions
experiment: Participants consumed one of three beverages and then rated moral transgressions. The study examined main effects of taste and tested moderation by political ideology. Taste effects on moral disgust were compared across beverage conditions.

what_manipulated: reminder of physical cleansing (purity cue vs. control)
what_measured: moral judgment of sexual-domain violations and political attitudes
experiment: In Study 1, participants in public settings received a purity cue or no cue and reported political attitudes. In Study 2, lab participants were reminded of cleansing or received a control and rated sexual transgressions and reported political attitudes. Comparisons assessed cue effects on specific and broad moral judgments.

what_manipulated: mortality prime (death thought vs. control) crossed with cultural self-construal group
what_measured: attitudes toward norm violators and attributions about victims
experiment: Participants from two cultural backgrounds received a mortality or control prime. Study 1 measured attitudes toward a norm violator; Study 2 measured victim attributions. The analysis tested whether mortality effects diverged by cultural group.

what_manipulated: brief exposure to American flag (flag vs. control visual prime)
what_measured: voting intentions, voting behavior, political beliefs, and explicit and implicit attitudes
experiment: Participants received a flag prime or control in online (Experiment 1) and lab (Experiment 2) settings. Voting intentions and behavior were tracked; some effects were assessed at 8-month follow-up. Political beliefs were measured immediately post-manipulation.

what_manipulated: intensity of negative emotional context (low vs. high via images or stimulation)
what_measured: choice between reappraisal and distraction as emotion-regulation strategies
experiment: Participants were exposed to emotional contexts of varying intensity (Experiments 1-2 using pictures; Experiment 3 using unpredictable stimulation) and chose between two emotion-regulation strategies. Choices were recorded across intensity levels.

what_manipulated: dominance expression (dominant vs. communal behavior) crossed with leader race and gender
what_measured: status conferred and backlash against the leader
experiment: Participants read scenarios or watched leaders displaying dominant or communal behaviors and rated leader status. The study tested whether race moderated the well-established gender-dominance backlash effect by comparing status conferral across race-gender-dominance combinations.

what_manipulated: sociometric status (face-to-face respect and admiration within groups)
what_measured: subjective well-being (life satisfaction, positive and negative emotions)
experiment: Four studies used correlational, experimental, and longitudinal methods. Participants reported or were assigned sociometric status in groups; well-being was measured concurrently or at follow-up. Mediation by feelings of power and acceptance was tested.

what_manipulated: activation of choice concept (emphasis on choice vs. control condition)
what_measured: acceptance of wealth inequality, underestimation of societal factors, and policy support
experiment: Six experiments manipulated choice salience and measured subsequent judgments about wealth inequality. Participants rated existing inequality facts, estimated societal influences on success, and indicated support for redistribution policies. Between-condition comparisons assessed the effect of choice activation.

what_manipulated: moral framing of environmental discourse (harm/care vs. purity framing)
what_measured: environmental attitudes and their relationship to political ideology
experiment: Studies 1a-1b correlated moral values and ideology with environmental attitudes. Studies 2a-2b analyzed environmental messaging in media for moral content. Study 3 experimentally reframed pro-environmental rhetoric in purity terms and measured ideology effects. Ideology was a moderator examined across studies.

what_manipulated: context of cognitive testing (memory test, vocabulary test, or no test; expectation of memory test)
what_measured: subjective age (how old participants feel)
experiment: Older adults completed a baseline subjective-age rating, participated in a memory or control task, and re-rated subjective age. Younger adults provided a comparison group. The effects of task type and expectancy were examined.

what_manipulated: product price (expensive vs. cheap) shown in sexual vs. neutral advertisements
what_measured: tolerance for sexual imagery and emotional reactions to sexual advertisements
experiment: Two experiments presented ads pairing sexual or neutral scenes with expensive or cheap products. Participants (women and men) rated sexual imagery and emotional reactions. The analysis tested whether product price moderated gender differences in sexual-imagery reactions.

what_manipulated: salience of racial progress (chronic exposure or experimental prime) crossed with beliefs about status-hierarchy legitimacy
what_measured: perceived anti-White discrimination and victimhood claims
experiment: Study 1 measured chronic salience of racial progress and legitimacy beliefs, then perceived bias. Study 2 experimentally manipulated racial-progress salience and measured bias perception. Study 3 examined self-affirmation as a mechanism. Moderation by legitimacy beliefs and mediation by threat were tested.

what_manipulated: salience of changing national or state racial demographics (majority-minority shift vs. control)
what_measured: political-party leaning and political ideology (conservatism)
experiment: Study 1 made California's demographic shift salient to unaffiliated White participants and measured party lean. Studies 2-3 primed national demographic change and measured ideology across party-affiliated participants. Moderation by group-status threat was examined as a mechanism.

what_manipulated: free-will beliefs (measured or experimentally reduced via neuroscience education)
what_measured: endorsement of retributive vs. consequentialist punishment attitudes
experiment: Study 1 measured free-will beliefs and punishment attitudes. Studies 2-4 reduced free-will beliefs via lab manipulation or course enrollment, then measured punishment attitudes. Comparisons examined effects of free-will reduction on retributive but not consequentialist attitudes.

what_manipulated: assigned hierarchical rank (high vs. low)
what_measured: acoustic properties of speech (pitch, pitch variability, loudness, loudness variability) and perceivers' inferences of rank
experiment: Experiment 1 assigned participants to high- or low-rank roles; their speech was recorded and acoustic properties measured. Experiment 2 used recordings to examine whether perceivers used acoustic cues to infer rank. Acoustic properties were examined as mediators of rank effects.

what_manipulated: presence of weak negotiation alternatives vs. no alternatives (affecting anchoring environment)
what_measured: first offers made and final negotiation outcomes
experiment: Negotiators with weak alternatives, strong alternatives, or no alternatives made first offers in a structured negotiation. Final outcomes were recorded. Mediation by first offers and moderation by focus on countervailing anchors were tested.

what_manipulated: exposure to God concept (reminders vs. control) crossed with perceived divine protection beliefs
what_measured: willingness to engage in risky behavior in nonmoral domains
experiment: Seven studies presented reminders of God or control and measured risk-taking choices across domains. Mediation by perceived danger and moderation by belief in divine protection were tested. Study 8 examined emotional reactions when divine protection failed.

what_manipulated: presence or salience of resource scarcity as a contextual frame
what_measured: magnitude of preference shift or context-effect susceptibility in valuation
experiment: Units are exposed to decision scenarios under conditions of either high or low resource scarcity. Their value judgments and choices are recorded. The analysis examines whether context effects (e.g., anchoring or framing) exert less influence when scarcity is salient, compared to control conditions. The comparison is between scarcity-present and scarcity-absent contexts. Statistical tests assess the interaction between scarcity framing and the magnitude of classical context-effect phenomena.

what_manipulated: instructions to suppress versus recall episodic memories of aversive scenes
what_measured: accuracy of memory suppression and subsequent involuntary retrieval of suppressed items
experiment: Units are trained on a think-no-think paradigm where they learn to either retrieve or suppress memories of aversive scenes when presented with retrieval cues. Performance is measured via recognition tests for suppressed versus rehearsed items. Groups include PTSD patients and trauma-exposed controls. The comparison examines suppression-induced forgetting (difference between suppressed and control items) and its correlation with symptom severity. Analysis tests whether inhibitory control mechanisms differ between groups.

what_manipulated: engagement in self-promotional behavior (claiming positive attributes) versus control baseline
what_measured: recipients' inferences about self-promoters' likeability and judgments of bragging or modesty
experiment: Units (self-promoters) communicate positive self-information to recipients under various conditions. Recipients rate their impressions via scales of likeability, perceived bragging, and emotional reactions (pride, annoyance). The comparison is between self-promotion conditions and control conditions. Separate experiments test self-promoters' predictions of recipient reactions (Experiments 1-2) and recipients' actual reactions (Experiments 3). A mismatch between predicted and actual recipient affect is analyzed as a projection bias mechanism.

what_manipulated: none (observational study examining baseline life satisfaction levels and their variability over time)
what_measured: mortality risk over 9-year follow-up period
experiment: Units are a sample of older adults (n=4,458, initially age ≥50) tracked for mortality over 9 years (546 deaths). Life satisfaction is measured repeatedly across time, yielding both a mean (average level) and a variability measure (fluctuation across occasions). Cox proportional hazards models are fit predicting time-to-death from mean satisfaction, variability, and their interaction, controlling for age. The effect structure is examined as an interaction, testing whether the protective effect of mean satisfaction is modified by within-person variability.

what_manipulated: domain-specific self-perceived expertise (e.g., boosted via instruction or measured as a trait)
what_measured: likelihood of claiming knowledge of nonexistent or fictitious concepts within that domain
experiment: Units are presented with lists of concepts, some real and some fictitious, within a domain (e.g., finance, biology, geography). They report their familiarity with each item. Self-perceived expertise is assessed via questionnaire (Studies 1b, 2) or experimentally boosted (Study 4). The comparison measures whether units with higher self-perceived expertise more frequently claim knowledge of nonexistent items (overclaiming). Analysis involves correlational and causal designs. Study 3 introduces a warning about fictional items to test whether impression management mediates overclaiming.

what_manipulated: gender of the creative person and/or gender-stereotypical characteristics of their behavior
what_measured: attributed creativity and inferred agency / agentic capability
experiment: Units are presented with creative outputs or descriptions of creative behavior, manipulating the agent's gender (Studies 2-5) or analyzing archival data on idea evaluations (Study 3). Ratings of creativity and related constructs (agency, competence) are collected. Studies 1 measures associations between creativity and masculine vs. feminine traits. Study 2 presents identical output from male vs. female agents. Studies 3-4 use archival data (idea evaluations, supervisor ratings of executives). Study 5 varies whether creative behavior is stereotypically masculine, testing whether the behavior-creativity link differs by agent gender. Mediation analysis (agency vs. competence) is conducted.

what_manipulated: salience of opportunity costs at the time of choice (explicit vs. implicit presentation of forgone alternatives)
what_measured: post-choice spread (difference in evaluation) between chosen items and opportunity costs
experiment: Units make choices among items in three experiments where opportunity costs (best forgone alternatives) are either explicitly presented or remain implicit. Post-choice evaluations of chosen items, focal alternatives, and opportunity costs are collected. The comparison examines whether explicit vs. implicit opportunity cost salience alters the magnitude of post-choice preference change (spread between chosen and unchosen items). Analysis tests whether making opportunity costs salient at choice time influences subsequent preference evaluations.

what_manipulated: type of linguistic intergroup bias (LIB) used by a speaker when describing a target (favorable vs. unfavorable bias pattern)
what_measured: perceived social-group membership overlap between speaker and target
experiment: Units read or hear a description of a target from a speaker, where the linguistic style indicates either favorable LIB (abstract positive, concrete negative behaviors) or unfavorable LIB (concrete positive, abstract negative behaviors). Participants infer whether the speaker and target share a social identity. Four studies use vignettes or naturalistic language samples. Analysis compares inference rates and confidence between favorable vs. unfavorable LIB conditions. The reversal test examines whether language patterns that signal in-group membership to the listener are indeed predicted by actual speaker-target group membership.

what_manipulated: presence and salience of Gestalt properties (connectedness, closure, continuity) in visual patterns, independent of expertise
what_measured: magnitude of holistic processing (e.g., part-to-whole interference, composite effects) in recognition or discrimination
experiment: Units view line patterns or shapes that vary in the saliency of Gestalt information (high vs. low connectedness, closure, continuity). Holistic processing is measured via tasks such as composite effects (difficulty ignoring the whole when processing parts) or part-to-whole interactions. Comparisons are made between high-Gestalt and low-Gestalt patterns, and between novel patterns and faces or expertise domains. Analysis tests whether patterns with salient Gestalt properties elicit facelike holistic processing without explicit training or expertise development. Reaction times and errors are recorded.

what_manipulated: race and age of briefly presented faces (Black vs. White; adult vs. child) preceding threat-categorization tasks
what_measured: speed and accuracy of identifying threatening versus nonthreatening objects or words
experiment: Units complete sequential priming tasks where faces of varying race (Black/White) and age (adult/child) are briefly presented, followed by categorization of threatening or nonthreatening stimuli. Response times and errors are recorded. The comparison examines whether Black faces facilitate threat detection (faster/fewer errors for threats) and inhibit nonthreat detection, compared to White faces. Process-dissociation-procedure analyses separate automatic from controlled influences. All four experiments use the same basic design. Analyses test whether the racial effect depends on target age (child vs. adult).

what_manipulated: economic insecurity (induced via unemployment, state-level cues, or priming) or manipulation of perceived control
what_measured: physical pain intensity/threshold or consumption of over-the-counter painkillers
experiment: Six experiments and one large observational study (n=33,720 households). Units are either experimentally exposed to economic insecurity (Studies 1-5: priming, layoff framing, unemployment experience, state unemployment context, past/future insecurity), or observed for painkiller consumption relative to economic insecurity indicators. Outcome measures include pain ratings (subjective), pain-tolerance tasks (behavioral threshold), or painkiller consumption (archival). Mediation analyses test loss-of-control as the psychological mechanism linking insecurity to pain. Meta-analytic integration across all studies testing the insecurity-pain link estimates overall effect magnitude.

what_manipulated: parents' implicit views of failure as debilitating versus enhancing, and communication of these views to children
what_measured: children's intelligence mind-sets (fixed vs. growth beliefs) and inferences of parental views
experiment: Multiple studies across four experiments link parental failure mind-sets (assessed via questionnaire) to parenting practices and children's subsequent intelligence mind-sets. Study 1 tests the correlational chain (parent failure view → parenting behavior → child intelligence belief). Study 2 details parenting practices that manifest failure mind-sets. Study 3a and 3b test whether children perceive and can report parents' failure views (vs. intelligence views) and whether perceived failure views predict children's intelligence beliefs. Study 4 uses a behavioral task (hypothetical failure scenario) to show that failure mind-sets causally influence parents' responses. Analysis examines both correlational and causal pathways.

what_manipulated: type of substitute offered (within-category vs. cross-category alternative to a desired stimulus) and relative quality of the substitute
what_measured: subjective satisfaction or perceived effectiveness in satisfying cravings for the desired stimulus
experiment: Units are asked to imagine or experience wanting a specific item (e.g., gourmet chocolate) but having access only to a substitute (e.g., store-brand chocolate [within-category] or granola bar [cross-category]). Experiments 1-2 measure predicted satisfaction; Experiments 3-4 measure actual consumption satisfaction. Units rate how well substitutes satisfy cravings. Quality of substitutes is varied (equivalent vs. inferior to the desired item). Comparisons test whether within-category substitutes, despite being more similar, produce a contrast effect (less satisfaction) unless quality-matched, whereas cross-category substitutes avoid this contrast penalty.

what_manipulated: observed regularities (typical patterns or norms) that units must explain and reason about
what_measured: tendency to infer moral goodness or appropriateness from descriptions of what is typical
experiment: Five studies with children and adults examine explanation-based reasoning about social regularities (e.g., giving roses on Valentine's Day). Units explain why observed patterns exist and then judge whether those patterns are good/appropriate ('ought'). Explanations are coded for inherence bias (attributing patterns to intrinsic properties vs. extrinsic/relational reasons). The comparison tests whether inherence-biased explanations (e.g., 'roses are beautiful') predict downstream value judgments more strongly than extrinsic explanations. Developmental analysis examines whether the bias emerges early and persists.

what_manipulated: relative ordinal rank of options at encoding versus relative rank at subsequent judgment (distribution change)
what_measured: reliance on original encoded rank versus updating to new absolute values or new distributions
experiment: Units encode and evaluate options (items, values, or stimuli) within an initial distribution (e.g., prices in a set). Later, they make judgments about these items either from memory in a new distribution context or in a changed context. Three experiments test whether memory-based judgments preserve the original ordinal ranking (relative standing at encoding) or incorporate information about absolute quality or new distributional context. Analysis compares accuracy against benchmarks of absolute judgment or updated relative judgment, revealing whether units rely on original encoding rank despite distributional changes.

what_manipulated: availability and accessibility of scientific explanations (information that scientists understand a phenomenon vs. scientists do not yet understand it)
what_measured: self-reported understanding of the phenomenon
experiment: Four experiments test whether units overestimate their own understanding when scientists understand a phenomenon. Units are told either that scientists understand or do not understand a natural phenomenon, then rate their own understanding. Experiment 2 varies whether explanatory information is ostensibly accessible (presented visibly) versus secret (held by scientists). Experiments 3 and 4 rule out alternative explanations involving task demands or pragmatic inference. Units are not given actual explanatory details. Analysis tests the hypothesis that people confound access to others' knowledge with their own understanding (community-of-knowledge hypothesis).

what_manipulated: task framing (spatial ability vs. empathetic/social ability) and inclusion of human figures in spatial perspective-taking test items
what_measured: accuracy on perspective-taking (rotation or navigation) tasks
experiment: Units complete perspective-taking tasks (e.g., mental rotation, viewpoint change) under different framings: (1) spatial condition (framed as spatial ability; males advantaged stereotype), (2) social condition (framed as empathy; females advantaged stereotype; items include human figures). Experiment 1 measures performance differences. Experiments 2 and 3 manipulate either stereotype threat induction or human-figure inclusion separately. Sex differences are examined as a function of framing, threat salience, and visual content. Task difficulty and gender effects are compared across conditions.

what_manipulated: felt power (high vs. low) and valence of reaction to a judgment object (ambivalent vs. univalent)
what_measured: action orientation or likelihood of taking action (e.g., deciding to promote or fire an employee)
experiment: Two experiments test an interaction between power and ambivalence on action tendency. Power is manipulated (e.g., via role-play or recall). Participants hold either univalent judgments (entirely positive or negative) or ambivalent judgments (mixed positive and negative reactions) about a decision target. They indicate their action tendency (promote, fire, or abstain). Analysis tests whether power strengthens whatever reaction is present: strengthening univalent reactions (increasing action), but among ambivalent reactors, power increases inaction because it validates and crystallizes the internal conflict. The two experiments test positive and negative decision contexts separately.

what_manipulated: none (observational study of mental representations and attitudes)
what_measured: attitudes toward welfare policy (support for giving benefits) and perceived attributes of welfare recipients
experiment: Studies 1-2 use a perceptual task where units estimate the average/prototypical image of welfare recipients versus non-recipients via morphing or adjustment. Study 3 takes the average images and tests whether separate raters are more or less supportive of giving benefits to the person shown in each image. Analysis examines whether the welfare-recipient image is perceived as more African American and stereotypical, and whether support for welfare is lower for this image. Archival or perceptual data measure mental representations; separate samples provide policy judgments. The logic tests whether stereotypical mental images drive policy opposition.

what_manipulated: whether a person condemns an immoral behavior and whether they privately engage in the condemned act (creating true vs. hypocritical positions), and whether they admit to the transgression
what_measured: negativity of judgment toward the person and perceived deceptiveness or false signaling
experiment: Multiple experiments test a false-signaling theory. Study 1 shows that verbal condemnation signals moral goodness. Study 2 compares condemnation vs. direct claims of morality. Study 3 presents scenarios where people either condemn and engage (hypocrite), condemn and abstain (truthful), or claim morality falsely. Study 4 tests 'honest hypocrites' who admit to the condemned transgression. Study 5 tests hypocrites who admit to unrelated transgressions. Judgments of likeability, immorality, and deceptiveness are collected. Analysis tests whether negative judgment tracks false signaling (conflicting messages about behavior) rather than mere action-statement contradiction.

what_manipulated: race of observed targets (Black vs White)
what_measured: accuracy of lie-versus-truth judgments
experiment: Units viewed videos of targets telling truths or lies about relationships. Units made binary judgments of truthfulness. The study examined whether accuracy rates differed by target race. Eye-tracking recorded response latencies. Mediation analysis tested whether motivation to avoid prejudice explained any difference in judgment accuracy. Comparisons used standard hypothesis tests.

what_manipulated: membership group status (out-group vs in-group) and health status (healthy vs infected appearance)
what_measured: whether units confuse out-group and infected in-group category membership during recall
experiment: Units participated in a memory-confusion task with large samples (nationally representative). They studied faces or descriptions with group membership labels, then attempted recognition or classification. The analysis tested whether errors of confusing out-group members with infected in-group members exceeded chance. Statistical tests evaluated the overlap in psychological categorization.

what_manipulated: novelty of group and valence of unique attributes (negative vs positive attributes)
what_measured: preference rating for groups
experiment: Units formed impressions of two fictional groups presented sequentially. The first group was familiar; the second was novel. Both groups were described with equal numbers of positive and negative attributes. Unique (differentiating) attributes were systematically varied as negative or positive. Units rated preference for each group. Comparisons examined preference differences depending on whether unique attributes were negative.

what_manipulated: immoral behavior (performed vs not performed) and self-dehumanization induction (present vs absent)
what_measured: self-reported humanness (Study 1) and subsequently immoral behavior (Study 2)
experiment: Studies 1a-1d: Units performed unethical tasks or control tasks, then rated their sense of humanness. Study 1d controlled for negative affect. Studies 2a-2c: Units were induced to self-dehumanize or serve as controls, then engaged in tasks measuring dishonesty or antisocial behavior. Study 3 examined a within-unit chain: unethical behavior leading to dehumanization leading to continued dishonesty. Comparisons used standard tests of group differences and chain-of-causation models.

what_manipulated: free-riding behavior (present vs absent) and intentionality (intentional vs unintentional noncontribution)
what_measured: evaluation (negativity) and sanctioning (punishment likelihood) of targets
experiment: Units (children and adults) observed others in resource-pooling scenarios. Actors either contributed fairly or free-rode. Noncontribution was varied as intentional or unintentional. Units rated their evaluation of the actor and willingness to punish. Comparisons examined main effects of free-riding, moderation by intentionality, and equivalence across first-party and third-party judgments. Possible confounds (nonconformity, group harm) were tested in separate conditions.

what_manipulated: implicit theory of interest (fixed vs growth mindset, assessed or induced)
what_measured: interest levels in new domains and persistence when difficulty arises
experiment: Studies 1-3: Units completed questionnaires measuring implicit theories and interest in unfamiliar domains, or were randomly assigned to fixed or growth mindset inductions before exploring new interests. Interest was measured by self-report. Studies 4-5 examined anticipations (Study 4: expected motivation when passion is found) and behavioral persistence (Study 5: continued engagement when a new interest becomes difficult). Comparisons used correlational analysis and experimental contrasts.

what_manipulated: actual corruption status of government officials (convicted vs clean records) and face characteristics (face width via digital manipulation)
what_measured: trait ratings (corruptibility, dishonesty, etc.) based on facial appearance
experiment: Studies 1-3 (preregistered): Units viewed photos of government officials with known corruption convictions or clean records, rating perceived traits. Mediation analysis tested whether perceived corruptibility predicted actual corruption. Study 4: Photos were digitally manipulated to vary face width. Units rated traits. Comparisons examined whether manipulated face width causally influenced corruptibility ratings and mediated the link between appearance and actual behavior.

what_manipulated: judgment target (self vs other) and base-rate relevance (morally permitted vs statistically rational)
what_measured: moral criticism and punishment (money shared, financial costs incurred) of the judgment maker
experiment: Units observed a judgment maker apply different base rates when evaluating members of different groups. The same judgment was presented as made by another person (criticized condition) or by the unit themselves (self condition). Units rated the morality and intelligence of the judgment, allocated money to the judge, and chose whether to incur costs to punish the judge. Comparisons examined whether units' own judgments matched the structure they criticized, using both self-report and behavioral measures.

what_manipulated: grammaticality of base sequence and adjacency of transposed words
what_measured: reaction time and accuracy in grammaticality judgments
experiment: Units made speeded grammaticality judgments on five-word sequences. Test sequences were created by transposing two adjacent words from either grammatical or ungrammatical base sentences. Equal numbers of correct sentences were intermixed. Correct rejection of ungrammatical sequences was compared between sequences derived from grammatical vs ungrammatical bases. Analysis examined reaction times and error rates using standard hypothesis tests.

what_manipulated: moral versus nonmoral appeal and initial attitude moralness (survey responses or study population)
what_measured: persuasiveness (attitude change or persuasion rating)
experiment: Two studies examined whether moral appeals were more persuasive to units with moralized attitudes. Units reported their initial attitudes toward an issue and the moral basis of those attitudes. They then received a counterattitudinal message framed as either moral or nonmoral. Persuasiveness was measured as attitude change or rated persuasiveness. Mediation analysis examined whether valenced thoughts about the message explained the effect. Moderation analysis tested whether political orientation changed the pattern.

what_manipulated: type of consensus (derived from single primary source vs multiple independent sources) and transparency of source dependence
what_measured: confidence in and believability of a conclusion
experiment: Four experiments presented units with information purporting to show consensus on a topic. Consensus could derive from one primary source (false consensus, all secondary sources cite the same origin) or multiple independent primary sources (true consensus). Units rated confidence in conclusions and believability. The studies tested whether units distinguished between these consensus types and whether explicit prior instruction about their difference altered judgments.

what_manipulated: age group (4-year-olds, 6-year-olds, young adults)
what_measured: holistic recollection (dependency in retrieval of multiple aspects of a single event)
experiment: Units (children and young adults) studied complex events, then attempted retrieval of various aspects (actors, locations, actions, etc.). Dependency analyses examined whether retrieval success for one aspect predicted retrieval success for other aspects of the same event. The strength of this dependency was compared across age groups.

what_manipulated: pseudoword phonetic properties (kiki-like vs bouba-like) and shape properties (spiky vs rounded)
what_measured: affective arousal ratings and sound-shape matching behavior
experiment: Three experiments examined the role of emotional arousal in sound-shape associations. Study 1-2: Units rated pseudowords and shapes for affective arousal using subjective ratings and acoustic models. Study 3 (preregistered): Units completed sound-shape matching tasks with novel pseudowords while arousal was measured. Mediation analysis tested whether arousal mediated the association between phonetic properties and matching behavior.

what_manipulated: whether lottery choice was made by unit or selected by experimenter
what_measured: curiosity (self-report ratings and willingness-to-wait behavior)
experiment: Units were presented with two lotteries on each trial. On some trials, units chose which to play; on others, selection was made for them. After a lottery was selected (regardless of source), units reported curiosity about the outcome or indicated willingness to wait for information. Comparisons examined curiosity differences between chosen and assigned lotteries, controlling for initial preference.

what_manipulated: not applicable (questionnaire development and validation study)
what_measured: safety behaviors in test anxiety (superstitious behaviors and reassurance seeking)
experiment: Three samples: interviews with units generated items; exploratory sample (N=301) completed 33-item measure; confirmatory sample (N=151) completed 19-item version and provided academic records. Item analysis and exploratory factor analysis identified item pool. Confirmatory factor analysis validated the structure. Correlational analyses examined criterion validity (relation to test anxiety and performance). Internal consistency and convergent/discriminant validity were assessed.

what_manipulated: callous-unemotional trait levels (high vs low) and psychopathy variant (primary vs secondary)
what_measured: childhood maltreatment history profiles and behavioral outcomes
experiment: Incarcerated adolescent boys (N=227) were assessed on callous-unemotional traits, childhood maltreatment types, aggression, and delinquency. Latent profile or cluster analysis identified variants of high-CU youth. Comparisons examined whether primary and secondary variants differed in maltreatment history (sexual abuse, neglect) and behavioral outcomes (type of delinquency, aggression). Differences from low-CU youth were also examined.

what_manipulated: units' generalized trust level (high vs low, measured dispositionally) and target truthfulness in videos (truth vs lies)
what_measured: accuracy of lie detection judgments
experiment: Units watched videos of eight simulated job interviews. Half of interviewees told truths; half told lies. Units made binary truthfulness judgments. Accuracy was compared between high-trust and low-trust units. Analyses examined lie detection accuracy and any differential sensitivity to lies versus truths.

what_manipulated: sensory modality (vision, hearing, touch, taste, smell) and spatial scope (global vs local processing)
what_measured: creative and analytic thinking performance
experiment: RETRACTED DUE TO DATA MANIPULATION. Original study design: Units were exposed to sensory stimuli varying in spatial scope (global or local). They then completed creative and analytic tasks. Comparisons examined whether task performance differed by sensory modality and spatial scope. University of Amsterdam found evidence of data irregularities indicating violation of academic integrity.

what_manipulated: mortality salience induction (present vs absent) and delay before measurement (immediate vs delayed)
what_measured: death-thought accessibility
experiment: Units received a mortality salience manipulation (writing about death) or control task. Death-thought accessibility was measured immediately or after a delay using response latency or recall tasks. Comparisons examined whether death-thought accessibility differed by condition and time, testing the prediction that accessibility would rebound after a delay.

what_manipulated: moral content of target's decision (moral vs immoral action) and decision speed (quick vs slow)
what_measured: moral character evaluations of the target
experiment: Two experiments presented units with vignettes or observations of targets making decisions with moral content. Decision speed was experimentally varied or manipulated. Units rated the moral character of the target. Mediation analyses examined whether perceptions of decision certainty explained the effect and whether attributions to character motives mediated further.

what_manipulated: self-concept clarity (primed low vs. baseline)
what_measured: interest in self-expansion and incorporation of partner attributes into self-concept
experiment: Participants were primed with instructions to induce low self-concept clarity or received no prime. Interest in self-expansion was measured via self-report questionnaire. Study 3 measured actual self-expansion by assessing whether participants incorporated partner attributes. Across three studies, the relationship between clarity manipulation and expansion outcomes was evaluated.

what_manipulated: presence of a smiley emoticon vs. no emoticon in text-based communication
what_measured: perceived warmth, perceived competence, and information-sharing intentions
experiment: Participants evaluated text messages in work contexts with or without smiley emoticons. Perceptions of warmth and competence were measured via rating scales. The mediating role of message-appropriateness perceptions and the moderating role of context formality were tested. Three experiments systematically varied conditions.

what_manipulated: exposure to critical historical education vs. control information (Study 2); prior knowledge of racism history and group-identity salience (Study 1)
what_measured: acknowledgment and denial of systemic racism in the present day
experiment: Study 1 measured existing knowledge and identity; Study 2 randomly assigned participants to learn critical history or a control topic, then measured racism perceptions. Differences between racial groups were examined as both a grouping factor and in relation to identity variables. Statistical associations between knowledge/identity and racism denial were estimated.

what_manipulated: anxiety disorder status (GAD vs. healthy vs. high trait anxiety)
what_measured: speed of disengaging attention from threat-related faces (angry, fearful)
experiment: Three groups of units (those with diagnosed GAD, healthy controls, high trait anxiety) were presented with faces and attention-disengagement latencies were measured via eye-tracking or reaction times. Two experiments compared the three groups on the same outcome under similar conditions.

what_manipulated: affective expectation (positive vs. control) and individual difference in need for cognition
what_measured: affective reaction to an ambiguous experience (unfunny film clip)
experiment: Participants varying in need for cognition were randomly assigned to receive a positive or neutral expectation before watching a film clip. Affective reactions were measured via self-report. The interaction between expectation and need for cognition was tested to determine assimilation vs. contrast effects.

what_manipulated: not applicable; observational grouping by identity development status
what_measured: personality traits (neuroticism, conscientiousness, openness, extroversion, agreeableness)
experiment: Young-adult participants completed an identity-status measure and a personality inventory. Correlations and patterns of associations between identity categories and personality dimensions were examined. Group differences were evaluated across the five traits.

what_manipulated: combination of feedback type (augmented vs. none), attentional focus (external vs. neutral), and monetary reward (present vs. absent)
what_measured: jump height performance and muscular activity (electromyography)
experiment: Participants performed countermovement jumps under six conditions: neutral, augmented feedback only, reward only, augmented feedback plus external focus, augmented feedback plus reward, and all three combined. Jump heights and muscular activation were measured across conditions.

what_manipulated: expectations about future earnings opportunities (high vs. low expectations induced experimentally)
what_measured: effort provision (time worked and money earned in a real-effort task)
experiment: Participants were randomly assigned to treatment conditions designed to induce high or low expectations about future payoffs. Participants then performed an effort task where they could choose how long to work. Effort provision was compared between treatment groups.

what_manipulated: market structure rules: liability regime, verifiability of quality, seller reputation mechanism, and degree of competition
what_measured: transaction efficiency, pricing, frequency of overtreatment and undertreatment
experiment: A large experimental market (936 participants) varied institutional rules systematically. Buyers and sellers interacted in credence-goods markets. Across treatments, efficiency was measured as the proportion of beneficial transactions occurring at equilibrium prices. Quality provision was also assessed.

what_manipulated: employer commitment technology (full commitment via fixed wage schedule, no commitment, computer-generated wages, reputation opportunity)
what_measured: worker effort provision over multiple periods and wage offers by firms
experiment: Firms and workers interacted over multiple periods in a labor market. Treatments varied whether firms could commit to a deferred wage schedule or must build reputation. Worker effort and firm wage offers were measured across treatments and periods.

what_manipulated: presence and operation of a pre-play asset market tied to coordination-game outcomes; payoff structures in the coordination game
what_measured: efficiency of coordination-game outcomes; asset prices; correlation between market prices and game outcomes
experiment: Participants traded assets in a market where values depended on outcomes in a subsequent coordination game. Two experiments varied payoff structures and interdependence. Efficiency and market-price forecasting were measured across treatments.

what_manipulated: feasibility of low-talent agents participating in a Pareto-improving outcome under hidden-information conditions
what_measured: success of communication, patterns of truth-telling and lying, efficiency of outcomes
experiment: Units with hidden information about their type communicated with each other to coordinate on a trading outcome. The design varied whether low-talent agents could participate in the efficient outcome. Communication patterns and efficiency were measured.

what_manipulated: presence and salience of a common group identity vs. no group identity
what_measured: coordination success (efficient vs. inefficient equilibrium achievement) in repeated play of a minimum-effort game
experiment: Participants with salient group identity and control participants without identity salience played a minimum-effort coordination game repeatedly. Learning and equilibrium selection over rounds were observed. Group-contingent preferences were modeled theoretically.

what_manipulated: induced affective state (mild positive affect vs. neutral affect randomly assigned)
what_measured: time preference for money (preference for present over future payments)
experiment: Participants were randomly assigned to receive a manipulation intended to induce positive or neutral mood. They then made choices between earlier and later monetary payments. Time preference was estimated from choice patterns.

what_manipulated: game structure (continuous-time play vs. discrete-period play with varying grid size)
what_measured: cooperation rate in a prisoner's dilemma (mutual cooperation frequency)
experiment: Groups of units played a prisoner's dilemma under different time structures. One treatment used continuous-time accumulation over 60 seconds; others used discrete subperiods of varying length. Cooperation rates were compared across treatments and over learning rounds.

what_manipulated: presence vs. absence of noise in action implementation (intended cooperation may result in accidental defection)
what_measured: cooperation rates and strategy types adopted in repeated prisoner's dilemma
experiment: Pairs of units played repeated prisoner's dilemmas. In treatment groups, actions were implemented with noise; controls had perfect action implementation. Strategy adoption and payoff success were analyzed across noisy and clear conditions.

what_manipulated: screening technology for work attitudes, wage-discretion level, and labor-market competition intensity
what_measured: adoption of high-performance work systems (high-discretion job design with high wages) vs. low-performance systems; worker effort and shirking
experiment: Firms and workers interacted in repeated labor-market games. Treatments varied whether firms could screen workers and the degree of competition. Job-design choices and worker performance were measured to identify which designs became viable.

what_manipulated: fundamental value process (constant vs. declining) and cash-to-asset ratio (constant vs. increasing); context framing (generic stocks vs. depletable gold mine)
what_measured: asset mispricing, overvaluation relative to fundamental value, and confusion about fundamentals
experiment: Participants traded assets in experimental markets under different fundamental-value trajectories and context framings. Prices were recorded and compared to theoretical fundamental values. Post-experiment questionnaires assessed understanding.

what_manipulated: organ-allocation policy giving priority to prior donors on waiting lists vs. control policy without priority incentive
what_measured: decision to register as an organ donor
experiment: Participants made decisions about organ-donor registration in an experimental game. Allocation-policy rules were varied between treatment and control groups. Registration rates were compared to assess the impact of priority policies.

what_manipulated: severity of costly punishment option (none vs. standard vs. severe) available in a repeated exchange task
what_measured: average net payoffs and frequency of cooperative behavior
experiment: Units play a repeated public good game over many rounds in a between-subjects design with two monitoring conditions: perfect and imperfect. In perfect monitoring, all contributions and defections are observable. In imperfect monitoring, observability is limited. Punishment severity (none, standard, severe) is varied between groups. Payoffs are calculated accounting for both contribution gains and punishment costs. Mean payoffs across all rounds and subjects are compared.

what_manipulated: authority allocation (retention vs. delegation) in a coordination task with differential incentives
what_measured: choice to retain or delegate authority, effort provision, and aggregate payoffs
experiment: Units play an authority-delegation game where one unit decides whether to retain control (and set effort incentives for both) or delegate control to another unit. The delegating unit can offer a contract to the controlling unit, creating a coordination problem. Choices are observed, effort is measured, and outcomes are compared between units with authority and those without. The analysis examines whether authority is retained despite material incentives to delegate and whether authority correlates with higher effort provision.

what_manipulated: procedure for selecting an arbitrator (simultaneous veto-rank vs. sequential shortlisting)
what_measured: outcomes and efficiency of arbitrator selection, consistency with theoretical predictions
experiment: Units representing conflicting parties participate in a procedure to select an arbitrator from a candidate pool. In one condition, units simultaneously veto candidates (veto-rank); in another, units sequentially eliminate candidates (shortlisting). The selected arbitrator then rules on a hypothetical dispute. Outcomes are compared to theoretical predictions about procedure performance, focusing on whether the shortlisting procedure yields predicted efficiency advantages over veto-rank.

what_manipulated: availability of money (present vs. absent) as a medium of exchange in an economic model
what_measured: equilibrium selection (monetary vs. gift exchange), aggregate payoffs, and efficiency
experiment: Units engage in repeated exchange interactions. In one condition, money is available as a store of value and medium of exchange; in another, only barter and gift exchange are possible. Units' trading behavior and payoffs are observed over repeated rounds. The analysis determines which equilibrium emerges, whether subjects coordinate on theoretical predictions, and how welfare compares across conditions.

what_manipulated: subsidy level (none, moderate, very high) for antimalarial drugs and availability of rapid diagnostic tests over-the-counter
what_measured: treatment-seeking behavior, drug uptake, over-treatment rate, and public health outcomes
experiment: Households in Kenya are offered antimalarials at varied subsidy levels, with diagnostic testing availability manipulated independently. Purchasing behavior, whether individuals actually have malaria, and medication use are tracked. Over-treatment (taking antimalarials without malaria) is measured as a proxy for public health cost. Statistical analysis compares treatment-seeking and medication adherence across subsidy and testing conditions to assess the trade-off between access and targeting.

what_manipulated: observability of career-related preferences (exam/problem sets unobserved vs. real-stakes questionnaire observed by classmates) and observer gender (single male peers vs. others)
what_measured: participation grade, stated salary aspirations, willingness to work long hours, willingness to travel
experiment: Female MBA students take exams and problem sets where performance is unobserved, and complete a real-stakes placement questionnaire where classmates may see their preferences. Responses are compared when observability is varied. A second experiment varies the presence of single male peers during observability manipulations. Participation grades, stated salary preferences, and hours/travel willingness are measured. Analysis compares single women's responses versus married women and other groups to isolate marriage-market signaling effects.

what_manipulated: exposure to migration and cultural contact (differential access to Western democratic vs. Russian non-democratic destinations following 1998 Russian crisis)
what_measured: electoral participation, voting for non-communist parties, individual political preferences
experiment: A quasi-experimental design leverages the 1998 Russian crisis and subsequent emigration from Moldova directed to both Western Europe and Russia.
